verification: American Red Cross and AAP have explicitly stated there is no evidence that swimming after eating causes cramps, drowning, or any specific risk. Postprandial blood-flow redistribution to digestion is mild and does not impair muscle function enough to cause drowning. The rule originated in 1908 Boy Scout Handbook with no cited basis. Statistics: no recorded swimming death is attributable to recent eating. Strenuous exercise immediately after a heavy meal causes some discomfort (slight cramping, side stitch) but is not dangerous.
wisdom: The proverb is a century-old caution your parents repeated without checking. Empty pool decks, families waiting on watches, for no documented reason. Not all folk wisdom is harmful, but some is just wrong.

title: Shaving makes hair grow back thicker.
verification: Hair grows from the follicle below the skin; the rate and thickness are genetically and hormonally determined, not influenced by what happens above the skin surface. Shaving cuts the hair shaft at an angle, leaving a blunt tip that feels coarser when grown out 1–2 mm; this is a tactile illusion of thickness, not actual thickness. Trotter (1928) and Lynfield (1970) both measured hair regrowth after shaving — no change in diameter, color, density, or growth rate. The myth has been disproved repeatedly across 100 years.
wisdom: What's above the skin doesn't change what's below. The proverb survives because the blunt regrowth feels different — but feels and measures are different things. Razor blade companies are not actually growing your hair.

title: Vitamin C prevents the common cold.
verification: Cochrane review (Hemilä & Chalker 2013, 29 trials, n=11,306): regular high-dose vitamin C did not reduce cold incidence in the general population but did reduce cold duration by ~8% in adults and ~14% in children. Exception: in subjects undergoing extreme physical stress (marathoners, soldiers in subarctic), regular vitamin C cut cold incidence by ~50%. Linus Pauling's famous claims (1970) of cold prevention with megadoses were not supported. Reality: vitamin C is necessary but cheap; deficiency is rare in modern diets; supplementation produces small benefits at the margin.
wisdom: Linus Pauling oversold it; the proverb followed. The reality is small effects on duration, large effects only in extreme physical stress. Don't load oranges expecting immortality.

title: Warm milk helps you sleep.
verification: Milk contains small amounts of tryptophan (precursor to serotonin and melatonin) — but the dose (~70 mg per glass) is too small to cross the blood-brain barrier in meaningful quantity, and it competes with other amino acids for transport. The actual sleep effect is likely from: 1) the calming ritual of a warm drink; 2) modest blood glucose stabilization; 3) Pavlovian conditioning (childhood association). Effects measured in sleep studies are small and dominated by placebo/expectation. Bedtime routines reliably improve sleep; warm milk is one such routine.
wisdom: The chemistry doesn't carry the load. The ritual does. Most successful bedtime aids work by the same mechanism: consistent, calming pre-sleep cues. Milk works as cue; lukewarm tea or chamomile work the same.

title: Eat your greens for iron.
verification: Spinach (Popeye's choice) contains ~2.7 mg iron per 100 g. However, plant-source iron is non-heme iron, which has only ~5–15% bioavailability vs. ~25–35% for heme iron from meat. Compounds in spinach (oxalates) further reduce iron absorption. Net usable iron from a serving of spinach is small; the same iron content in red meat is much more available. Spinach is still valuable nutritionally (vitamin K, folate, magnesium), but the iron-from-greens proverb overstates the case. The famous 1870 spinach iron value was reportedly a decimal-point error (35 mg/100g was the original figure; the actual is ~3 mg). Popeye's mythology rests partly on a typo.
wisdom: Eat the greens. Just not entirely for the iron. The decimal place did the most heavy lifting in this proverb's history. The greens are good for many reasons; iron is just not the strongest of them.

title: Cold weather causes colds.
verification: Cold weather does not directly cause viral infection. However, several indirect mechanisms are real: 1) people gather indoors more, increasing viral transmission; 2) low ambient humidity dries respiratory mucosa, reducing barrier function; 3) cold air may temporarily reduce neutrophil response in nasal passages (Foxman et al. 2015 — rhinovirus replicates better at 33°C than 37°C, so cooler nasal temperatures favor the virus). The folk wisdom of 'don't go outside with wet hair, you'll catch cold' is wrong about the mechanism but in the right ballpark on the season. Indirect causation is real; direct causation is not.
wisdom: Mother was right about the season; wrong about the mechanism. You don't catch colds from being cold; you catch them from being indoors with infected people in dry air. The fix is hand-washing and ventilation, not extra sweaters.

title: RICE — rest, ice, compression, elevation — for sprains.
verification: The classic RICE protocol (introduced 1978 by Dr. Gabe Mirkin) was clinical standard for sprains and acute injuries for decades. Recent evidence: ice may actually DELAY healing by suppressing inflammatory signaling that initiates tissue repair (van den Bekerom et al. 2012, Cochrane). Mirkin himself recanted ice in 2014. Current consensus favors PEACE & LOVE protocol (Dubois & Esculier 2020): Protect, Elevate, Avoid anti-inflammatories, Compress, Educate — followed by Load, Optimism, Vascularization, Exercise. Compression and elevation remain confirmed; ice is now controversial; rest is replaced by early gentle motion.
wisdom: The protocol that everyone learned in high school football is partially obsolete. Even the doctor who invented it changed his mind. Knowledge updates; folk wisdom lags.

title: Eggs raise cholesterol; limit to 2 a week.
verification: Dietary cholesterol has minimal effect on blood cholesterol in most people. The body manufactures 75% of its own cholesterol; dietary intake feedback-suppresses endogenous synthesis. Eggs contain ~187 mg cholesterol per egg but their net effect on LDL/HDL is small. American Heart Association removed the 300 mg/day cholesterol cap in 2015. Dietary Guidelines for Americans 2015 removed cholesterol from list of nutrients of concern. Eggs are highly nutritious (complete protein, choline, vitamins B2/B12/D, lutein). The 1980s-1990s 2-eggs-per-week recommendation is now considered outdated for the general population (still relevant for some with specific genetic conditions like familial hypercholesterolemia).
wisdom: 40 years of dietary advice that turned out to be wrong. Most population-level eaters can eat an egg a day without measurable LDL impact. The proverb has lagged the science by over a decade.

title: Breakfast is the most important meal of the day.
verification: Originated as a 1944 General Foods marketing slogan for breakfast cereals (echoed earlier by Kellogg's). Empirical evidence: meta-analyses (Sievert et al. 2019 BMJ) show no significant weight loss benefit from regular breakfast vs skipping for adults. Some studies show modest cognitive benefit in school-age children. Time-restricted eating (intermittent fasting, common pattern: eat 12 PM–8 PM, skip breakfast) has equivalent or superior metabolic outcomes in some trials. Bottom line: breakfast is a fine meal if you're hungry; not eating it is also fine if you're not. The 'most important' framing was advertising that became folk wisdom.
wisdom: A century-old cereal-company slogan promoted to medical advice. The evidence doesn't support the hierarchy. Eat when you're hungry; the timing matters less than the food.

title: Sugar makes children hyperactive.
verification: Meta-analysis by Wolraich, Wilson & White (1995, JAMA) of 16 controlled trials found no measurable effect of dietary sugar on children's behavior or cognitive performance. The perception of sugar-induced hyperactivity is a classic case of confirmation bias: parents who BELIEVE their child consumed sugar rate their behavior as more hyper, regardless of actual sugar intake (Hoover & Milich 1994). Parties, birthdays, and Halloween are stimulating environments independent of cake intake. The proverb is a textbook example of mistaken causal inference in human cognition.
wisdom: The kids are wild because it's a party, not because of cake. Parents read the room and assign the cause incorrectly. The proverb is mostly evidence of how humans construct false-correlations from messy environments.

title: Run a burn under cool water.
verification: Cool water (15–25°C) running over a burn for 20+ minutes (within 3 hours of injury) significantly reduces tissue damage by halting the 'thermal cascade' — residual heat in burned tissue continues damaging deeper layers for minutes after the initial burn. Australian Red Cross and British Burn Association: 20 minutes of cool running water is first-line treatment. Use cool, not ICE-cold (which causes vasoconstriction and worsens deeper tissue damage). The principle: pull heat out before the burn deepens. Folk wisdom matches the evidence-based protocol exactly.
wisdom: Common sense aligned with measurement. Cool water for 20 minutes saves skin depth. Cold and fast is wrong (vasoconstriction); cool and patient is right. The granny remedy is also the ER protocol.

title: Put butter on a burn.
verification: Butter is hot from being room temperature, traps heat against the skin (insulator), and contaminates the wound with bacteria from the kitchen environment. American Burn Association explicitly warns against any greasy substance (butter, oil, lard, toothpaste, egg whites). Modern ER treatment: cool water as above, then sterile gauze. Butter delayed healing in the small studies that have been done; in the worst cases it caused infection requiring surgical debridement. The remedy is folk hand-me-down from before refrigeration; it should have died with the icebox.
wisdom: One of the most damaging folk remedies that persists. Cool water beats every greasy substance the kitchen offers. Some folk wisdom is wrong; this one is wrong AND harmful.

title: Baking soda neutralizes heartburn.
verification: Heartburn is caused by stomach acid (HCl, pH ~1.5–3) refluxing into the esophagus. Sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO₃) reacts: NaHCO₃ + HCl → NaCl + H₂O + CO₂. The acid is neutralized; symptom relief is immediate. 1/4 to 1/2 teaspoon in a glass of water is the standard dose. Caveats: excessive sodium load (concerning for hypertensives), occasional rebound acidity, the CO₂ released can cause distension. Not for daily long-term use, but acceptable as occasional remedy. Same active ingredient as some commercial antacids (Alka-Seltzer, Tums use related compounds).
wisdom: The pantry has the chemistry the pharmacy charges $5 for. Same active ingredient, much smaller package. Not for daily use; fine for occasional.

title: Ginger helps nausea.
verification: Ginger (Zingiber officinale) contains gingerols and shogaols, which act on 5-HT₃ serotonin receptors in the gut — the same receptors targeted by ondansetron (Zofran), the gold-standard antiemetic. Multiple RCTs (Ernst & Pittler 2000; Marx et al. 2013) show ginger is effective for: motion sickness, pregnancy-related nausea (1 g/day, considered safe in pregnancy), chemotherapy-induced nausea (modest), post-operative nausea. Effect size: roughly 30–50% symptom reduction vs placebo. Not as potent as prescription antiemetics but accessible, cheap, low side-effect profile. Folk remedy with mechanism + RCT validation.
wisdom: The plant hits the same receptor as the prescription drug. Less potent, more accessible. Folk medicine that mostly checks out under microscopes.

title: Echinacea boosts your immune system.
verification: Cochrane review (Karsch-Völk et al. 2014, 24 trials): some Echinacea preparations show modest benefit in shortening colds (perhaps 1.4 days vs ~7 day duration) and in prevention. However, results are inconsistent across preparations — different Echinacea species (E. purpurea, E. angustifolia, E. pallida), different plant parts (root, aerial), different extraction methods give different bioactivity. The 'immune boost' framing oversimplifies; specific compounds (alkamides, polysaccharides) interact with specific immune pathways with measurable but modest effects. Verdict depends on the specific product.
wisdom: Not all Echinacea is the same. The marketing label says one thing; the chemistry varies. Modest evidence for some preparations, no evidence for others. The proverb omits the asterisks.

title: Heat helps a tight muscle; ice helps an injury.
verification: Heat (40–45°C local) increases blood flow, reduces muscle viscoelastic resistance, and stimulates nociceptive gate-control. Useful for: chronic muscle tension, lower back stiffness, menstrual cramps, pre-exercise warm-up. Ice (0–10°C local) constricts blood vessels, reduces nerve conduction velocity, dampens acute inflammatory cascade. Useful for: acute traumatic injury (first 24–48 hours), post-workout inflammation. The principle: heat for chronic, ice for acute. Wrong combination (ice on chronic tension, heat on fresh sprain) can worsen outcomes. The proverb compresses sports-medicine practice into one rhyming line.
wisdom: The folk version of the protocol. Heat opens; ice closes. Chronic wants opening; acute wants closing. Standard order of operations in any athletic training room.

title: Chocolate causes acne.
verification: Original 1969 study (Fulton et al.) found no link and was widely cited as 'chocolate doesn't cause acne.' But it had methodological issues (small sample, problematic placebo). More recent research (Caperton 2014; Cordain 2002) has reopened the question: high-glycemic-index foods (including milk chocolate due to its sugar/dairy load) appear to worsen acne in susceptible individuals via insulin/IGF-1 signaling on sebaceous glands. Dark chocolate (low sugar) has less effect. Dairy may matter more than cocoa. Individual response varies enormously. The simple 'chocolate causes acne' is too crude; the more accurate version is 'high-glycemic + dairy combinations may worsen acne in some.'
wisdom: The proverb is too coarse. Sugar plus dairy moves the needle for some skins; cocoa itself doesn't. Modern dermatology rewrote the answer with more nuance than the lunch room offered.

title: Smiling, even when you don't feel it, lifts your mood.
verification: The facial feedback hypothesis (James 1890; Strack et al. 1988) — that physical expressions influence subjective emotion — was widely cited for decades. The original Strack pen-in-mouth experiment failed to replicate in a 17-lab Many Labs replication (Wagenmakers et al. 2016). Recent meta-analyses (Coles et al. 2019, 138 studies) find a small but statistically reliable effect (~0.07 in standardized mean difference), much smaller than originally claimed. The effect is real but modest; it won't cure depression but may marginally lift everyday mood. The proverb is a simpler claim than the science; the simpler claim is roughly right at small effect size.
wisdom: A small real effect, oversold for decades. Won't fix anything serious. Might nudge a mediocre Tuesday into a slightly less mediocre Tuesday. The face does talk to the brain a little.

title: Get vitamin D from the sun.
verification: UVB radiation (280–315 nm) on bare skin converts 7-dehydrocholesterol to previtamin D3, then to cholecalciferol (D3). 10–30 minutes of midday summer sun on face and arms produces ~1,000 IU of D3 in light-skinned adults. Vitamin D status correlates with bone health, mood, immune function, and many other markers. Indoor lifestyle + sunscreen + dark skin in high latitudes can all produce deficiency (Holick 2007 estimated 1 billion people globally are vitamin D deficient or insufficient). Supplementation works too but sunlight is the original delivery system. Caveat: UV exposure also causes skin cancer; the optimization is not 'maximize sun' but 'minimum effective dose.'
wisdom: The sun makes vitamin D. Sunscreen + indoor work blocks it. Modern life is unusually D-deficient because it's unusually indoors. Either get some sunlight or supplement; choose deliberately.

title: Time is money.
verification: Benjamin Franklin, 'Advice to a Young Tradesman' (1748): 'Remember that time is money.' Economically: opportunity cost is real. An hour spent on activity A is an hour not spent earning, learning, or producing anything else. The principle drives wage labor, hourly billing, and discounted cash flow analysis (a dollar today is worth more than a dollar in a year due to time value). At a deeper level: time is the one resource that cannot be bought back. Money lost can be earned again; time lost cannot. The proverb compresses the time-value-of-money + opportunity-cost concept into three words.
wisdom: Franklin meant it operationally: every hour has a price. The proverb is right and incomplete — time also has values money can't buy. The trade-offs matter; the metaphor is sharp on one axis only.

title: Buy low, sell high.
verification: Mathematically trivial; operationally near-impossible. Identifying 'low' and 'high' in advance is the hard part — that's the entire field of investment management. Behavioral finance research (Kahneman, Thaler) shows individual investors systematically do the opposite: they buy during bull markets (fear of missing out) and sell during bear markets (panic). Dalbar's Quantitative Analysis of Investor Behavior shows the average equity-fund investor underperforms the funds they invest in by 4–5% annually because of mistimed entry/exit. The proverb is a true statement of the goal that omits the difficulty.
wisdom: True as goal, useless as advice. Knowing 'buy low, sell high' is the easy half; knowing WHICH is low and which is high requires luck or skill most people don't have. Index funds and dollar-cost averaging are the honest workaround.

title: Don't put all your eggs in one basket.
verification: Markowitz Modern Portfolio Theory (1952): for any given expected return, diversifying across uncorrelated assets minimizes variance. The mathematics: portfolio variance = Σ wᵢwⱼσᵢσⱼρᵢⱼ; when correlations ρᵢⱼ are less than 1, total variance is less than the weighted sum of individual variances. Diversification is the rare 'free lunch' in finance — risk reduction without proportional return reduction. The proverb captured intuitive understanding centuries before Markowitz proved it (Cervantes 1605, Don Quixote: 'It is the part of a wise man to keep himself today for tomorrow, and not to venture all his eggs in one basket'). Recent caveat: in 2008-style global crises, correlations approach 1 across asset classes — diversification provides less protection than expected. Still better than no diversification.
wisdom: Cervantes had it 350 years before Markowitz proved it. The math agrees. Same idea: spread the risk; don't bet the farm on one season's chicken count.

title: A fool and his money are soon parted.
verification: Thomas Tusser, 1573: 'A fool and his money is soon parted.' Behavioral finance abundantly confirms: low financial literacy correlates with worse outcomes across every measurable dimension — higher credit card debt, lower retirement savings, higher exposure to fraud, higher participation in lottery and casino gambling. Lottery and gambling industries explicitly target populations with lower financial literacy; the math is unfavorable but the players keep playing. The mechanism isn't intelligence per se but understanding of compound interest, probability, and asymmetric risk. 'Fool' in the proverb means specifically the financially uneducated, not the generally unintelligent.
wisdom: The fool isn't necessarily stupid; the fool is the person who hasn't learned the math. Financial literacy is the cheapest insurance against the proverb. Schools should teach it.

title: Past performance is no guarantee of future results.
verification: SEC-required disclosure on mutual fund prospectuses since the 1940s. Mathematically: if returns followed a stationary distribution (constant mean and variance over time), past would predict future; they don't. Markets exhibit regime changes, mean reversion, fat tails, and structural breaks. Carhart (1997) showed mutual fund performance persistence is weak — top-quartile funds in one period only modestly outperform in the next. The Spike of 'star manager' funds is largely random — Fama & French showed most fund performance is statistical noise around the market average. The disclaimer is statistically accurate; the marketing brochure that ignores it is statistically misleading.
wisdom: Said on every prospectus, ignored by most investors. The disclaimer is correct; the brochure features the past anyway. Both halves of the document are the human market.

title: If something can go wrong, it will.
verification: Murphy's Law (attributed to Edward Murphy, 1949, USAF rocket-sled experiments). Behind the joke is a serious engineering principle: in any system with many possible failure modes, given enough trials, every failure mode will occur. The probability of a specific failure in a single trial may be tiny, but cumulative probability over N trials approaches 1 as N grows. Industrial reliability engineering builds in redundancy precisely because Murphy's Law is a statement of probability theory. Selection bias also matters: failures are memorable; successes are not. So Murphy seems even truer than the actual frequency.
wisdom: Reliability engineers take this as a design principle: build the system assuming every failure mode will eventually occur. Most catastrophic failures are someone assuming this won't apply to them.

title: Work expands to fill the time available.
verification: C. Northcote Parkinson (1955), originally about civil service expansion. Empirical evidence is overwhelming: studies of academic deadlines, project management, government bureaucracies, and consulting engagements consistently show that increased time allocation produces equally increased completion time (rather than equally improved quality). Mechanism: humans expand task complexity, perfectionism, and procrastination to use available time. The corollary: tight deadlines often produce equal-quality output to loose ones. Practical application: set deadlines slightly tighter than 'comfortable.'
wisdom: More time isn't more done; it's more spread. The proverb is operations research compressed to one line. Tight deadlines exploit the same principle in reverse.

title: Everything takes longer than you expect — even when you account for Hofstadter's Law.
verification: Douglas Hofstadter, Gödel Escher Bach (1979). Self-referential observation that estimates of duration are systematically too low, even when the estimator knows about this bias and tries to correct for it. Empirically validated in software development (Lawler's 'Mythical Man-Month'), construction projects (Flyvbjerg 2014 megaproject database: 9 of 10 major infrastructure projects over-run cost or time), policy implementation, and individual planning. Mechanism: planning fallacy + optimism bias + ignorance of unknown unknowns. The recommended correction: take your honest estimate and multiply by 1.5–3× for software, larger for novel projects.
wisdom: The corrections we add for over-optimism are themselves over-optimistic. The honest move: multiply your gut estimate by 2 and you're closer than you started. Hofstadter encoded the human cognitive failure mode into a self-referential joke.

title: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.
verification: Charles Goodhart, 1975 (economic policy context); generalized by Marilyn Strathern (1997). When a metric is tied to incentive, people optimize for the metric rather than the underlying value the metric was meant to measure. Examples: school testing → teaching to the test; surgical mortality metrics → surgeons declining hard cases; police arrest quotas → over-policing minor offenses; engagement metrics → clickbait. The phenomenon is robust across every system where a measure has been turned into a target. The correct response: use multiple metrics, change them periodically, watch for gaming, and most importantly understand that the measure is a proxy for the value, not the value itself.
wisdom: The most reliable failure mode of metric-based management. Every system that has tried to scale via single-metric optimization has gamed the metric within months. Use multiple measures; rotate them; expect gaming.

title: Don't take down a fence until you know why it was put up.
verification: G.K. Chesterton, 'The Thing' (1929). Argument: an existing rule, structure, or institution exists because someone put it there for a reason; before removing it, understand the reason. Engineering analog: 'safety codes are written in blood' — each rule typically corresponds to a past accident. Software analog: don't remove a check until you understand why it was added. Political analog: institutions accumulated through centuries solve problems that may not be visible to anyone who only sees the cost of the institution. The principle resists both progressive impulse (tear down what seems unjust) and reactionary impulse (preserve what seems traditional) — it requires investigation of the WHY first.
wisdom: Most reform fails because the reformer didn't understand what was being reformed. Chesterton's principle is methodological humility. Find out why before you tear out; sometimes the fence was holding back a tiger.

title: Don't throw good money after bad.
verification: Formal name: sunk cost fallacy. Money already spent is irrelevant to future decisions; only future expected costs and benefits should drive choices. Demonstrated experimentally by Arkes & Blumer (1985); replicated in domains from gambling to military strategy to relationship decisions. Yet humans persistently 'finish what they started' even when continuing has negative expected value. The proverb is older than the experimental literature and accurate: yesterday's investment is yesterday's; today's decision needs today's math. Concorde, Vietnam War, doomed startups: all canonical examples of sunk-cost reasoning leading institutions into ruin.
wisdom: Yesterday is sunk. The only relevant question is whether tomorrow's actions produce more value than tomorrow's costs. The proverb is finance's most important sentence; most failures of will are the inability to stop pouring resources into a hole the previous you dug.

title: The customer is always right.
verification: Coined by retail magnates Harry Gordon Selfridge and César Ritz (~1908). Originally intended as employee instruction to treat customer complaints respectfully — don't argue, listen, resolve. Modern misreading: customer is always factually correct, can demand anything. Reality: rude customers exist, scam customers exist, customers can be wrong about pricing, fit, suitability. Operationally: 80% of customer disputes resolve well if the employee assumes the customer's complaint is sincere; 20% require pushback. Best modern reformulation: 'the customer's experience is real' — their perception, even if factually incorrect, is the data the business must respond to. The 'always' was always hyperbole.
wisdom: Originally a hiring philosophy, not a moral claim. Service workers must absorb most complaints with grace; that doesn't mean the complaints are correct. The 'always' has done more damage than the rest of the phrase.

title: Under-promise and over-deliver.
verification: Expectation-management research consistently shows: customer/stakeholder satisfaction is a function of (delivered) - (expected), not (delivered) absolute. Same output exceeds expectations or disappoints based on what was promised. The mechanism: people anchor on the promise; deviations from anchor produce strong subjective reactions. Practical applications: project estimates, service guarantees, sales forecasts, relationship commitments. Caveat: chronic under-promising erodes credibility (people stop believing the commitments); the optimum is to estimate accurately and then aim to slightly exceed.
wisdom: Math: satisfaction = output - expectation. Manage the right-hand side; over-deliver on the left. Salespeople who promise the moon spend the project apologizing for the dust.

title: If it ain't broke, don't fix it.
verification: Bert Lance (1977), Carter administration. The proverb correctly cautions against changes to working systems whose interactions are not fully understood. Engineering: tested, working code has zero known bugs; changes introduce risk. However: 'not broken' is a snapshot, not a sustainability statement. Many systems break slowly via accumulating tech debt, ignored maintenance, or environmental change. The aphorism is correct against unnecessary tinkering, wrong against necessary upkeep. The honest reformulation: 'if it works AND the cost of leaving it alone is bounded, leave it alone.'
wisdom: True against vanity changes. False against necessary maintenance. The roof doesn't leak yet doesn't mean shingles last forever. Distinguish: 'working' from 'will keep working without intervention.'

title: Better safe than sorry.
verification: Asymmetric risk-aversion advice. Correct when downside is catastrophic and reversal is costly (seatbelts, smoke detectors, vaccinations for serious disease). Incorrect when applied universally — overprotection has its own costs (helicopter parenting, regulatory paralysis, missed opportunities). The proverb is one half of the optimization equation; the other half is 'better act than miss.' Engineering version: 'minimize expected regret across all outcomes' — sometimes safe, sometimes bold, depending on payoff structure. The proverb is the conservative half; wisdom is knowing when it applies.
wisdom: Half the equation. Safety matters; so does cost of safety. Children raised with this proverb at every decision grow up risk-averse to adults who will not start anything. Safety is one input, not the only one.

title: Kill two birds with one stone.
verification: Efficiency aphorism. Real when domains overlap: an exercise that's also social (walking with a friend), a job that funds learning, errands combined geographically. Operationally, true 'two-fer' decisions are valuable. However: the metaphor becomes failure when applied to incompatible goals. Doing two things at once usually means doing both poorly (multitasking research, Strayer & Watson 2010, shows productivity declines 30–50% when tasks compete for attention). The proverb is correct when goals genuinely align, wrong when forcing alignment of goals that don't. The honest version: 'when goals align, exploit the alignment; when they don't, sequence them.'
wisdom: True when truly two birds. False when the user is forcing two unrelated outcomes into one motion. Multitasking advocate's misreading. Find genuine alignment; don't fabricate it.

title: Every choice has a cost — the path not taken.
verification: Opportunity cost is the foundational concept of microeconomics: the value of the next-best alternative foregone. Every decision allocates finite time and resources, and the value lost from non-chosen options is real even if invisible. Behavioral econ: humans systematically under-weight opportunity cost (focus on what they got, ignore what they didn't). Robert Frost's 'Road Not Taken' captures it artistically; Alfred Marshall captured it analytically (Principles of Economics, 1890). The proverb is the unstated assumption of every smart decision and the visible regret of most regrets.
wisdom: You're always paying for what you didn't choose with the dollars you spent on what you did. Most regret is about the dollars unspent; opportunity cost is the bill you didn't see at the time.

title: High risk, high reward.
verification: Capital Asset Pricing Model (Sharpe 1964; Lintner 1965): expected return scales with systematic risk (beta). On average, riskier assets compensate with higher expected return — otherwise no one would hold them. But: the relationship is statistical, not deterministic. Individual high-risk investments often produce ruin, not reward. The market pays a 'risk premium' for risk-bearers on average, while individual outcomes vary widely. Behavioral: people often misread 'volatility' as 'high return potential'; many ventures are 'high risk, low return' — a strictly losing proposition. The proverb is correct about the population, dangerous when applied to single picks.
wisdom: True for the universe of investments. False for any one investment. The lottery has high variance and negative expected value; the casino has the same. The proverb describes a population, not a guarantee.

title: A rising tide lifts all boats.
verification: John F. Kennedy used the phrase to argue for broad economic growth (1963). Mechanically: aggregate growth does increase total wealth, and some flows to every income decile via employment, taxes, and consumption. But: distribution is unequal. From 1970–2020 in the US, real income for the bottom 90% grew much slower than for the top 1% (Piketty & Saez data). 'Rising tide' raised some boats more than others; some boats took on water. The proverb is accurate at the aggregate level and incomplete at the distributional level. Used politically, it often functions to dismiss inequality concerns; that use is not supported by data.
wisdom: True in the aggregate, misleading when used to argue distribution doesn't matter. Some boats had holes in them; the tide didn't help. The proverb describes economics; politics has used it to argue against fixing the holes.

title: Compound interest is the eighth wonder of the world.
verification: Attributed to Einstein but probably apocryphal — no documented original source. The MATH it points to is real and remarkable: $1 invested at 7% for 50 years becomes $29.46 (29× growth); for 70 years, $113 (113×). The exponential function compounds slowly then explosively; small early advantages become huge late advantages. Empirically: the population of millionaires is dominated by long-time savers, not short-time high-rollers. The proverb (correct about math, wrong about attribution) is the most-quoted finance quote that Einstein probably never said.
wisdom: The math is correct; the attribution is wrong. Exponential growth is genuinely astonishing if you let it run. Most household wealth in retirement is the patient half-century version of this proverb in action.

title: Penny wise, pound foolish.
verification: Behavioral economics: humans frequently optimize on visible small costs while ignoring much larger invisible costs. Examples: buying the cheapest tires that wear out in 6 months (saving $20, costing $100/year); choosing the lowest-quality tools that break and need replacement (saving $30 today, costing $200 over 5 years); avoiding the cheaper preventive checkup that catches major problems early. The pattern is robust across consumer behavior, business operations, government procurement. Mechanism: visible costs vs invisible costs; near-term focus vs long-term outcomes. Total cost of ownership (TCO) frameworks exist specifically to combat this.
wisdom: The pennies are visible; the pounds are not. Most amateur procurement makes this mistake; institutional procurement learned to use TCO for the same reason. Watch where the savings come from, and where they go.

title: There ain't no such thing as a free lunch.
verification: TANSTAAFL — popularized by Milton Friedman (1975) and Robert Heinlein (1966). Originates in 19th-century US saloon culture: bars offered 'free lunch' but required purchase of a drink. Modern application: every 'free' offer has hidden costs (advertising-funded products sell your attention; subsidized services come with strings; 'free' apps monetize data). Physics analog: thermodynamics — no closed system produces net energy gain. Economic analog: equilibrium pricing — persistent free goods get arbitraged away. The proverb is one of the more reliable heuristics in economics.
wisdom: Find the actual cost; it's there somewhere. Time, attention, data, dignity, future obligation — those are the currencies the 'free' was paid in. The lunch arrives; the bill arrives differently.

title: Share the credit; take the blame.
verification: Leadership research (Collins 'Good to Great' Level 5 leaders; Useem 'Leadership Moment'): the most consistently effective leaders attribute success outward and absorb failure inward. The pattern produces: team retention, follower trust, sustainable scaling. Inverse pattern (take credit, deflect blame) produces: team attrition, low follower trust, eventual collapse when the leader becomes the bottleneck. The proverb is a leadership protocol with deep statistical support. Inverse is selfishness disguised as confidence.
wisdom: The math of trust over time. Leaders who do this scale further than the leaders who do the opposite. Self-promotion peaks early and decays; servant leadership compounds.

title: Never attribute to malice what is adequately explained by stupidity.
verification: Hanlon's Razor (probably coined by Robert J. Hanlon, 1980, but similar formulations go back to Goethe and Napoleon). Bayesian reasoning: most observed bad outcomes are produced by error, ignorance, or incompetence — which are common — rather than malice, which is rarer. Attributing malice when stupidity will do is itself a cognitive error (fundamental attribution error). Applied to social conflicts: most slights are unintentional. Applied to business failures: most are organizational, not adversarial. The razor reduces false-positive enemy detection.
wisdom: Most things that look like conspiracy are just incompetence. Most slights are inattention. Hanlon's razor cuts down enemies you imagined into errors you can correct.

title: Slow is smooth, smooth is fast.
verification: Originating in special operations (Navy SEAL, etc.) and adopted broadly. The principle: under stress, conscious slowdown produces smoother motor sequence which actually finishes faster than panicked rapid motion. Mechanism: stress narrows attention, increases motor error, increases probability of having to redo steps. Slowing down (slightly) keeps error-free execution. Empirically observed in: surgery, aviation emergency procedures, target shooting, musical performance, complex software changes. The proverb compresses 'deliberate practice + error reduction = real speed' into six words.
wisdom: Counter to intuition. Speed in emergencies usually causes errors that cost more time than the rush saved. Slow down, do it right, finish first. The proverb is dance instructor's wisdom translated to high-stakes work.

title: Correlation does not imply causation.
verification: Two variables can correlate due to: 1) X causes Y; 2) Y causes X; 3) Z causes both; 4) chance. Without controlled intervention, correlation alone cannot distinguish these. Famous Spurious Correlations (Tyler Vigen) catalog includes pairs like 'US cheese consumption' correlating with 'bedsheet entanglement deaths' (r=0.95). The phrase is the bedrock of modern statistics — Hume in 1748 made the philosophical point, Pearson and Fisher made it quantitative in the early 1900s. Yet popular science reporting still mistakes correlation for cause routinely. Engine relevance: the four gates require demonstrated causation, not mere co-occurrence.
wisdom: Two things move together. They might cause each other, neither might cause the other, both might be caused by something else. The proverb names the most common reasoning error in popular science.

title: You only see the survivors.
verification: Abraham Wald (1943, WWII): asked where to add armor to bombers, examined returning planes' bullet hole locations, recommended armoring NOT where holes were (those planes returned) but where holes weren't (planes hit there didn't return). The dataset of 'planes that came back' is biased; the question requires the 'all planes' dataset. Modern examples: 'successful CEOs all woke up at 5 AM' (we don't survey unsuccessful ones); 'medieval buildings are sturdier' (we only see the ones that didn't collapse); 'old movies are better' (only the good ones survived). The bias is ubiquitous and invisible to anyone who hasn't been taught to look for it.
wisdom: The dataset you can see is rarely the dataset that answers your question. Wald's bombers, the medieval cathedral, the famous startup founder — all are filtered samples. Look for the missing data.

title: Extremes tend to be followed by less-extremes.
verification: Regression to the mean (Francis Galton 1886): in any repeated measurement with noise, extreme values are likely to be followed by less extreme values, simply because the extreme was partly due to luck. Sports: 'rookie of the year curse' — top rookies regress next season. Tests: students who score 99th percentile on one test likely score lower on the next (still high, but lower). Medicine: spontaneous recovery is often misattributed to treatment when the treatment happened during a regression-to-mean event. Misinterpretation of regression as a treatment effect is a major source of false 'cure' claims.
wisdom: The bad doesn't keep getting worse forever; the good doesn't keep getting better forever. The proverb cures both panic and hubris. Galton was Darwin's cousin and the most underappreciated 19th-century statistician.

title: Don't forget the base rate.
verification: Base rate fallacy: humans systematically ignore prior probabilities when given specific information. Classic example (Kahneman & Tversky): if a test for a disease is 95% accurate and the disease affects 1 in 1,000 people, what's the probability a positive test is correct? Most people say ~95%; actual answer is ~2% (most positives are false positives because most people don't have the disease). Bayesian inference shows the base rate dominates rare-condition diagnostics. Same fallacy underlies racial profiling, medical screening anxiety, terrorism-detection alarm, and 'startup success stories.' The base rate is the silent giant of probability problems.
wisdom: How rare it is matters as much as how accurate the test is. Most people inverting the math of a screen test for a rare disease panic for no reason. Base rates are quiet but they win.

title: Past flips of a fair coin don't change the odds of the next.
verification: Gambler's fallacy: the belief that random outcomes 'self-correct' — a string of heads must be followed by tails to 'balance out.' Mathematically: independent events have no memory. P(heads next flip) = 0.5 regardless of the previous 100 flips. The intuition arises from the law of large numbers (long-run frequencies converge) being misapplied to short runs. Monte Carlo casino, 1913: roulette landed on black 26 times in a row; gamblers lost millions betting on red 'because it was due.' The wheel had no memory then; it has no memory now.
wisdom: The coin has no memory of last week's flips. The proverb is the corrective to the most common error at casinos and in trading floors. The next flip is 50/50 even after 100 heads. The crowd disagrees; the math does not care.

title: We see what we expect to see.
verification: Confirmation bias: humans preferentially seek, remember, and interpret information that confirms existing beliefs. Demonstrated by Wason (1960) — subjects given a number-sequence rule (any increasing triple) test confirmations rather than disconfirmations. Replicated thousands of times across politics, religion, science, medicine. Most discussion of 'evidence' on social media exhibits the bias heavily — people share confirming articles, ignore disconfirming ones. The bias is so robust that even scientists trained in scientific method exhibit it unless protocols enforce double-blind, pre-registration, etc.
wisdom: The world is messier than our hypotheses; we ignore the mess that doesn't fit. Pre-registered studies and adversarial review exist because un-aided humans (including scientists) confirm. Most everyday wrongness is this bias.

title: The probability of evidence given guilt is not the probability of guilt given evidence.
verification: Prosecutor's fallacy: confusing P(E|G) with P(G|E). If a DNA match has 1 in 1,000,000 random match probability, a prosecutor may argue 'probability of innocence is 1 in 1,000,000.' But: in a population of 10,000,000, there are 10 random matches; P(guilt | match) is ~1/10 if guilt is uniform-prior. The proper Bayesian calculation requires the prior. The Sally Clark case (UK, 1999) is the most famous miscarriage — Roy Meadow testified probability of two SIDS deaths was 1 in 73,000,000, leading to wrongful conviction; the calculation ignored that her sons might not be independent, and used the wrong conditional probability. Math errors in courtroom statistics still happen because lawyers (and many forensic experts) don't reliably distinguish the two conditional probabilities.
wisdom: P(A|B) and P(B|A) are different numbers. The legal system has put innocents in prison because the math was inverted. Bayesian thinking is not optional in courts; it should be required.

title: The simplest explanation is usually the best.
verification: Occam's Razor (William of Ockham, 14th century): 'entities should not be multiplied beyond necessity.' Modern interpretation: among competing hypotheses that explain the data equally well, prefer the one requiring fewer entities, assumptions, or independent moving parts. Why it works: each additional assumption multiplies the probability of being wrong somewhere. A theory with 10 parts has more failure modes than a theory with 3. Bayesian formalization: Bayesian Model Averaging penalizes complex models that don't materially improve fit. Caveats: 'simplest' must explain the data — over-simplification is its own error. The Razor cuts unnecessary complexity, not necessary complexity.
wisdom: Multiplying explanations multiplies the ways to be wrong. The razor is right when explanations are equally good; wrong when used as an excuse to ignore complexity that actually exists. Watch for the second mistake.

title: Always switch doors.
verification: Monty Hall problem: three doors, one prize, contestant picks door 1; host (who knows the prize location) opens door 3 to reveal a goat; should contestant switch to door 2? Yes — switching wins 2/3 of the time, staying wins 1/3. Mechanism: contestant's first pick was 1/3 probability correct; the host's action conditions on knowing where the prize is and gives information. Marilyn vos Savant published this in Parade (1990), received thousands of angry letters from Ph.D. mathematicians who insisted the answer was 1/2; she was correct. Simulation settles the dispute (try 1000 trials; switching wins ~667). The problem demonstrates how counterintuitive conditional probability can be.
wisdom: Even Ph.D. mathematicians get this wrong on first encounter. The math is not negotiable. Switch every time and win 2/3 of the games. The proverb is one of those rare cases where mathematics outright contradicts intuition and intuition has to lose.

title: In a room of 23 people, two share a birthday more than half the time.
verification: Birthday paradox: with n people, the probability at least two share a birthday is 1 - 365!/(365^n · (365-n)!). At n=23, this exceeds 0.5 (specifically ~50.7%). The counterintuitive part: people think 'n=183 is needed for 50%' because they confuse the question (do any two share?) with a simpler one (does anyone share MY birthday?). The actual question concerns the (n choose 2) ≈ 253 pairs in a room of 23 — that's why it works out faster than intuition expects. Useful in cryptography (hash collision attacks: a 64-bit hash has 50% collision risk after ~2^32 ≈ 4 billion tries, not 2^64).
wisdom: The combinatorics is the point. Counting pairs, not people. The proverb generalizes to cryptography: assumed security collapses faster than naive intuition predicts.

title: Two prisoners betray each other; both lose.
verification: Game theory's foundational example (Tucker 1950). Two suspects, separated, each offered: betray your partner for reduced sentence. Rational individual decision: betray (no matter what the other does, you're better off). But: if both betray, both get more years than if both stayed silent. Tragedy: rational individual choice produces collectively-bad outcome. Iterated prisoner's dilemma (Axelrod 1984) showed cooperation strategies (tit-for-tat) outperform always-betray in repeated games. Real-world applications: arms races, climate negotiations, antitrust enforcement, social dilemmas. The dilemma names why cooperation is hard even between rational agents.
wisdom: Each rational choice produces a collective failure. The single-shot version. The iterated version inverts: cooperation is the rational long-run strategy. Most human relationships are iterated, not single-shot — make sure you're playing the right game.

title: Many things in nature follow a bell curve.
verification: Central Limit Theorem: the sum of many independent random variables, regardless of underlying distribution, approaches a normal distribution. Applies to: human height, IQ test scores, measurement errors, particle velocities in gases, financial returns over short periods. Power and ubiquity: bell curve is the default expectation. However: many real-world distributions are NOT normal — wealth (power law), city sizes (Zipf), word frequencies (Zipf), file sizes, social network degrees. Misapplying normal to power-law phenomena causes major underestimation of tail risk (Taleb's 'black swan' argument). The bell curve is universal for sums of small independents and wrong for power-law systems.
wisdom: The bell curve is everywhere — until it isn't. Sums of small things go normal; products of many things, or systems with feedback, do not. Most financial blowups were models assuming normal when the data was power-law.

title: Algorithms don't scale; their costs do.
verification: Big-O notation classifies algorithm cost growth: O(1) constant, O(log n) logarithmic, O(n) linear, O(n log n) linearithmic, O(n²) quadratic, O(2^n) exponential. As input size n grows, asymptotic class dominates wall-clock performance. An O(n²) sort that's fine for 100 items takes ~1000× longer for 10,000 items — feasibly slow becomes infeasible. Every CS undergraduate learns: choose the algorithm class first, optimize within it second. Most production bugs in scaling startups are algorithmic — an O(n²) loop that worked at 100 users is unusable at 100,000 users. The proverb compresses 'asymptotic complexity matters' into one line.
wisdom: Constants don't save you. The wrong algorithm class fails at scale regardless of how fast each step is. Most catastrophic scale failures are choosing the wrong class.

title: You can't always tell if a program will finish.
verification: Halting Problem (Turing 1936): no general algorithm exists to determine, for arbitrary program P and input I, whether P(I) halts. Proven by reductio: assume halt(P,I) exists; construct PARADOX(P) that does opposite of halt(P,P); contradiction. The result is foundational to theoretical computer science and connects directly to Gödel's incompleteness theorems. Practical: static analysis tools can detect SOME non-halting programs (infinite loops in finite state machines) but not all. Programs are inherently unpredictable in this specific sense; no future tool will solve this.
wisdom: Some things cannot be computed. Computer science has a clear list of them. The halting problem is the canonical impossibility, and it's not going away. Hard limits exist on what software can do; the proverb names one.

title: Information is the resolution of uncertainty.
verification: Claude Shannon (1948 'A Mathematical Theory of Communication'): information content of a message equals reduction in uncertainty (entropy). Bits measure exactly this — 1 bit = 1 binary decision resolved. Formula: H = -Σ p(x) log₂ p(x). Compression algorithms exploit: predictable content has low information, can be compressed; random content has high information, cannot. The framework underlies all digital communication, data compression, cryptography, and machine learning. It also generalizes to: 'how surprised were you by the message?' is a quantifiable thing. Shannon proved this is mathematically equivalent to entropy in physics.
wisdom: The bit is the unit of surprise. Shannon's 1948 paper transformed engineering and is one of the most important single documents of the 20th century. The proverb compresses his entire framework into one line.

title: Run it enough times and the law of averages takes over.
verification: Law of Large Numbers (Jacob Bernoulli, 1713): as the number of trials of a random experiment grows, the observed mean approaches the true mean. For a fair coin, after 100 flips you might be at 0.55 heads; after 10,000 you'll be at 0.5005 (very close to 0.5). The theorem is foundational to statistics, polling, insurance, casinos, and quality control. Caveat: it says nothing about short runs (gambler's fallacy abuse this), and it requires independence among trials. House edges work because casinos play millions of hands; players play hundreds. The math grinds down individual luck and the house wins.
wisdom: The number of trials matters. Casino math wins in the long run by definition; player math gambles on the short run. The proverb is the silent reason why the casino is always there next year.

title: Every program is just if-then-else, loops, and assignments.
verification: Structured Programming Theorem (Böhm-Jacopini, 1966): any computable function can be expressed using only three control structures: sequence, selection (if-then-else), and iteration (loops). All higher-level constructs (recursion, exception handling, classes) can be reduced to these primitives. This is why structured programming replaced GOTO-based unstructured code in the 1970s. Practical implication: complex code is just compositions of these three primitives — there is no 'magic' in software, only abstractions over these elements.
wisdom: Every program is the same three primitives in different combinations. Mastering them is the foundation; learning libraries is the decoration. The proverb is computer science's foundational simplicity.

title: No formal system can prove its own consistency.
verification: Gödel's Second Incompleteness Theorem (1931): any consistent formal system capable of expressing arithmetic cannot prove its own consistency from within itself. Combined with the First Incompleteness Theorem: such systems contain true statements that cannot be proved within the system. The result demolished Hilbert's program of formalizing mathematics from the inside. Implications: 1) mathematical truth is broader than mathematical proof; 2) absolute certainty about any sufficiently rich formal system requires resources outside the system; 3) self-validation in any axiomatic system is impossible.
wisdom: The system cannot certify itself. Gödel's theorems are one of the great results of 20th-century thought. They apply to mathematics, formal logic, and (cautiously generalized) to many human institutions — no system can be its own ultimate validator.

title: The map is not the territory.
verification: Alfred Korzybski (1933, Science and Sanity). Models, abstractions, and representations are never identical to the reality they represent. Maps omit detail, simplify, distort projections. Even very accurate maps cannot capture every feature. Implication: confusing the model for the reality it describes causes errors. Modern relevance: all data, all models, all financial reports, all medical diagnoses are maps. Decisions made on maps without remembering they're maps are decisions made on partial information. George Box (statistician) said it more famously: 'All models are wrong; some are useful.'
wisdom: The map is built for purposes; the territory has none. Mistaking the model for reality is the most common smart-people error. Every map omits something; every territory has more than any map.

title: Trends in groups can reverse when groups are combined.
verification: Simpson's Paradox (Edward Simpson 1951): a trend present in subgroups can disappear or reverse in the aggregate. Classic example: UC Berkeley 1973 graduate admissions appeared to discriminate against women in aggregate (44% men admitted vs 35% women), but disaggregated by department showed women were admitted at slightly HIGHER rates than men in most departments. Women had applied to more competitive departments. The paradox arises when group sizes and group acceptance rates are correlated. Implications: aggregate statistics can mislead; always check disaggregations. The paradox is named for Simpson but had been described by Pearson in 1899.
wisdom: Aggregates lie. Disaggregations tell a different story than aggregates 5-10% of the time. The proverb is a warning to look at the slices, not just the pie chart.

title: No voting system is perfect.
verification: Arrow's Impossibility Theorem (Kenneth Arrow 1951): no voting system with ≥3 candidates can simultaneously satisfy: unrestricted domain (any preferences allowed), non-dictatorship (no single voter decides), Pareto efficiency (if everyone prefers A to B, A wins), and independence of irrelevant alternatives (A vs B doesn't depend on C). Implication: any rule for aggregating individual preferences into collective decisions has known failure modes. Plurality (winner-take-all) suffers from vote-splitting; ranked-choice has its own pathologies; approval voting has its own. The proverb generalizes: democracy is a least-bad mechanism, not a perfect mechanism.
wisdom: There is no perfect voting system. Each has well-documented failure modes. The proverb is a corrective to anyone selling 'the right voting reform' as a cure-all. Choose the one whose failure modes you can live with.

title: You can't compress every message without loss.
verification: Lossless compression has a hard mathematical limit (Shannon's source coding theorem). For a random message of N bits, no lossless compression scheme can reduce it below N bits on average (pigeonhole principle: 2^N possible messages cannot map to 2^M unique codes if M < N). Predictable messages (English text, photos) compress well because they have low entropy; random data does not. ZIP, GZIP, BZIP2 all achieve ~3:1 compression on English text and ~1:1 on random data. The proverb: not every information stream can be made smaller without losing something.
wisdom: You can compress what's predictable. You cannot compress randomness. The internet's transmission economics rely on the fact that most data is predictable; that fact has a mathematical limit.

title: Some problems are not just hard; they are impossible.
verification: Computability theory distinguishes 'undecidable problems' — those with NO algorithm that solves them. Examples: the Halting Problem (Turing 1936); the Entscheidungsproblem (Hilbert's decision problem, proven unsolvable by Church and Turing 1936); Wang's tiling problem; Hilbert's 10th problem (Diophantine equations, proven undecidable by Matiyasevich 1970); program equivalence in any sufficiently powerful language. These are not 'hard' — they are mathematically impossible. The proverb names the distinction between problems we haven't solved yet and problems we know can never be solved.
wisdom: Some failure modes aren't lack of effort. They are mathematical structure. Knowing what cannot be solved helps you spend time on what can.

title: Thinking fast and slow — two systems.
verification: Daniel Kahneman (Thinking Fast and Slow, 2011). System 1: fast, automatic, intuitive, emotional (recognizing a friend, driving on familiar route, sensing danger). System 2: slow, deliberate, effortful, logical (mental arithmetic, parking in tight space, conscious reasoning). Most everyday cognition is System 1; System 2 is recruited only when System 1 fails or alerts. The framework explains many cognitive biases as System 1 outputs going unchallenged by overworked System 2. Some replication concerns for specific Kahneman/Tversky findings since 2011, but the broad dual-system framework remains useful and well-supported.
wisdom: You have two cognitive modes that run different work. The fast one gets it right most of the time and wrong in characteristic ways. Knowing when to engage the slow one is the difference between calibrated and over-confident.

title: Average outcomes for many gamblers ≠ expected outcomes for one gambler over time.
verification: Ergodicity (mathematical concept; popularized by Ole Peters in economics): the ensemble average (across many parallel gamblers in one round) can differ from the time average (one gambler across many rounds) when outcomes are not 'ergodic.' Famous example: 50/50 bet, win 50% gain or lose 40%. Ensemble: average gain per round = +5%. Time average for ONE player: long-run growth rate is negative — they go broke. The non-ergodicity is path-dependent: ruin is absorbing. Most economic models assume ergodicity and produce misleading conclusions about long-run individual outcomes. Taleb has written extensively on this. The proverb is the essence: don't confuse the population's average with what happens to you over time.
wisdom: The casino's average bettor breaks even; the individual bettor's time-average loses everything to absorbing-state ruin. The mistake is treating the population statistic as the individual experience.

title: Rare events happen more often than your model predicts.
verification: Nassim Taleb (The Black Swan, 2007). Black swans: rare, high-impact events that are 'unpredictable' from prior data but rationalizable in retrospect. The 2008 financial crisis, 9/11, COVID-19, the 1987 market crash, and most major historical events fit this pattern. Mechanism: financial returns (and many other phenomena) have fatter tails than normal distributions predict. A '10-sigma' event under Gaussian assumption is one-in-10^23; under empirical fat-tail data, it's far more common. Long-Term Capital Management blew up in 1998 using models that assumed normal distributions; LTCM lost $4.6 billion in months. Most catastrophic financial blowups have the same shape: 'we modeled it as impossible, then it happened.'
wisdom: The tail is fatter than the model. The model said impossible; reality said often-enough. Build systems robust to events your model claims cannot happen.

title: An elephant never forgets.
verification: Elephants (Loxodonta africana and Elephas maximus) have demonstrably exceptional memory: matriarchs lead family groups along migration routes hundreds of km long, remembered for decades. Studies (Foley et al. 2008; McComb et al. 2001) show elephants recognize and remember individual humans for 12+ years, distinguish kin voices from non-kin, and pass on water-source knowledge across generations. Hippocampus relative to brain size is large. The proverb is biologically accurate, especially for matriarchs over 50 years old whose accumulated knowledge is generationally load-bearing.
wisdom: Elephants remember water; humans remember insults. Whose memory is being asked about determines what 'never forget' means. Either way, the elephant comes out ahead in calibration.

title: A goldfish has a 3-second memory.
verification: Multiple studies (Pike & Laland 2010; Brown 2015; Plymouth University training studies) demonstrate goldfish (Carassius auratus) can remember tasks, locations, and learned associations for months. Fish trained to push a lever for food retain the skill after months without practice. The '3-second memory' folklore appears to have no scientific origin and possibly arose from observation of fish circling small bowls (they did remember the corners — but the bowl looked identical at each turn). The proverb is wrong; fish memory is comparable to many small mammals.
wisdom: Goldfish were libeled for centuries. Their memory is months, not seconds. The proverb is one of those folk facts that turned out to be entirely fictional once anyone bothered to test it.

title: Cats always land on their feet.
verification: Cats have a well-developed 'righting reflex': from a fall of >0.3 m, cats can rotate mid-air using conservation of angular momentum (without external torque) to land feet-first. Studies show ~90% of cats falling from 1-2 stories land on their feet. Surprising statistical finding (Whitney & Mehlhaff 1987, NYC veterinary records): cats falling from 7+ stories have HIGHER survival than cats falling from 4-6 stories — the higher fall provides time to fully achieve terminal velocity (~60 mph for cats, vs ~120 mph for humans due to body shape) and adopt a spread-eagle posture distributing impact. The proverb is true at survival rate but not at 100%, and the height paradox is real.
wisdom: Mostly true with a strange height paradox. Cats are flying squirrels with claws. Don't test the limits of this proverb.

title: Blind as a bat.
verification: Bats have functional eyes and useful vision. Megachiropterans (fruit bats, flying foxes) have excellent vision — comparable to dogs. Microchiropterans (echolocating bats) have smaller eyes but can still see in dim light and recognize landmarks visually. They supplement vision with echolocation (Griffin 1944) for prey detection in total darkness. The 'blind' folklore likely comes from bats flying erratically at dusk (relying more on echolocation) and entering houses (homing via echo, not sight). Bats are not blind; they have a navigation system humans don't.
wisdom: The proverb is wrong — bats see. They also do something humans can't, which is hear shapes. The misnomer survives because echolocation is mysterious to humans who only see.

title: Bulls hate the color red.
verification: Cattle are dichromats — they cannot distinguish red from green. Their visual system has two cone types, sensitive to blue and yellow-green wavelengths. The matador's cape (muleta) provokes the bull via motion, not color (white capes work equally well in laboratory tests). The tradition of red cape may relate to hiding the bull's blood from the audience, not enraging the bull. The Mythbusters episode tested this directly with red, blue, and white capes; bulls charged all three equally. The proverb is folklore propagated by bullfighting tradition, but biologically false.
wisdom: The bull doesn't see red the way humans do. The motion provokes; the color does not. Centuries of bullfighting kept the myth alive in the absence of color-vision data.

title: Opossums play dead — it's a real defense.
verification: Virginia opossum (Didelphis virginiana) exhibits thanatosis (death-feigning) when severely stressed. The state is involuntary — a vagal-mediated shock response causing flaccid muscle tone, slowed breathing, and tongue lolling. It can last 40 minutes to 4 hours; the opossum cannot voluntarily 'wake up.' Effective against predators that respond to motion (foxes, coyotes), less effective against scavengers. The behavior is genetically programmed, not learned, and one of the most reliable thanatosis examples in mammals. The proverb correctly identifies the strategy but oversimplifies — the opossum isn't 'pretending'; it's having a stress-induced seizure that happens to look like death.
wisdom: The strategy is real and not voluntary. Evolution wired the opossum to crash when threatened, and many predators don't eat what they didn't kill. The proverb is mostly biology, lightly wrapped in 'pretend.'

title: Salmon return to the stream where they were born.
verification: Anadromous salmonids (Pacific salmon: chinook, coho, sockeye, etc.) hatch in freshwater, migrate to ocean, mature for 2-5 years, then return to spawn in their natal stream with high precision (~95%+ to within 100 m). Mechanism: olfactory imprinting on chemical signature of natal stream during smoltification (Hasler 1966); salmon literally smell their way home via mineral and organic chemistry unique to each watershed. Magnetic-field navigation (Lohmann et al. 2008) handles the oceanic portion. The combined system is one of biology's most impressive navigation feats — fish find a single drainage basin from thousands of km of open ocean.
wisdom: The fish smells its way home from the Pacific. The proverb hides a navigation system so reliable that fish runs in distinct rivers maintain genetic distinctness over centuries. Evolution wrote the homing system; the fish runs it without thinking.

title: Dogs see in black and white.
verification: Dogs are dichromats — two cone types (blue and yellow-green), similar to red-green colorblind humans. They see colors, just fewer than trichromatic humans. Dogs cannot distinguish red from green well, but blue, yellow, and gray are clearly differentiated. Their visual system is also stronger in low-light (more rods) and motion detection. The 'black and white' folklore is wrong; the more accurate version is 'dogs see a reduced color palette.' Hunting and herding behaviors confirm: dogs respond to color cues in field tests.
wisdom: Closer to colorblind than to gray-scale. The proverb under-sells dog vision. They see less than us in color, more than us in motion and dim light. Different system, not deficient.

title: Whale songs travel hundreds of miles.
verification: Blue whale (Balaenoptera musculus) vocalizations at 10-39 Hz are among the loudest natural sounds on Earth (~188 dB underwater). At those frequencies, sound propagates through the deep SOFAR channel (~1 km depth) with minimal attenuation. Pre-industrial estimates: blue whale calls audible across ocean basins (1000+ km). Modern shipping noise has reduced effective range to ~100 km in many areas. Humpback whale (Megaptera novaeangliae) songs are higher frequency and shorter range (~30 km) but more melodically complex. The proverb refers to either species' range, and is accurate.
wisdom: Whale acoustics built the original undersea network. Shipping noise has degraded it. The proverb survives because the ocean physics that supported it is still there, even if humans now jam the signal.

title: Geese fly in V formation to save energy.
verification: Each goose generates wingtip vortices producing an upwash to the side; following birds in the V can ride this upwash, reducing required power. Lissaman & Shollenberger (1970) calculated up to 70% range improvement for 25-bird V over solo flight; empirical measurements (Weimerskirch et al. 2001, instrumented pelicans) show heart rates 11-14% lower in formation than solo. The lead bird does the most work; geese rotate the lead position. Same principle applies to cyclists drafting, military planes in formation, and migrating crane flocks.
wisdom: Birds discovered drafting before bicycles existed. The V is an energy budget made visible. Lead birds work harder; following birds work less; rotation keeps the group efficient.

title: Crows are as smart as primates.
verification: Corvid cognition research (Bernd Heinrich, Nathan Emery, Nicola Clayton): crows, ravens, and jays demonstrate tool use, tool manufacture (New Caledonian crows bend wire), planning for future needs, individual face recognition (Marzluff 2010: crows remember specific human faces for years and pass the memory to offspring), mirror self-recognition (debated), and mathematical concepts. Their brains are smaller than primate brains but have higher neuron density; total forebrain neurons in some corvids exceed several primate species. The proverb is supported by 30+ years of cognitive ethology.
wisdom: Different brain architecture, comparable cognitive output. The proverb collapses 30 years of corvid research into one line. Don't underestimate the bird looking down at you from the telephone wire.

title: A bee colony is a single super-organism.
verification: Honey bee colonies (Apis mellifera) display superorganism properties: collective decision-making (Seeley 2010 'Honeybee Democracy' — swarm site selection via waggle-dance voting), distributed regulation (worker thermoregulation, brood care, defense), and reproductive specialization (queen + sterile workers). No individual bee survives long without the colony; the colony survives generations through reproductive cycles. The 'super-organism' framework (Wheeler 1911) applies. Genetically: workers share ~75% of their genes with sisters via haplodiploid sex determination, which mathematically explains worker altruism (Hamilton 1964 inclusive fitness).
wisdom: The bee is to the hive what your cell is to you. Hamilton's inclusive fitness math explains why the bee dies to save the hive — the gene survives. Evolution wrote a single organism distributed across 50,000 bodies.

title: Dolphins sleep with half their brain at a time.
verification: Cetaceans (dolphins, whales, porpoises) display unihemispheric slow-wave sleep (USWS) — only one cerebral hemisphere shows slow-wave EEG patterns at a time. The other hemisphere remains active. Mechanism allows dolphins to surface for breathing (voluntary in cetaceans, unlike mammals with autonomic breathing) and watch for predators while still getting slow-wave rest. Each hemisphere sleeps ~4 hours/day; total daily sleep ~8 hours. Same adaptation in some sharks, manatees, and various birds during migration. The proverb is biologically precise.
wisdom: Evolution solved the 'need to breathe but also sleep' problem by parallelizing the brain. The proverb is one of those biological facts that sounds made up and isn't.

title: Bears don't truly hibernate — they enter torpor.
verification: Hibernation (true): body temperature drops to within a few degrees of ambient (5-10°C), metabolic rate drops to 2-5% of normal, animal is essentially unresponsive. Examples: ground squirrels, marmots. Torpor (bears): body temperature drops 5-7°C only (still 31-35°C), metabolic rate drops to ~25% of normal, bears can rouse quickly when disturbed. Female bears give birth during torpor and nurse cubs. The technical distinction matters because true hibernators have different physiological controls; bears were misclassified for decades. Black bears, brown bears, polar bears all torpor; some smaller bears (sun bears, sloth bears) don't.
wisdom: Words matter. Bears do something impressive but not what the textbook said. Carolyn Carey and others corrected this in the 1980s. The proverb prefers precision.

title: Ants follow chemical trails.
verification: Foraging ants deposit pheromone trails (species-specific blends of small organic compounds, often containing methyl-3-butenyl-2-one or similar). The chemistry: returning foragers laying pheromone on successful trips reinforce the path; pheromone evaporates from unused trails. The shortest path to food becomes the strongest-marked, drawing more ants — a positive feedback loop that solves shortest-path optimization without central planning. Computer scientists adapted this into 'ant colony optimization' algorithms (Dorigo 1992) for combinatorial problems. The proverb is biology that became algorithm.
wisdom: Distributed optimization, no central planner needed. The ant colony solves traveling-salesman variants every day. The proverb is the bridge between biology and computer science.

title: Octopuses are intelligent.
verification: Cephalopod intelligence (Mather, Anderson, Wood research; Sy Montgomery 'Soul of an Octopus'): octopuses demonstrate problem-solving (opening screw-cap jars, navigating mazes), tool use (carrying coconut shells), individual recognition of humans, play behavior, and rapid learning. Their nervous system has ~500 million neurons (vs ~16 billion in humans) — but distributed: ~2/3 of neurons are in the arms, each capable of semi-autonomous processing. Their cognitive architecture is alien — closer to a distributed network than a centralized brain. Octopuses also display unprecedented chromatophore-based dynamic camouflage with no central color-vision system (their skin can sense light directly).
wisdom: Different architecture, recognizable intelligence. The octopus arrived at cognition via 600 million years of independent evolution from the line that produced vertebrates. Convergent intelligence is rarer than we knew.

title: Spider webs are precisely geometric.
verification: Orb-weaver spiders (Araneidae) construct webs in a highly stereotyped sequence: radial threads first, then a spiral 'auxiliary' frame, then the sticky capture spiral built outward from center using the auxiliary as a guide. The geometric regularity (radii separated by consistent angles; spiral pitch ~constant) is achieved without measurement tools — the spider uses leg-length proprioception. Different species produce different web geometries reliably (Araneus diadematus orbs, Latrodectus tangle webs, Linyphia sheet webs). Web architecture is so consistent that arachnologists identify species from web pattern alone. The silk itself is one of nature's strongest materials (tensile strength ~1 GPa, comparable to steel).
wisdom: Geometry without rulers, engineering without blueprints. The spider builds a perfectly proportional structure using only its body as measuring tool. Evolution wrote the algorithm; the spider runs it without thinking.

title: Bees communicate locations with a dance.
verification: Karl von Frisch (1923, Nobel 1973): the honey bee waggle dance encodes direction and distance to a food source. Direction: angle of the waggle run from vertical equals angle of food source from the sun (angle compensated for time of day if sun has moved). Distance: duration of the waggle phase, ~75 ms per 100 m. Information content: ~3 bits of direction + 4 bits of distance per dance. Receiving bees can find new food sources hundreds of meters away with accuracy of ~10-15%. The waggle dance is one of the few animal communication systems formally established to encode abstract spatial information.
wisdom: The bee's dance is a vector. Frisch decoded it; the bees use it. Animal communication is more sophisticated than humans long assumed; the dance is the canonical example.

title: Wolf packs are family groups, not alphas.
verification: Schenkel (1947) and Mech (1970) early work in captive wolves produced the 'alpha-dominance' model. Mech himself reversed this in 1999 after 13 years observing wild wolves on Ellesmere Island: wild wolf packs are extended families — breeding pair and their offspring. The 'alphas' are simply the parents; there's no daily dominance contest among unrelated adults. Captive packs (unrelated wolves forced together) DO display dominance behavior, but that's an artifact of artificial composition. Mech has spent the past 25 years trying to retract the alpha model that he co-popularized; popular culture has largely ignored the correction. The proverb represents the corrected science.
wisdom: The model based on captive observation was wrong. Mech corrected it; pop culture didn't get the memo. Wolves work like extended families, not corporate hierarchies. Calling someone an 'alpha male' is using biology that was overturned in 1999.

title: Birds can count.
verification: Multiple species (corvids, parrots, pigeons) demonstrate small-number discrimination up to 4-7 items. New Zealand robins (Hunt et al. 2008) can match counts of items presented sequentially. African grey parrots (Alex, trained by Pepperberg) used numbers up to 8 in vocal English. Crows in Wolfgang Schraml's experiments distinguish 1-vs-5 stimulus arrays. The cognitive ability is similar to that of small children before they learn counting words. Numerical cognition is older and more widespread than previously thought; it isn't a uniquely human capability.
