When rainfall rate > infiltration capacity, excess becomes surface runoff. Paved surfaces have near-zero infiltration → 100% runoff. Forested watersheds have high infiltration → low peak flows. Urban flood mitigation: green roofs, permeable pavement, retention ponds — all engineering infiltration capacity back into otherwise paved areas. The proverb is one of hydrology's foundational equations.
wisdom: What the soil can absorb determines what the stream gets. Pave the soil and the stream gets all of it at once. Urban flood control is mostly restoring infiltration the city eliminated.

title: Groundwater flows from high head to low head.
verification: Darcy's Law (Henri Darcy, 1856): Q = -K·A·(dh/dl), where Q is volumetric flow rate, K is hydraulic conductivity, A is cross-section area, dh/dl is hydraulic gradient. Discovered while designing public water systems for Dijon. Holds for laminar flow through porous media (most soils, fractured rock, gravel beds). Used in: well design, aquifer modeling, dam seepage analysis, contaminant transport (Love Canal, Hinkley CA chromium plume). Hydraulic conductivity varies 10 orders of magnitude: gravel K ≈ 10⁻² m/s; clay K ≈ 10⁻⁹ m/s. The proverb is the equation behind most groundwater engineering.
wisdom: Water finds the path of least resistance through dirt. Darcy quantified it; engineers design with it. Contaminant plumes follow Darcy's Law decade after decade — the proverb tracks lawsuits and lawsuits chase the proverb.

title: Glycemic index measures how fast a food raises blood sugar.
verification: Glycemic index (GI): area under blood glucose curve over 2 hours after eating, relative to pure glucose (GI = 100). Low GI (<55): legumes, most non-starchy vegetables, whole grains. Medium (56-69): brown rice, sweet potato. High (≥70): white bread, white rice, watermelon, instant oatmeal, most processed foods. Glycemic load (GL) = GI × carbs per serving / 100 — more clinically useful than GI alone. High-GI/GL diets correlate with type 2 diabetes risk, weight gain, and inflammation. Low-GI diets improve glycemic control in diabetics by HbA1c ~0.5 percentage points. The proverb is nutrition science's most-quoted single number.
wisdom: Same calories, different blood sugar profiles. White bread spikes; lentils don't. The proverb captures one of the few nutritional levers that survives rigorous trials.

title: Complete protein contains all nine essential amino acids.
verification: Nine essential amino acids (human body cannot synthesize): histidine, isoleucine, leucine, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, threonine, tryptophan, valine. Complete proteins (contain all nine in adequate ratio): meat, fish, eggs, dairy, soy, quinoa, buckwheat. Incomplete proteins (deficient in at least one): most plant proteins. Combining strategies: rice + beans = complete (rice deficient in lysine, beans in methionine — they complement). Frances Moore Lappé's 'Diet for a Small Planet' (1971) emphasized combining; modern research shows combining within a day is sufficient, not necessarily within a meal. The proverb is a useful summary; the practical application is broader than the strict interpretation.
wisdom: Nine boxes to check. Animal sources tick all nine in one bite; plant sources need combinations. The proverb is decent nutrition; the strict 'every meal must be complete' was an overreach that's been corrected.

title: A nutritional calorie is actually a kilocalorie.
verification: Physics calorie (cal) = 4.184 joules. Nutritional Calorie (Cal, sometimes capitalized) = kilocalorie (kcal) = 4184 J = 1000 cal. Food labels confusingly use 'calorie' to mean kcal. Body's metabolic rate: ~60-100 W resting (5,000-8,500 kJ/day = 1,200-2,000 kcal). A McDonald's Big Mac: 550 kcal = 2,300 kJ. Energy density: carbs and protein 4 kcal/g, fat 9 kcal/g, alcohol 7 kcal/g, fiber ~2 kcal/g. The unit confusion has persisted since the 19th century. Some European countries label in kJ instead; same physics, less ambiguity.
wisdom: A food label that says '500 calories' means 500,000 physics-calories. The label dropped the prefix a century ago and the confusion has never fully recovered. The proverb is one of nutrition's worst pieces of nomenclature.

title: VO2 max measures cardiovascular fitness.
verification: VO2 max: maximum rate of oxygen uptake during exhaustive exercise, in mL O₂/(kg·min). Elite endurance athletes: 70-85+ mL/kg/min. Healthy young adult: 35-50. Sedentary middle-aged: 25-35. Predicts cardiovascular health better than weight, BMI, or blood pressure (Mandsager et al. 2018, JAMA Network: low VO2 max correlated with mortality at rates exceeding smoking and diabetes). Improvable: structured aerobic training increases VO2 max by 15-25% over 6 months. Genetic ceiling exists (some research suggests 30-40% of variance is genetic). The proverb is one of the most powerful single health metrics — and one of the least known.
wisdom: Heart-and-lung-and-mitochondria capacity in one number. Higher is longer life on average. Most chronic-disease risks beat predictions when this number is high.

title: Resting metabolic rate is what you burn just being alive.
verification: Resting metabolic rate (RMR): energy expenditure at complete rest, measured in kcal/day. Mifflin-St Jeor equation (1990, gold standard): RMR (men) = 10·W + 6.25·H − 5·age + 5; (women) same but −161. Where W = weight (kg), H = height (cm). Average adult: ~1,400-1,700 kcal/day. Adds up to 60-75% of total daily energy expenditure for sedentary adults. Factors: muscle mass (highest contributor; ~13 kcal/lb/day at rest), thyroid function, age (declines ~1-2% per decade after 30), genetics. The proverb is metabolic math at its most basic.
wisdom: You burn calories sleeping. Most of your daily expenditure is just keeping the lights on. Exercise adds; muscle increases the base; the proverb names the floor.

title: Higher entropy means harder to predict.
verification: Shannon entropy: H = -Σ p(x)·log₂ p(x). Fair coin: H = 1 bit (2 outcomes, each p=0.5). Biased coin (90/10): H ≈ 0.47 bits (more predictable, lower entropy). Uniform 6-sided die: H = log₂(6) ≈ 2.58 bits. Maximum entropy for n outcomes: log₂(n) (when all equally likely). Compression algorithms work by exploiting low entropy in source data: English text ~1-1.5 bits per character (vs theoretical max 8 for ASCII). Password strength is exactly this — entropy of the keyspace determines brute-force resistance. The proverb is information theory's central yardstick.
wisdom: Predictability and information content are inverse. A predictable message tells you little new; an unpredictable one tells you a lot. The proverb is the universe's unit of surprise.

title: Reactions cross an activation energy barrier.
verification: Arrhenius equation (1889): k = A·exp(-Ea/RT), where k is reaction rate, Ea is activation energy, R is gas constant, T is temperature. Reactions need to climb a transition-state energy barrier. Higher T → higher fraction of molecules with enough energy → faster reaction. Rule of thumb: rate doubles every ~10°C increase (rough; depends on Ea). Catalysts lower Ea without being consumed (enzymes do this in biology). Without activation barriers, all favorable reactions would happen instantaneously — the world would be in chemical equilibrium and life impossible. The proverb is why your car engine works (combustion) and why your body works (enzyme kinetics).
wisdom: Energy is needed to start a reaction, even one that releases more later. Without the barrier, the universe runs to equilibrium too fast for life. The proverb is one of chemistry's most consequential constants.

title: A buffer resists changes in pH.
verification: Buffer: solution containing a weak acid (HA) and its conjugate base (A⁻) that absorbs added acid or base while maintaining nearly constant pH. Henderson-Hasselbalch equation: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]). Maximum buffering capacity when [A⁻] = [HA], i.e., pH = pKa. Biological buffers: blood bicarbonate buffer maintains pH 7.35-7.45 (deviations >0.3 are medical emergencies — acidosis/alkalosis); intracellular phosphate buffer; protein side chains. Acid rain damages ecosystems precisely because freshwater lakes have limited buffering against added H⁺. The proverb is biochemistry's stability mechanism.
wisdom: The body buffers blood pH within 0.1 unit of 7.4 across enormous metabolic challenges. The proverb is one of chemistry's most life-critical mechanisms.

title: Gothic cathedrals stand because of pointed arches.
verification: Pointed (Gothic) arch redirects load more vertically than the rounded Roman arch, reducing the outward thrust against walls. Combined with flying buttresses (external supports absorbing lateral thrust), Gothic builders achieved unprecedented heights and large window openings. Notre-Dame de Paris (begun 1163), Chartres (1194), Cologne (1248) — 30-40m vault heights impossible in Romanesque. Force analysis confirmed by modern FEA: thrust line stays within the cross-section of wall + buttress system, even with 800 years of accumulated settling. The cathedrals are stone proofs of a geometry theorem.
wisdom: The pointed arch was an answer to a geometry problem the Romans hadn't solved. Stone built upward 40 meters and stayed there for 800 years. Engineering as worship.

title: Phi — the golden ratio — appears everywhere in design.
verification: φ = (1+√5)/2 ≈ 1.618. Defined: a/b = (a+b)/a. Fibonacci sequence ratios converge to φ. Real appearances: phyllotaxis (leaf spirals on plants — golden angle 137.5° optimizes light capture), some chambered nautilus growth, viral capsid icosahedra. Architectural claims: Parthenon, Pyramid of Giza, Notre-Dame are often cited as containing φ — but careful measurement shows many are coincidental or post-hoc fits. Le Corbusier explicitly used φ in his Modulor system. Cosmetic claims (faces, paintings) mostly fail rigorous testing. Math is real; aesthetic universality is overstated.
wisdom: Real in plants and nautilus shells. Overclaimed in architecture and art. The proverb is half-mysticism, half-mathematics; the math is the durable half.

title: Magnitude 7 earthquake is 32 times stronger than magnitude 6.
verification: Richter scale (1935) and modern moment magnitude (Mw) scale are both logarithmic in seismic energy release. Each whole-number step on the magnitude scale represents ~32× more energy released (specifically, 10^1.5 = ~31.6). Magnitude 5: minor damage; 6: moderate; 7: major; 8: great (Tōhoku 2011 was 9.0 = ~1,000× magnitude 6). Frequency falls off logarithmically too: roughly 10× fewer earthquakes for each magnitude step. The Richter math has been refined (Mw replaces ML for large quakes) but the energy-step ratio holds. A 'mag 6 vs 7' difference is enormous, not incremental.
wisdom: The scale hides how much worse each step is. Mag 7 is 32× the energy of mag 6; mag 9 is a million times mag 5. The proverb is what most people don't realize about every earthquake news report.

title: The ground beneath your feet is moving slowly.
verification: Plate tectonics (Wegener's continental drift 1912; vindicated 1960s with sea-floor magnetic stripe data): Earth's lithosphere divided into ~15 major plates moving 2-10 cm/year (about the speed fingernails grow). Mid-ocean ridges produce new crust; subduction zones consume old crust. Earthquakes, volcanoes, mountain-building all happen at plate boundaries. The Atlantic widens ~2.5 cm/year; the San Andreas Fault slips ~3.5 cm/year. GPS now measures continental motion in real time, confirming the theoretical predictions to ~0.1 mm/year. The proverb is one of geology's foundational reframes.
wisdom: Continents drift; you're moving with them. North America heads west at fingernail-growth speed. Wegener was mocked for decades; sea-floor magnetism vindicated him posthumously.

title: Update your beliefs with evidence — Bayes' theorem.
verification: P(H|E) = P(E|H) · P(H) / P(E). Bayes (1763, posthumous). Updates the prior P(H) given evidence E into the posterior P(H|E). Foundation of: medical screening (false-positive paradox), spam filters (probability email is spam given words), machine learning (Bayesian inference), legal evidence weighing (when applied correctly). Most people, including doctors, get conditional-probability problems wrong without explicit Bayes computation. The proverb names the right way to update beliefs; the actual computation is rarely intuitive but always defensible.
wisdom: Beliefs are probabilities; evidence shifts them. The math is unambiguous; the intuition is unreliable. Bayes' theorem is the corrective for every 'I knew it!' that wasn't.

title: Stock prices wander randomly in the short term.
verification: Efficient Market Hypothesis (Fama 1970): if information is fully incorporated into prices, future price changes are unpredictable (random walk). Empirical: daily stock returns are approximately log-normal with no significant autocorrelation in the short term. However: anomalies exist (momentum, value premium, January effect, post-earnings drift). Long-run direction is up (equity risk premium ~5-7% real). The 'random walk in the short term, drift up in the long term' is the standard finance model. Active managers consistently underperform index funds — strong evidence that markets are mostly efficient.
wisdom: Tomorrow's price is mostly unpredictable. Next decade's price is mostly up. The proverb is finance's most studied conjecture and roughly correct, with footnotes.

title: Markov chains — the next step depends only on the current state.
verification: Markov property: P(X_{n+1} | X_n, X_{n-1}, ..., X_0) = P(X_{n+1} | X_n). History before now is irrelevant given the current state. Andrey Markov (1906). Applications: PageRank (web as Markov chain over hyperlinks), speech recognition (Hidden Markov Models), board games (chutes & ladders, Monopoly), epidemiology (SIR models), text generation. Hidden Markov Models (state hidden, observations visible) power most pre-deep-learning NLP and bioinformatics. The proverb is simplification, not always realistic — but extraordinarily useful when applicable.
wisdom: Forget where you came from; only the current state matters. A useful simplification that turns intractable problems tractable. Most of speech recognition before deep learning ran on this.

title: Independent events: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B).
verification: Two events A and B are independent iff P(A ∩ B) = P(A) · P(B). Equivalently, P(A|B) = P(A) and P(B|A) = P(B) — knowing one doesn't update the other. Coin flips are independent. Roll-then-flip is independent. Two cards from same deck without replacement are NOT independent (the first card affects probabilities for the second). Common error: assuming independence when it doesn't hold (Sally Clark case: prosecutor incorrectly multiplied two SIDS probabilities as if independent; siblings share genetics and environment, so not). Independence is the simplifying assumption behind most basic probability — easy to assume, sometimes wrong.
wisdom: Multiply only when truly independent. The proverb is one of statistics' most-quoted formulas and one of its most-misapplied. When events share causes, multiplication overstates how unlikely the conjunction is.

title: With 50 people, two share a birthday 97% of the time.
verification: P(no collision among n people) = 365!/(365^n · (365-n)!). At n=23: ~50.7% chance of a shared birthday (the famous 'paradox'). At n=50: ~97%. At n=70: 99.9%. At n=366: 100% (pigeonhole). Cryptography analog (the birthday attack): a hash function with N possible outputs is vulnerable to collision after ~√N tries, not N. A 64-bit hash is breakable in ~2^32 (~4 billion) tries, not 2^64. Why MD5 (128-bit) collisions are findable today and SHA-256 still isn't. The proverb is the same math at two scales.
wisdom: Counting pairs, not people. Collisions arrive much sooner than naive intuition expects. The same surprise math hits both birthday-coincidence math and hash-function cryptanalysis.

title: The human brain has ~86 billion neurons.
verification: Herculano-Houzel et al. (2009) used isotropic fractionator method: 86.1 ± 8.1 billion neurons in adult male human brain (16.3 billion in cortex; 69 billion in cerebellum; rest distributed). Glial cells in equal number (previous 'glia 10:1 over neurons' was wrong). Connectivity: each cortical neuron averages 7,000 synapses → ~10^14 total synapses. Comparison: African elephant has 257 billion neurons (most in cerebellum); human cortex still has the most. Bumblebee brain: ~1 million neurons but capable of counting, simple problem-solving. The proverb is neuroscience's most-quoted single number.
wisdom: The number is real but not the whole story. Connectivity matters more than count; cortical vs cerebellar location matters; integration patterns matter. The proverb is the magnitude; the architecture is what makes it consequential.

title: Myelinated axons fire 100 times faster than unmyelinated.
verification: Action potential propagation in unmyelinated axons: ~1 m/s. In myelinated axons (insulated by glia, with regular gaps called Nodes of Ranvier): up to 120 m/s — 100×+ faster due to saltatory conduction (signal jumps node to node). Myelin loss: multiple sclerosis, Guillain-Barré syndrome — direct motor and sensory deficits because conduction slows or fails. Babies' axons partially unmyelinated → slower reflexes; full myelination completes by mid-20s. Mammals myelinate; invertebrates don't (limiting their neural conduction speed). The proverb is why your reflexes work as fast as they do.
wisdom: Glia don't think but they make thinking fast. The proverb is the difference between you ducking the ball and not.

title: Dopamine signals reward prediction error.
verification: Schultz et al. (1997) recorded VTA dopamine neurons in monkeys. Neurons fire MORE when reward exceeds expectation, BASELINE when reward matches expectation, LESS when reward falls short of expectation. This is reward prediction error — dopamine is the brain's learning signal, not its 'pleasure' signal as pop neuroscience often claims. Implications: addictive substances hijack the dopaminergic prediction system (artificially high signals); ML's reinforcement learning explicitly uses prediction error to update Q-values. The proverb is one of the most replicated findings in modern neuroscience.
wisdom: Dopamine doesn't measure pleasure. It measures the gap between what you expected and what you got. The proverb is one of the most-misquoted neuroscience facts and one of the most consequential.

title: R0 — how many people one sick person infects on average.
verification: Basic reproduction number R0: expected secondary cases per primary case in a fully susceptible population. Measles: 12-18 (one of the most contagious). COVID-19 original: ~2.5; Delta: ~5; Omicron: ~8-10. Seasonal flu: 1.3. R0 > 1 → epidemic spreads exponentially; R0 < 1 → outbreak dies out. Herd immunity threshold: (1 - 1/R0) of population needs immunity to stop spread. Measles: ~95% vaccination required. Adjusted R_t (effective reproduction number) accounts for behavioral changes and existing immunity. The proverb is epidemiology's central number for any communicable disease.
wisdom: Above 1 the disease spreads exponentially. Below 1 it fades. Public health interventions reduce R_t to <1 by any means available — vaccination, distancing, treatment. The proverb is the math behind every quarantine policy.

title: Statisticians correct for the family-wise error rate.
verification: Family-wise error rate (FWER): probability of at least one Type I error in a 'family' of related hypotheses. If testing N hypotheses each at α: FWER ≈ 1 - (1-α)^N (approaches 1 quickly). Bonferroni correction: use α/N per test → FWER ≤ α. Šidák correction: 1-(1-α)^(1/N) — slightly less conservative than Bonferroni. Holm-Bonferroni: sequential, more powerful than plain Bonferroni. Tukey-Kramer for all pairwise comparisons. Each method trades power for false-positive control. Genomic studies (thousands of tests) use False Discovery Rate (FDR) controls (Benjamini-Hochberg) — controls expected proportion of false discoveries rather than any single false positive.
wisdom: Multiple tests stack failure chances. Correcting for FWER preserves the overall Type I rate. The proverb is the difference between defensible and laughable statistics.

title: You can compress high dimensions while preserving distances.
verification: Johnson-Lindenstrauss lemma (1984): N points in any-dimensional space can be projected to k = O(log N / ε²) dimensions with all pairwise distances preserved within factor (1±ε). Surprising because k doesn't depend on original dimension — only on N and tolerance. Applications: locality-sensitive hashing (similarity search at scale), neural network embedding compression, FAISS vector databases. Random projection (Gaussian matrix multiplication) achieves the bound. The math is one of the surprising shortcuts of high-dimensional geometry.
wisdom: High-dimensional data can be projected to lower dimensions while preserving the things that matter (distances). A million-dimensional vector compresses to a thousand without losing similarity structure. The proverb makes modern semantic search possible.

title: SVD decomposes any matrix into rotations and stretches.
verification: Singular Value Decomposition: A = UΣV^T, where U and V are orthogonal (rotations/reflections), Σ is diagonal (stretches). Works for ANY matrix (rectangular, singular, etc.). Singular values (entries of Σ) are non-negative; ordered by magnitude they reveal the importance of each direction. Applications: low-rank approximation (Eckart-Young: best rank-k approximation truncates SVD), recommendation systems (Netflix Prize), latent semantic analysis, image compression, noise reduction, PCA (PCA = SVD on mean-centered data). One of linear algebra's most general and useful decompositions.
wisdom: Every linear transformation is a rotation, a stretch, and another rotation. The proverb compresses one of the deepest results in linear algebra. Recommendations, search, and noise reduction all run on this decomposition.

title: A digital signature proves who signed and what was signed.
verification: Digital signature scheme (RSA, ECDSA, Ed25519): signer hashes the document, then encrypts the hash with their private key. Verifier hashes the same document, decrypts the signature with the signer's public key, compares. If they match: document is unmodified AND signed by the holder of the private key. Used in: HTTPS certificates, software distribution (Apple/Microsoft code signing), email (PGP, S/MIME), blockchain transactions, document attestation. Engine's own promotion receipts use Ed25519. The math: signing requires the private key; verifying requires only the public key — asymmetric cryptography's central trick.
wisdom: Two properties from one operation: integrity (nothing changed) and authenticity (right signer). The proverb is the foundation of every secure transaction on the internet.

title: CSV looks simple; it isn't.
verification: RFC 4180 (2005) attempted to standardize CSV. Edge cases: 1) quoted fields containing commas ("Smith, John"); 2) quoted fields containing newlines; 3) escaping quotes within quoted fields (""""); 4) BOM markers for UTF-8; 5) line endings (CRLF vs LF); 6) Excel's locale-specific separator (semicolon in German, comma in English). 'CSV' files in the wild violate RFC 4180 constantly; every CSV parser must be liberal. The format is older than the spec; the spec described what the format almost was. Pandas pd.read_csv has 50+ parameters for handling the variations. The proverb is a programmer's-experience-distilled.
wisdom: What looks like a comma-separated file is actually a small dialect of a poorly-specified format. The proverb is what every data engineer learns the hard way. Use a proper parser; don't write your own.

title: JSON is six data types and a few brackets.
verification: JSON (ECMA-404, RFC 8259): six types — object (key-value map), array, string, number, boolean, null. No date type, no comments, no trailing commas. Strict subset of JavaScript object syntax. Designed by Douglas Crockford ~2001 because XML was too verbose. Dominant data interchange format since ~2010. Variants: JSON5 (relaxed, supports comments); JSONC (comments only); BSON (MongoDB binary form); MessagePack and Protocol Buffers (binary alternatives). Numbers in JSON are IEEE 754 doubles — large integers lose precision above 2^53. The proverb is web infrastructure's most-used format and one of its smallest specs.
wisdom: Six types is enough for the web. Smaller than XML, simpler than YAML, more universal than CSV. The proverb is what made every modern API possible.

title: Innocent until proven guilty.
verification: Presumption of innocence: defendant in a criminal trial is presumed innocent; burden of proof on the prosecution to prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt. Roots in Roman law (Digest of Justinian: 'Ei incumbit probatio qui dicit, non qui negat'). UN Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948, Article 11). US: explicitly in Coffin v. United States (1895). The principle is asymmetric: a wrongful conviction (false positive) is worse than a wrongful acquittal (false negative) — Blackstone's ratio: 'better that ten guilty escape than one innocent suffer.' Implementation varies by jurisdiction; the principle is near-universal in functioning legal systems.
wisdom: The asymmetry is the point. Convicting the innocent does worse damage than acquitting the guilty, because the wrongful conviction can't be undone and the wrongful acquittal can often be retried. The proverb is Type I/II error policy in law.

title: Old claims expire — statute of limitations.
verification: Statute of limitations: time period after which a civil or criminal claim cannot be filed. Civil claims (contracts, torts): typically 2-6 years depending on type and jurisdiction. Felonies: longer (often 5-7 years) or none (murder typically has no statute of limitations). Rationale: 1) evidence decays (memories fade, witnesses die, documents disappear); 2) defendants need finality; 3) plaintiffs should not sleep on rights. Exceptions: tolling (delays the clock when defendant is hiding, plaintiff is a minor, fraud was concealed). Different statutes apply to different claims; complex litigation requires careful tracking. The proverb is one of law's clearest time-bound rules.
wisdom: Justice has an expiration date for most claims. Not from injustice, but from the practical decay of evidence. The proverb names a procedural rule that has been argued about since Roman times.

title: A contract needs offer, acceptance, and consideration.
verification: Common-law contract formation requires three elements: 1) offer (definite proposal by offeror); 2) acceptance (matching acceptance by offeree, mirror-image rule); 3) consideration (something of value exchanged — money, services, promise to forbear). Without consideration, the agreement is gratuitous and unenforceable (with narrow exceptions: charitable pledges, promissory estoppel). UCC for goods relaxes mirror-image. Civil-law jurisdictions (most of Europe, Quebec) use different framework (causa instead of consideration) but reach similar results. The proverb is the basic test every law student learns.
wisdom: A handshake plus a promise plus an exchange. Without all three, no binding contract. The proverb is contract law's foundation, taught in week one of every legal education.

title: Tides are predictable years in advance.
verification: Tide prediction uses harmonic analysis: superposition of sinusoidal components at known frequencies (M2 = principal lunar 12.42h; S2 = principal solar 12h; N2 = lunar elliptic; K1 = lunisolar diurnal; etc.). Lord Kelvin built the first mechanical tide-predicting machine in 1872. Modern NOAA tables give tide times accurate to within 5 minutes for routine ports. Anomalies: storm surge can elevate tides 3-5 m above prediction in hurricanes; tsunamis are non-harmonic events not captured. Predictions valid decades ahead barring catastrophic earth-orbit changes. The proverb is one of oceanography's most reliable forecasts.
wisdom: Tides are deterministic to better than 5 minutes a year out. Weather a week out struggles; tides a decade out succeed. Astronomical math vs atmospheric chaos.

title: Ocean currents distribute Earth's heat.
verification: Thermohaline circulation (ocean conveyor belt): density-driven flow of seawater driven by temperature (thermo-) and salinity (-haline). Warm tropical water flows north in the Atlantic, releases heat to atmosphere, becomes denser as it cools, sinks near Greenland, returns south as deep cold water. Cycle takes ~1,000 years. Carries ~1.4×10^15 W (1.4 petawatts) of heat — roughly 1% of total solar input redistributed by ocean. Climate-critical: weakening Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) is one of the IPCC's tipping points; would chill Northwestern Europe dramatically. The proverb is one of climate science's load-bearing systems.
wisdom: The ocean is a slow heat engine. It moves more heat than the atmosphere does. Climate models that simulate ocean circulation poorly miss the planet's biggest thermal flywheel.

title: CO2 in air dissolves in seawater — and acidifies it.
verification: CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻. Increased atmospheric CO₂ → more dissolved CO₂ in oceans → lower pH. Pre-industrial surface ocean pH ~8.2; current ~8.1; predicted ~7.8 by 2100 — sounds small, but pH is logarithmic, so ~30% increase in H⁺. Effects: shell-forming organisms (corals, mollusks, coccolithophores) struggle to build CaCO₃ in lower-pH water. Already-observed coral bleaching, oyster larvae die-offs in Pacific Northwest, projected shifts in marine food web. The ocean has absorbed ~30% of anthropogenic CO₂ since 1750. Faster than any natural acidification event on geological record (which is saying something).
wisdom: The ocean is a buffer absorbing what the atmosphere can't keep. The pH drops while the climate stays slightly less hot than it would. Both halves of the bargain are now under stress.

title: Language shapes thought — partially.
verification: Sapir-Whorf hypothesis (1929-40s): the structure of a language affects its speakers' cognition. Strong version (linguistic determinism): cognition is entirely shaped by language — debunked. Weak version (linguistic relativity): language influences but doesn't determine — supported by modern evidence. Color terminology research: speakers of languages with distinct blue/green terms ('grue' vs separate) show measurable but small differences in color discrimination tasks (Winawer et al. 2007). Time/space metaphors vary by language. Number-system language affects mental math performance. The big effects claimed in the 1950s-60s were oversold; the small effects are real and reproducible.
wisdom: Language nudges thought; it doesn't dictate it. The proverb survived the strong version's collapse and quietly stands as the weak version. Bilinguals report subtle perception shifts; that's the proverb's real claim.

title: All languages descend from older languages.
verification: Comparative linguistics reconstructs proto-languages from systematic sound correspondences across descendant languages (Grimm's law for Germanic; verner's law for accent placement). Major reconstructed proto-languages: Proto-Indo-European (~4500 BCE, ancestor of English, Greek, Latin, Sanskrit, Persian, ~3 billion speakers' worth of descendants), Proto-Afroasiatic, Proto-Sino-Tibetan, Proto-Austronesian, Proto-Bantu. Languages drift at ~10-20% lexical replacement per millennium (glottochronology, contested but suggestive). Language families are rigorous; proposed deeper relationships (Nostratic, Amerind) are speculative. The proverb is solid historical linguistics.
wisdom: Language has a family tree. English's grandfather is Old English; great-great-great-great-grandfather is Proto-Indo-European. The 19th-century Brothers Grimm did the linguistics; the 20th formalized it.

title: Every language has a phoneme inventory.
verification: Phonemes: distinct sound units that signal meaning differences in a language. English: ~44 (24 consonants, 20 vowels). Rotokas (Papua New Guinea): 11 phonemes (smallest documented). !Xóõ (southern Africa): ~110 phonemes including click consonants (largest). Average ~30 phonemes. Phonemic restructuring during language acquisition: infants distinguish all phonemes universally; by 12 months, lose discrimination for sounds not in their language (Kuhl 1992). Adults can re-learn with effort. The proverb is one of phonetics' foundations.
wisdom: A finite alphabet of sounds, learned by 12 months, drives every language. Lose the ones your language doesn't use and re-learning is hard. The proverb is why Japanese speakers struggle with English /r/ vs /l/ — Japanese doesn't distinguish them.

title: Half your DNA from each parent.
verification: Diploid organism: each cell has two copies of each chromosome (one maternal, one paternal). Gametes (egg, sperm) are haploid — one copy each, produced by meiosis. Fertilization restores diploid count. Exception: mitochondrial DNA is inherited from mother only (sperm mitochondria are destroyed at fertilization). Y chromosome is paternal-only. ~50% of nuclear DNA from each parent in expectation; variation from independent assortment and recombination. Implication: siblings share ~50% of variable DNA on average (range 37-61% by chance). Identical twins: 100%. The proverb is genetics' foundational arithmetic.
wisdom: Mom contributes half, dad contributes half. Mitochondria are mom's alone. Y is dad's alone. The proverb is true for most of the genome and has elegant exceptions.

title: Recombination shuffles the genome each generation.
verification: During meiosis (gamete formation), homologous chromosomes pair and exchange segments via crossing over. Average ~1 crossover per chromosome arm per meiosis. Result: each gamete is a unique mosaic of maternal and paternal segments. Independent assortment (Mendel's 2nd law): 23 chromosome pairs × 2 (which copy) × crossing over → 2^23 ≈ 8 million distinct gamete types per person without crossing over; effectively infinite with it. Why siblings (other than identical twins) differ. Why genetic algorithms in computer science mimic this. The proverb is biology's mechanism for variation.
wisdom: No two children of the same parents (except identical twins) get the same genome. The 8-million-gamete combinatorics times recombination makes each child genetically unique. The proverb is why families look related but not identical.

title: Small populations lose genetic diversity by chance.
verification: Genetic drift: random fluctuation in allele frequencies due to finite population size. Rate of drift ∝ 1/N (smaller populations drift faster). Founder effect (subset of population establishes new colony with non-representative allele frequencies): Amish high incidence of Ellis-van Creveld syndrome from single 1700s founder; cheetahs' low genetic diversity from ~10,000-year-ago population bottleneck. Bottleneck effect: catastrophic population reduction leaves only random subset. Conservation biology uses drift math to set minimum viable population sizes (typically 500+ for long-term viability). The proverb is population genetics' core stochastic force.
wisdom: Genetic diversity is fragile. Small populations lose alleles by chance every generation; once lost, hard to restore. The proverb is conservation biology's central math.

title: Octave is 2:1, fifth is 3:2, fourth is 4:3.
verification: Just intonation ratios: octave (2:1), perfect fifth (3:2), perfect fourth (4:3), major third (5:4), minor third (6:5), major sixth (5:3). Discovered by Pythagoreans (~500 BCE) on monochords. Simple ratios produce consonant intervals; complex ratios produce dissonance. Modern equal temperament approximates: octave still exactly 2:1; fifth = 2^(7/12) ≈ 1.4983 (vs 1.5 just) — 1.96 cents flat. The human ear tolerates these tiny deviations; some musicians prefer just intonation for choral or string ensembles. The proverb is acoustics' simplest pattern.
wisdom: Music is integer arithmetic in the air. Pythagoras heard it in hammers and ran the math; we still use his ratios. The proverb is why a piano sounds wrong to a violinist tuning purely.

title: A note contains its harmonics.
verification: A vibrating string or air column produces a fundamental frequency f₀ and integer multiples (harmonics): f₀, 2f₀, 3f₀, ... Each harmonic adds a partial. The relative amplitude of harmonics defines timbre — why a violin and a flute playing the same note sound different. Fourier analysis: any periodic waveform decomposes into a sum of sinusoids. Square waves, sawtooth waves, etc. have characteristic harmonic spectra. The harmonic series mathematically encodes the major and minor scales: ratios within the first 16 harmonics include octave, fifth, fourth, third, minor third, all the intervals of Western music. The proverb is why music has the scales it has.
wisdom: Every note is a chord. The instrument decides which harmonics are loud. Timbre is harmonic recipe. The proverb is why your voice is recognizable in two notes — the harmonic mix is a fingerprint.

title: Counterpoint weaves independent melodies into one fabric.
verification: Counterpoint: writing multiple independent melodic lines that produce harmonic consonance when sounded together. Codified by Fux (Gradus ad Parnassum, 1725) into species counterpoint rules: parallel fifths and octaves forbidden (collapse voices), contrary motion preferred, dissonances must resolve. Bach's fugues are counterpoint at its highest achievement (Art of Fugue, 1750 — unfinished). Constraints (avoid parallel motion, prepare dissonance, etc.) reduce the space of valid combinations to a tractable set; great counterpoint navigates this space. Computer counterpoint generation works by formalizing Fux's rules and searching.
wisdom: Many lines, one whole. The math of voices that respect each other and combine into something neither could be alone. Bach is the proverb's deepest expression.

title: Words are made of meaningful parts — morphemes.
verification: Morpheme: smallest unit of meaning. 'Unbelievable' = un + believe + able (three morphemes). Free morphemes stand alone (believe); bound morphemes attach (un-, -able). English is moderately morphological; Turkish is highly agglutinative (one Turkish 'word' can encode an entire English sentence: 'evlerinizden' = 'from your houses'). Chinese is mostly isolating (one morpheme per word). The proverb is morphology's foundational concept and explains why dictionaries list root forms — derivations are productive.
wisdom: Words are sentences in miniature for highly inflected languages. The proverb compresses morphology into one line.

title: Mountains rise faster than they erode — for a while.
verification: Tectonic uplift rates: Himalayas ~10 mm/year (Indian plate colliding with Eurasian); Sierra Nevada ~1 mm/year; Appalachians ~0 (no longer rising). Erosion rates: 0.01-0.1 mm/year for old worn ranges; 1-10 mm/year for tectonically active mountains. Equilibrium when uplift = erosion. The Appalachians were once as tall as the Himalayas (~480 million years ago); 480 My of erosion at 0.1 mm/year removed ~50 km of elevation. The Himalayas will look like the Appalachians eventually. The proverb compresses geomorphology's central force balance.
wisdom: The mountain is in a battle with the rain. Eventually the rain wins, given enough time. The Appalachians once looked Himalayan; the Himalayas will once look Appalachian.

title: Caves form where rainwater dissolves limestone.
verification: CO₂ + H₂O + CaCO₃ → Ca(HCO₃)₂ (soluble bicarbonate). Atmospheric CO₂ dissolved in rainwater produces mild carbonic acid (pH ~5.6). Over millennia, this water enlarges joints in limestone bedrock, forming caves. Mammoth Cave (Kentucky): 686 km of mapped passage in carboniferous limestone, formed over 10-100 million years. Stalactites and stalagmites grow when supersaturated water re-deposits CaCO₃ at typical rates of 0.1-1 mm/year. Karst topography (sinkholes, springs, caves) is the surface expression. The proverb is geochemistry of the long view.
wisdom: The slowest dissolution in geology is making the biggest underground spaces. Mammoth Cave is acid rain ten million years old. The proverb is the chemistry behind every karst landscape.

title: Latitude predicts climate first; everything else corrects it.
verification: Solar input varies with latitude (cosine law for solar angle, plus day-length variation). Tropics receive ~250 W/m² annual average; polar regions ~80 W/m². This drives the broadest pattern: hot tropics, temperate mid-latitudes, cold polar. Corrections: altitude (Quito at equator is cold because high elevation); ocean currents (Iceland is warmer than Russian Siberia at same latitude because Gulf Stream); continentality (interior of continents has greater temperature swings than coasts); mountain barriers (rain shadows). The first-order rule is latitude; corrections account for the rest. Köppen climate classification systematizes the pattern.
wisdom: Get latitude right and you've got most of climate. Get the corrections right and you've got Köppen's map. The proverb is climatology's order of operations.

title: Every map projection distorts something.
verification: Mathematical theorem (Carl Gauss, Theorema Egregium 1827): a sphere cannot be mapped to a plane without distorting either area, shape, distance, or direction. Mercator (1569): preserves shape and direction (conformal); badly distorts area (Greenland looks larger than Africa). Gall-Peters: preserves area; distorts shape. Robinson: compromise of both. Azimuthal projections: preserve distance from a central point. Web Mercator (used by Google Maps, OSM): variant of Mercator with simplified spherical math. The proverb is cartography's foundational compromise.
wisdom: Every flat map of the round earth lies somewhere. Mercator lies about area; Peters lies about shape; the globe is the only truthful map. Choose which lie serves the purpose.

title: Total solar eclipses require precise geometry.
verification: Solar eclipse: moon passes between Earth and sun, casting shadow on Earth. Coincidence: sun's diameter (1.39M km) is ~400× moon's (3,474 km); sun is ~400× farther from Earth than moon → similar apparent size (~0.5°). Total eclipse requires moon between Earth and sun at near-perfect angular alignment. Average ~2 total eclipses per year somewhere on Earth; specific location experiences total eclipse on average every ~375 years. Path of totality is narrow (~100-200 km wide). NASA computes eclipses to the second decades in advance. The proverb is astronomy at its most elegant: a numerical coincidence enables totality.
wisdom: The sun and moon look the same size from Earth — a coincidence that won't last forever (moon receding ~3.8 cm/year, will lose total eclipses in a few hundred million years). Enjoy them while we can.

title: Stellar parallax measures distance to stars.
verification: Stellar parallax: apparent shift of nearby stars relative to distant background as Earth orbits the sun. Half-angle of shift (in arcseconds) × 1 pc = 1/parallax_angle. 1 parsec = distance at which parallax = 1 arcsecond = 3.26 light years. Proxima Centauri (closest star): parallax 0.7687 arcsec → 1.3 pc → 4.24 light years. First measured by Bessel (1838) for 61 Cygni. Gaia spacecraft (2013+) measures parallax for 1+ billion stars with microarcsecond precision. Foundation of the cosmic distance ladder. The proverb is astronomy's most direct distance measurement.
wisdom: Stars closer than ~100 light years can be measured by parallax directly. Beyond that, the angles get too small and astronomy stacks indirect methods. The proverb is the bottom rung of the distance ladder.

title: The universe is expanding.
verification: Hubble's law (1929): v = H₀·d. Galaxies' recession velocities are proportional to their distances. Current best Hubble constant: ~70 km/s/Mpc (some tension between local-measurement ~73 and CMB-derived ~67 — the 'Hubble tension'). Implications: universe was hotter and denser in past (Big Bang model); current age ~13.8 billion years. Confirmed independently by: redshift-distance Type Ia supernovae, cosmic microwave background, primordial nucleosynthesis ratios. Accelerating expansion (1998) attributed to dark energy. The proverb is cosmology's foundational fact.
wisdom: Everything is moving away from everything, faster for things farther away. The universe doesn't have a center; the expansion is uniform. The proverb is the 20th century's most consequential astronomical discovery.

title: Carbon-14 dating works to about 50,000 years.
verification: ¹⁴C is continuously produced in upper atmosphere by cosmic rays + nitrogen. Living organisms exchange C with atmosphere; ratio of ¹⁴C/¹²C stays roughly constant during life. After death, ¹⁴C decays with half-life 5,730 years. Measuring residual ¹⁴C/¹²C ratio gives age. Useful range: 200-50,000 years (after ~10 half-lives, signal is too weak). Calibration via dendrochronology (tree rings) corrects for variable atmospheric ¹⁴C production. Older samples: K-Ar dating, U-Pb dating reach to billions of years. The proverb is archaeology's most-used dating method.
wisdom: For 50,000 years of past, organic material carries its own clock. Beyond that, you need different isotopes with longer half-lives. The proverb is archaeology's deepest reachable time.

title: Every calendar is a compromise between sun and moon.
verification: Solar year: ~365.2422 days. Lunar month: ~29.5306 days. 12 lunar months: ~354 days (11 days short). Pure lunar (Islamic, ~622 CE): months align with moon; year drifts vs seasons (Ramadan moves through year). Pure solar (Egyptian, Julian, Gregorian): months align with seasons; months ignore moon. Lunisolar (Jewish, Chinese, Hindu): months follow moon, periodically insert intercalary months (~7 per 19 years) to keep year in season. Modern Gregorian (1582): 365.2425-day average, achieved via 400-year leap-year rule. Accurate to ~1 day per 3,236 years. The proverb names the universal compromise no calendar fully solves.
wisdom: Sun and moon don't cooperate at the year/month boundary. Every calendar picks one to honor and compromises the other. The proverb is what's behind every leap-year rule and intercalary month.

title: The 60-year cycle pairs 12 animals with 5 elements.
verification: Chinese sexagenary cycle: 12 earthly branches (animals) × 10 heavenly stems (5 elements, each yin/yang) → LCM is 60 unique combinations. Cycle has been used for date-keeping in East Asia for ~3,500 years. Each year, day, month, and hour can be expressed in a stem-branch pair. The cycle is older than the Julian calendar by over a millennium and remains in use for traditional dates, age calculations, and astrology in China, Korea, Vietnam, Japan. The proverb compresses one of the world's oldest continuous calendrical systems.
wisdom: 60 years before the pair repeats. The math is LCM(10, 12) = 60. Three and a half millennia of continuous use makes it one of the longest-running calendrical systems in human history.

title: Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion.
verification: US Constitution First Amendment (ratified 1791): 'Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion, or prohibiting the free exercise thereof; or abridging the freedom of speech, or of the press; or the right of the people peaceably to assemble, and to petition the Government for a redress of grievances.' Two religion clauses (Establishment and Free Exercise) + four civil liberties (speech, press, assembly, petition). Foundation of US religious-freedom and free-speech jurisprudence. Tested in: Reynolds v. US (1879, polygamy not protected), West Virginia v. Barnette (1943, flag salute), Sherbert v. Verner (1963), Employment Division v. Smith (1990). The proverb is one of governance's most-litigated clauses.
wisdom: Six clauses in one sentence, each generating its own jurisprudence. The proverb is American constitutional law's most-tested protection. Hard cases continue.

title: Luther nailed 95 theses to the church door.
verification: October 31, 1517: Martin Luther sent his Ninety-Five Theses (mostly about indulgences) to Archbishop Albert of Mainz; the famous nailing to the Wittenberg church door is reported by Philip Melanchthon (later) but historically uncertain — door served as university bulletin board, but the dramatic act may be embellishment. Theses spread rapidly via printing press (within months across Europe). Sparked Reformation: Diet of Worms (1521), Augsburg Confession (1530), Council of Trent (1545-63), 30 Years' War (1618-48). Reshaped Western Christianity into Protestant + Catholic. The nailing is iconic; the content (95 specific theological objections) is the actual revolution.
wisdom: The nailing may be legend; the theses are not. 95 specific objections, printed and distributed, restructured Western Christianity within 50 years. The proverb is what Protestants remember; what mattered was the printing press.

title: The Nicene Creed defined orthodox Christianity.
verification: First Council of Nicaea (325 CE): convened by Constantine to resolve Arian controversy (whether Son is fully divine or a created being). Resulted in original Nicene Creed affirming Son's full divinity ('begotten, not made; of one substance with the Father' — homoousios). Council of Constantinople (381) expanded the creed to its modern form, including the Holy Spirit's procession. Recited weekly in Catholic, Orthodox, Anglican, and many Protestant traditions. Filioque clause ('and the Son') was added in Latin West, eventually contributing to the 1054 East-West Schism. The Creed is Christianity's foundational doctrinal summary, agreed by all major branches except for the filioque.
wisdom: 325 years after Christ, the church wrote down what it believed. The Creed has held for 1,700 years across schisms. The proverb is Christianity's doctrinal floor.

title: Bowling Alone — social capital is declining.
verification: Robert Putnam, 'Bowling Alone' (2000): documented decline in US social capital (memberships, trust, civic engagement) from 1960s to 1990s. Bowling participation up but league participation down — emblematic. Mechanisms: TV (10 hours/day for many households), suburbanization (lower density, less spontaneous interaction), longer commutes, two-earner households, generational replacement (civic Greatest Generation replaced by less-engaged successors). Subsequent data (2000-2020s) shows social isolation worsening further with smartphones/social media. Public health: low social capital correlates with higher mortality, worse mental health (see loneliness_kills). Real measurable phenomenon.
wisdom: Trust between strangers has declined; community membership has declined; institutional trust has declined. The proverb is Putnam's 25-year-old observation that has only gotten more true. The cost is measurable in health and democracy.

title: Greater efficiency can increase total use.
verification: Jevons paradox (William Stanley Jevons, 1865): increases in efficiency of resource use can paradoxically lead to greater total consumption, because efficiency makes resource cheaper, which expands demand. Original observation: more efficient coal-burning engines led to more coal use, not less. Modern examples: more efficient cars → more total miles driven (rebound effect ~10-30%); more efficient lighting (LEDs) → more total lighting installed; more efficient cooling → more buildings cooled. Policy implication: pure efficiency mandates are insufficient for total consumption reduction; combine with pricing (carbon tax) or absolute limits. The proverb is one of energy economics' most counterintuitive results.
wisdom: Efficiency is necessary but not sufficient. The proverb is why technical fixes alone don't solve consumption problems; the price signal has to come along.

title: Two witnesses are better than one — three are better than two.
verification: Deuteronomy 19:15, Matthew 18:16, 2 Corinthians 13:1 all establish two-or-three-witness rule. Statistical foundation: if each witness has independent error probability p, the probability that all N witnesses make the same mistake is p^N (decreases exponentially with N). For p=0.2 (witness accuracy 80%): 1 witness gives 80% confidence; 2 → 96%; 3 → 99.2%. Forensics, journalism, scientific replication, legal evidence all run on this. Real witnesses are not fully independent (shared environment, suggestibility, confirmation bias) — so the math overstates the gain. But the principle holds: more independent witnesses, higher confidence.
wisdom: Scripture set the floor at two; the math says higher is better. Independent corroboration shrinks error exponentially. The proverb is one of the oldest pieces of statistical wisdom in the human record.

title: Eyewitness testimony is less reliable than people think.
verification: Elizabeth Loftus's research (1970s+): memory is reconstructive, not photographic. Post-event information contaminates memory (misinformation effect). Confidence does NOT track accuracy — confident witnesses are often wrong. Innocence Project data: ~70% of DNA-overturned convictions involved mistaken eyewitness identification as primary evidence. Cross-race identification is especially weak (own-race bias). Police identification procedures (lineups, photo arrays) can suggest answers if not carefully blinded. Modern best practice: sequential blind lineups, no confidence prompts before ID, double-blind administration. The proverb is one of forensic psychology's most established findings.
wisdom: What the witness saw is less reliable than what the witness remembers; what the witness remembers is less reliable than what the witness says with confidence. The proverb is uncomfortable; the data is overwhelming.

title: Ford's assembly line: same task, repeated, fast.
verification: Henry Ford's Highland Park plant (1913): moving assembly line reduced Model T assembly time from 12.5 hours to 1.5 hours. Each worker specialized in one task; the car moved past them. Productivity gain ~10×; price dropped from $850 to $260 (1923 dollars), making cars affordable to working-class buyers. Negative side: monotonous work, high turnover (370% in 1913, fixed by Ford's $5/day wage in 1914). The principle (division of labor + standardized parts) is older than Ford (Eli Whitney 1798 muskets, James Watt steam engines) but Ford industrialized it for mass consumer goods. Foundation of 20th-century manufacturing.
wisdom: Specialization, standardization, sequencing. The three legs of modern manufacturing. The proverb is what made the middle class possible in the US.

title: Kaizen — continuous improvement, small daily.
verification: Kaizen (改善, 'change for better'): Toyota Production System principle. Every worker empowered to suggest small improvements; many tiny gains compound over time. Andon cord: any worker can stop the line if they spot a problem (used 1,000+ times daily at Toyota). 'Five whys' for root-cause analysis. Standardized work as a baseline for improvement (cannot improve what isn't standardized). Result: Toyota became world's largest automaker by ~2008, with quality scores consistently top-tier. Adopted (with varying fidelity) by every modern manufacturer. The proverb captures one of the most successful management philosophies of the 20th century.
wisdom: Hundreds of 1% improvements over years beat one 10% breakthrough every few years. Kaizen is the math of compounding interest applied to operations. The proverb is Toyota in one word.

title: Lean manufacturing eliminates seven wastes.
verification: Lean (Taiichi Ohno, TPS): seven categories of waste (muda). Transport (moving stuff), Inventory (stuff waiting), Motion (moving people), Waiting, Over-production, Over-processing, Defects. Mnemonic: TIMWOOD. Eighth waste sometimes added: underutilized talent. Each waste consumes resources without adding customer value. Lean tools: value stream mapping, 5S workplace organization, single-minute exchange of die (SMED), poka-yoke (mistake-proofing). Toyota cut inventory 75%, defects 90%, lead time 80% over decades. Lean has been applied beyond manufacturing — healthcare, software development (Lean Startup), construction. The proverb is the operational result of 60+ years of refinement.
wisdom: Seven kinds of waste. Hunt them all; never eliminate them entirely; the hunt itself is the system. The proverb is the operations research of doing less to deliver more.

title: Green buildings cut energy use by half.
verification: LEED-certified buildings (US Green Building Council, 2000+) use 25-50% less energy and 11% less water than comparable conventional construction. Techniques: better insulation (R-25 wall vs R-13 standard), passive solar orientation, high-efficiency HVAC, heat recovery ventilation, LED lighting, green roofs. Costs ~2-5% more upfront, paid back in 5-7 years through energy savings. Embodied energy (manufacturing + transport of materials) is now the next frontier: concrete contributes 8% of global CO₂. Mass timber and reclaimed steel are reducing embodied carbon. The proverb is the operational result of architectural engineering's last 25 years.
wisdom: The right windows, walls, and orientation cut bills in half. Most buildings still don't bother. The proverb is what we know how to do; the obstacle is amortization.

title: Form follows function.
verification: Louis Sullivan (1896): 'form ever follows function.' Modernist architectural credo. Frank Lloyd Wright extended: 'form and function are one.' Empirically: buildings designed strictly for function (warehouses, factories) often achieve a brutal beauty; buildings designed strictly for form (some monuments) often fail functionally. The most enduring buildings serve both. Counterargument (Robert Venturi 1966, 'Less is a bore'): postmodernism deliberately re-introduced ornament and metaphor. The proverb survives as a useful heuristic; it's not an absolute law. Frank Gehry's Bilbao Guggenheim contradicts the maxim and still works.
wisdom: The 20th century's most-quoted design principle and its most-disputed one. Form should serve function; pure function without form is sterile; pure form without function is decoration. Both halves matter.

title: Sound absorption is measured in sabins.
verification: Sabin: unit of acoustic absorption (named for Wallace Sabine). One sabin = absorption of 1 m² of perfectly absorbent surface. Concrete: ~0.02 sabins/m² (highly reflective). Heavy carpet: 0.4-0.6. Acoustic ceiling tile: 0.7-0.9. Open window: 1.0 (sound never returns). Performance spaces designed to target RT60 = 0.161·V/A (see reverberation_time). Office buildings increasingly use sound absorption to combat noise distraction — open-plan offices need absorption equivalent to ~50% of floor area for acceptable acoustics. The proverb is acoustic engineering's basic unit.
wisdom: Hard surfaces reflect; soft surfaces absorb. The proverb is one of architecture's quietest design parameters — invisible until it's wrong.

title: Air speeds up where the channel narrows.
verification: Continuity equation: A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ for incompressible flow. Narrower cross-section → higher velocity. Bernoulli: higher velocity → lower pressure. The Venturi effect (Giovanni Battista Venturi, 1797). Practical: carburetors (atomize fuel via low pressure at narrow throat), airplane pitot tubes (measure airspeed via pressure differential), water aspirators in chemistry labs, wind speed in mountain passes (compressed by terrain). Architectural: wind tunnels between tall buildings can produce dangerous pedestrian-level winds. The proverb is one of fluid mechanics' most everyday principles.
wisdom: Mass continuity is the law; narrow channel means faster flow. The Bernoulli low-pressure consequence drives carburetors, paint sprayers, and the discomfort of walking in a Manhattan wind tunnel.

title: A wing stalls when angle of attack exceeds the critical angle.
verification: Critical angle of attack (typically 15-20° for most airfoils): beyond this, airflow over the top separates from the wing surface, causing dramatic lift loss and increased drag. 'Stall' is misnamed — the engine isn't stalling, the wing is. Pilots train to recognize stall warning signs (buffet, sluggish controls, stall horn) and recover by reducing angle of attack (push nose down, add power). Stall-spin accidents are a leading cause of light-aircraft fatalities; recovery training is mandatory. Stall behavior is independent of airspeed (other than indirectly — slow flight forces high angle of attack). The proverb is one of aviation's most consequential physics.
wisdom: The wing stops flying when air no longer sticks to the top. Recoverable if you push the nose down and add power; deadly if mistaken for engine failure. The proverb is what every pilot drills.

title: The brain runs on 20% of body's glucose.
verification: Adult brain consumes ~120 g glucose/day (~480 kcal). Total brain mass: ~1.4 kg = ~2% of body weight. Yet brain uses ~20% of resting metabolic energy. Per-gram metabolic rate ~10× rest-of-body average. Glucose-dependent: brain cannot use fatty acids directly (blood-brain barrier excludes them); during prolonged fasting/ketosis, switches to ketones (β-hydroxybutyrate). Hypoglycemia (low blood glucose) impairs cognition within minutes; severe drops cause loss of consciousness. The proverb is why missing breakfast affects thinking and why ketogenic diets affect cognition (positively or negatively varies by individual).
wisdom: Two percent of body weight, 20% of energy. The brain is the most energy-dense organ. The proverb is why feeding yourself well measurably feeds your thinking.

title: The prefrontal cortex matures last — into mid-20s.
verification: Brain maturation proceeds back-to-front: sensory and motor areas first (infancy), parietal/temporal lobes (childhood), prefrontal cortex last (early-to-mid 20s). Myelination, synaptic pruning, dopamine system refinement all continue into the 20s. Prefrontal cortex governs: impulse control, long-term planning, abstract reasoning, social judgment. Implications: teenage risk-taking is partly neurological, not just behavioral. Legal frameworks for juvenile justice, contract competency, military service age, drinking age — all interact with this developmental timeline. Roper v. Simmons (2005) cited neuroscience in barring juvenile death penalty. The proverb is one of developmental neuroscience's clearest findings.
wisdom: Twenty-five is approximately when the planner-in-chief comes online. The proverb is why insurance premiums drop at 25, and why teenage decisions are sometimes baffling in retrospect.

title: 30 mA is the threshold of cardiac fibrillation.
verification: AC current effects on humans: 1 mA — perceptible tingle; 10 mA — muscle contraction, can't let go; 30 mA — diaphragm spasm, respiratory failure if sustained; 100 mA — ventricular fibrillation likely (cardiac arrest). GFCI trips at 5 mA differential in <0.025s — safely below fibrillation. DC current effects: roughly 5× higher thresholds (DC less arrhythmogenic). Voltage doesn't kill directly — current does, modulated by body resistance (1-100 kΩ depending on skin moisture, contact area). 120V through wet skin: ~50 mA = potentially lethal. 1000V through dry callus: same current or less. The proverb names the cardiac threshold that drives electrical safety codes.
wisdom: Current kills, not voltage. 30 milliamps is the threshold to take seriously. GFCIs and proper grounding exist precisely because of this number.

title: Matrix multiplication is row times column.
verification: For matrices A (m×n) and B (n×p), product C = AB is (m×p) with C[i][j] = Σ A[i][k]·B[k][j] over k. Non-commutative (AB ≠ BA in general). Foundation of: rotation/scaling in graphics, neural networks (every dense layer is matmul + activation), linear systems Ax = b, change of basis, projective transformations. Computational complexity: naive O(n³); Strassen (1969) O(n^2.807); current best O(n^2.373). Hardware-accelerated on GPUs and TPUs — modern AI is mostly matrix multiplication at extreme scale. The proverb is the operation that powers most of modern computing.
wisdom: Three nested loops. Trillions of them per second on a modern GPU. The proverb is the math operation that runs ChatGPT, your gaming rig, and most scientific computing.

title: Least squares: minimize the sum of squared residuals.
verification: Method of least squares (Legendre 1805, Gauss 1809): given data points and a model, find parameters minimizing Σ(y_observed − y_predicted)². Linear least squares has a closed-form solution: β = (X^T X)^(-1) X^T y. Why squared (not absolute) residuals: differentiable, gives unique solution, has optimal statistical properties under Gaussian noise (best linear unbiased estimator, Gauss-Markov theorem). Used in: regression analysis, signal processing, GPS positioning, surveying, image registration. Robust alternatives (L1 norm, Huber loss) preferred when outliers present. The proverb is statistics' most-used optimization criterion.
wisdom: Square the errors to penalize big ones more. Minimize. The closed-form solution falls out of linear algebra. Used to find lines through scattered points since 1805; the math is unchanged.

title: Older rocks underneath, younger on top — law of superposition.
verification: Nicholas Steno (1669): in undisturbed sedimentary sequences, lower layers are older than higher layers. Foundation of stratigraphy. Combined with: principle of original horizontality (sediments deposit horizontally), principle of lateral continuity (layers extend until they thin out or hit a barrier), principle of cross-cutting relationships (younger intrusions cut older rocks). These let geologists construct relative chronologies before absolute (radiometric) dating existed. Grand Canyon strata span ~2 billion years from Vishnu Schist (1.8 Ga) at bottom to Kaibab Limestone (270 Ma) at top. The proverb is geology's most foundational principle.
wisdom: Layers stack by gravity; gravity is the timekeeper. Steno worked it out in the 17th century by looking at shark teeth in stone (early paleontology). The proverb is geological time made visible.

title: Index fossils date the rock that contains them.
verification: Index fossil: species that lived for a geologically short time but spread widely. Trilobites are classic index fossils for Paleozoic Era; ammonites for Mesozoic. Finding a known index fossil in rock strata gives a relative age window of ~1-10 million years. Foraminifera (microscopic) are used for oil exploration — different species pin different layers. Combined with radiometric dating and stratigraphy, fossil correlations build the geological time scale. Index fossils are how 19th-century geologists assembled global stratigraphic columns before radioactive dating existed. The proverb is paleontology's bridge to chronology.
wisdom: Some species lived briefly and traveled widely. Finding them in a rock layer tells you when. The proverb is how Lyell and Smith mapped the geological column before anyone knew about radioactive decay.

title: Hash with salt. Always.
verification: Password hashing best practice: hash with cryptographic hash (Argon2id, bcrypt, scrypt — NOT plain SHA-256, which is too fast) and per-password random salt. Salt defeats rainbow-table attacks (precomputed hash dictionaries). Modern hashes intentionally slow: Argon2 at default settings takes ~1 second to compute one hash, multiplying attacker cost by ~10⁹ vs raw SHA-256. Database breaches with unsalted hashes (LinkedIn 2012, Adobe 2013) had millions of passwords cracked within days. Salted + slow hashes from breaches typically only ~30% cracked even years later. The proverb is the difference between credentials surviving a breach and not.
wisdom: Plain hash is broken security; salted slow hash buys decades. The proverb is the only acceptable way to store passwords in 2024.

title: A witnessed signature is stronger than an unwitnessed one.
verification: Notarized or witnessed signatures: a neutral third party attests to identity + voluntary act. Required for: wills (most US states require 2 witnesses), real estate deeds, power of attorney, marriage certificates. Notary public also creates official record (notary log). Self-executed documents (without witnesses) can be challenged for fraud, duress, lack of capacity; witnessed signatures shift burden of proof. Some jurisdictions accept electronic signatures (E-SIGN Act 2000, ESIGN Act in US); others still require wet ink with witnesses for certain instruments. The proverb is law's expression of a Deuteronomy 19:15 principle in modern form.
wisdom: One signature is testimony; two are corroboration. The notary makes the witnessing official. The proverb is why important documents require more than just your name.

title: Plurality, majority, and supermajority — different thresholds.
verification: Voting thresholds: plurality (most votes, ≤50% possible); majority (>50%); supermajority (typically 2/3 or 3/5). US Constitution amendments require 2/3 of Congress + 3/4 of states (extreme supermajority). UN Security Council resolutions: 9 of 15 affirmative + no veto. UK Parliament: simple majority for ordinary legislation. Plurality systems (FPTP) can elect leaders with <40% support; runoff and ranked-choice systems require effective majority. Higher threshold = more stability + harder to act; lower threshold = more agile + risk of minority overreach. The proverb names governance's most-debated tradeoff.
wisdom: Hard decisions require higher thresholds. The proverb is governance's calibration. Most political fights are at the boundary between threshold choices.

title: Drawing districts can predetermine elections.
verification: Gerrymandering: drawing electoral district boundaries to favor one party. Named for Massachusetts Governor Elbridge Gerry (1812). Techniques: 'packing' (concentrate opposition voters into few districts), 'cracking' (split opposition across many districts). Quantifiable via efficiency gap, partisan symmetry, mean-median difference. US Supreme Court (Rucho v. Common Cause 2019) ruled partisan gerrymandering claims are non-justiciable at federal level. Independent redistricting commissions (CA, AZ, MI, CO) produce measurably less biased maps. North Carolina 2018: 50% vote share → 77% of seats (3:13 split), one of the most studied examples. The proverb is one of democracy's most-exploited mathematical weaknesses.
wisdom: Geography becomes politics through line-drawing. The proverb is why redistricting reform matters and why courts and commissions try to constrain the practice.

title: Water cycles: ocean evaporates, falls as rain, runs back to sea.
verification: Global water cycle (annual averages): evaporation ~505,000 km³/year (84% from ocean, 16% from land); precipitation matches. Ocean loses more to evaporation than gains from precipitation; the deficit returns via river runoff (~37,000 km³/year). Average residence time: ocean ~3,200 years, glaciers ~10,000 years, groundwater ~1,400 years, atmosphere ~8 days. The cycle has been running essentially unchanged since oceans formed ~4 billion years ago. Modern climate change is altering rates (more evaporation from warmer oceans) but not the underlying structure. The proverb is hydrology's biggest accounting.
wisdom: All Earth's water is on a loop. Every glass of water you've ever drunk passed through countless other organisms first. The proverb is the planet's central plumbing.

title: Five ocean zones by depth — epi, meso, bathy, abysso, hado.
verification: Vertical ocean zones: Epipelagic (0-200 m) — sunlit, photosynthesis possible, holds most life by biomass. Mesopelagic (200-1000 m) — twilight, no photosynthesis, diel migrators. Bathypelagic (1000-4000 m) — total darkness, sparse life. Abyssopelagic (4000-6000 m) — deep ocean floor. Hadopelagic (>6000 m) — only in deep trenches (Mariana ~11 km). Each zone has distinct fauna (anglerfish, giant squid, hydrothermal vent ecosystems). 80% of ocean by volume is below 1000 m, almost entirely cold (2-4°C). Most species unknown — deep-sea biology is still in age-of-exploration phase.
wisdom: The ocean's surface is the thin warm lit shell on a cold dark deep. Most of the ocean is unfamiliar to humans because we evolved in the shell. The proverb is the structure beneath the surface.

title: The continental shelf is the drowned edge of the continent.
verification: Continental shelf: shallow (typically <200 m) sea floor adjacent to continents, formed by continental crust extending under water. Width varies from <1 km (Pacific coast of Americas) to ~1500 km (Siberian shelf). At edge: continental slope dropping steeply to abyssal plain (~4000 m). During ice ages with lower sea levels, shelves were dry land (Bering land bridge, Doggerland in North Sea). Most commercial fisheries are over continental shelves (sunlit, nutrient-rich, biologically productive). UN Convention on Law of the Sea: coastal nations own resources in their 200-nautical-mile EEZ, often extending across the shelf. Geopolitically valuable real estate.
wisdom: The shelf is continent that the ocean covered. Half the world's fish, much of the world's oil, and most maritime disputes happen over these underwater extensions of countries.

title: The genetic code has built-in redundancy.
verification: DNA uses 4 bases (A, T, G, C). Triplet codons → 4³ = 64 possible codons → 20 amino acids + stop signal. Excess capacity gives redundancy: most amino acids are encoded by multiple codons (e.g., leucine has 6, arginine has 6, methionine has 1). Third codon position is often 'wobble' — multiple bases map to same amino acid. Adaptive: third-base mutations are often silent (don't change amino acid), reducing fitness cost of point mutations. The genetic code is also nearly universal across life (small variations in mitochondria and some protists), suggesting last universal common ancestor used essentially this code. Coding theorists note striking similarity to error-correcting codes.
wisdom: More codons than amino acids, on purpose. Most mutations are silent because of the wobble. The proverb is biology's foundational error-correcting code, present since the first cells.

title: CRISPR cuts DNA at specific sequences.
verification: CRISPR-Cas9: bacterial adaptive immune system (Mojica 1993, characterized 2007; Doudna & Charpentier engineered for editing 2012, Nobel 2020). Guide RNA directs Cas9 nuclease to specific 20-base DNA sequence; Cas9 cleaves both strands. Cell's repair machinery (NHEJ or HDR) repairs the break, allowing insertion, deletion, or substitution. Revolutionized molecular biology: ~$50/edit vs ~$10,000 for previous methods, in weeks vs months. Clinical: first CRISPR therapy approved 2023 (Casgevy for sickle cell). Ongoing ethical debates about germline editing (He Jiankui's 2018 unauthorized human embryo edits). The proverb names biology's most consequential 21st-century tool.
wisdom: A bacterial defense system became biology's scalpel. CRISPR collapsed costs of genetic engineering by 100×. The proverb is the technology shaping medicine and food's next 50 years.

title: Western music uses 12 tones per octave — but most scales use 7.
verification: Chromatic scale: 12 equal semitones per octave. Major scale: 7 notes (W-W-H-W-W-W-H pattern of whole-step / half-step). Minor scales (natural, harmonic, melodic) also 7 notes with different patterns. Modes (Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian) — different starting points on the same 7-note chromatic subset. Other systems: pentatonic (5 notes — common in folk music globally), chromatic (all 12 — atonal, dodecaphonic), microtonal (>12 — Middle Eastern, Indian classical). The 7-of-12 structure dominates Western music; 7! = 5040 possible permutations but only a handful (major, minor, modes) are commonly used. The proverb is music theory's mathematical bedrock.
wisdom: Twelve possibilities, seven used at a time, in patterns established over centuries. The proverb is why most Western melodies sound familiar — they share an alphabet of 7 letters in 12 positions.

title: Time signatures organize rhythm into bars.
verification: Time signature: top number = beats per measure, bottom number = note value of one beat. 4/4 (most common in Western pop, classical, rock — 'common time'). 3/4 (waltz). 6/8 (compound duple — folk, jazz). 5/4 (irregular — Take Five by Brubeck). 7/8 (Balkan folk, prog rock). Polyrhythms layer multiple meters simultaneously (West African drumming, modern jazz). Beat hierarchy: strong beats (1), weak beats (3 in 4/4), syncopation = emphasizing weak beats. Meter is one of music's two organizing dimensions (the other being pitch). The proverb is rhythm's mathematical notation.
wisdom: Music is two grids: pitch in space, beats in time. The proverb is the beat grid. Pop is mostly 4/4; everything interesting tends to break that.

title: Convolution is how signals combine.
verification: Convolution: (f * g)(t) = ∫ f(τ)g(t-τ)dτ. Captures the response of one signal acted on by another. Foundation of: signal processing (filtering audio, blurring images), neural networks (convolutional layers in CNNs), probability (sum of independent random variables has PDF equal to convolution of individual PDFs), reverb effects (convolve dry signal with room impulse response — exact reproduction of a cathedral's acoustics). FFT computes convolution in O(n log n) instead of O(n²). The proverb is the math operation that runs image recognition, audio engineering, and probability.
wisdom: One signal smeared through another. The proverb is what audio engineers do with reverb, what photo apps do with blur, what neural networks do for image classification. Same math, three industries.

title: 100 cents in a semitone — pitch's metric system.
verification: Cent: 1/100 of a semitone, 1/1200 of an octave. Two notes a cent apart have frequency ratio 2^(1/1200) ≈ 1.000578. Human pitch discrimination: ~5 cents for trained musicians, ~25 cents for average listener in isolation. Used to specify tuning systems precisely: equal temperament (12 semitones × 100 cents = 1200 cents/octave); Pythagorean comma is 23.46 cents; syntonic comma 21.51 cents. Tuners and software (Auto-Tune) work in cents. The proverb is the unit of measurement that makes tuning systems comparable across cultures and centuries.
wisdom: Pitch has a centimeter equivalent. The cent lets musicians and engineers compare tunings precisely. The proverb is one of music theory's quietest standardizations.

title: People connect with similar people — homophily.
verification: Homophily: tendency to associate with similar others (McPherson, Smith-Lovin, Cook 2001 — one of sociology's most-cited papers). Strongest along race, age, religion; moderate along education, occupation, gender; weaker along values when controlled for the others. Mechanisms: meeting opportunity (we encounter similar people in similar contexts), comfort (less cognitive load), confirmation seeking. Implications: social network filter bubbles, persistent racial segregation in friendships, assortative mating in marriages (educational and cultural). Counter-strategies: diversity hires, integrated education, deliberate exposure programs. Sociology's strongest empirical pattern.
wisdom: Like attracts like, statistically. The proverb is sociology's most-replicated finding — and the reason every diversity initiative is uphill against gravity.

title: Weak ties carry information; strong ties carry support.
