verification: Jones 2019 GWAS (UK Biobank, n=697K): chronotype heritability ~46%, with PER, CRY, and ARNTL gene variants explaining major fraction. Schedule mismatch ('social jet lag') correlates with metabolic and cardiovascular risk.
wisdom: Some people are larks; some are owls. It's largely genetic. Schedule to your chronotype where possible.

title: Diversification reduces idiosyncratic risk, not market risk
verification: Modern portfolio theory (Markowitz 1952). ~30-stock equal-weight portfolio captures ~95% of available diversification benefit. Beyond that, only market beta remains.
wisdom: 30 stocks captures most of diversification's benefit. After that, you're just hedging market risk — and you can't.

title: 1% annual fee = ~28% terminal value loss over 30 years
verification: Compound arithmetic: a 1.0% fee compounded over 30 years reduces terminal portfolio value by 1 - (1-0.01)^30 ≈ 26-30% (depending on return base). The fee compounds against you the same way returns compound for you.
wisdom: A 1% fee isn't 1% lost — it's nearly 30% of your retirement nest egg over a career.

title: Active drivers test brakes more often than they think; ABS doesn't help if you don't press hard
verification: Driving research: most drivers in emergencies brake too softly initially, then release. ABS allows steering input under maximum braking — but only if applied at maximum force without modulation.
wisdom: In a panic stop: stomp and steer. Don't ease off — ABS handles the modulation for you.

title: Seatbelts protect pregnant women and fetuses; positioning matters
verification: ACOG guidance: lap belt below the belly, across the hips; shoulder belt across chest, between breasts. Unbelted pregnant drivers have ~5x fetal mortality in crashes vs belted.
wisdom: Belt position matters in pregnancy: below the bump, never across it. Belted always beats unbelted.

title: Smoke kills before flame in house fires; close bedroom doors
verification: NFPA 'Close Before You Doze' campaign: closed bedroom door drops temperature from ~1000°F to ~100°F and CO from lethal to survivable for the duration most fires need to be contained or extinguished.
wisdom: Close bedroom doors before sleep. A closed door buys minutes you don't have otherwise.

title: Choking adult who can cough should keep coughing; intervene only when air stops
verification: Red Cross / AHA guidelines: if victim can speak/cough, the airway isn't fully blocked. Heimlich only when victim cannot speak or breathe. Premature intervention can dislodge a partial obstruction into full.
wisdom: Coughing = don't help. Silent = back blows + Heimlich. The wrong move at the wrong time hurts.

title: Bystander CPR: chest compressions only, 100-120/min — no rescue breaths needed
verification: AHA hands-only CPR guidelines (2008+): compression-only is non-inferior to compression+breath for adult bystander CPR. Hesitation about mouth-to-mouth is removed; outcomes improve via faster response.
wisdom: You don't need to breathe for them. Push hard, push fast, push until help arrives.

title: Concussion recovery is more 'gradual return' than 'rest fully'
verification: 2022 Berlin consensus on concussion: ≤48h relative rest, then gradual return to cognitive and physical activity. Prolonged complete rest worsens outcomes (Thomas 2015). Sub-threshold aerobic exercise reduces symptom duration.
wisdom: Rest 1-2 days, then return gradually. Total bed rest past 48h is iatrogenic.

title: Sunscreen SPF beyond 50 offers minimal incremental protection
verification: FDA monograph data: SPF 30 blocks ~97% UVB, SPF 50 blocks ~98%, SPF 100 blocks ~99%. Reapplication every 2 hours matters more than the headline number. Look for 'broad spectrum' for UVA coverage.
wisdom: SPF 30 + reapply > SPF 100 once. The label number is marketing past 50.

title: Melanoma screening: ABCDE — Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter >6mm, Evolving
verification: AAD/American Cancer Society guidance. Self-screen monthly using a mirror. 'Evolving' is the most sensitive feature — changing moles deserve dermatologist evaluation.
wisdom: A changing mole is the most important clue. The other letters help describe; the E catches.

title: HPV vaccination is most effective before exposure but still useful through age 45
verification: ACIP/CDC: routine vaccination ages 11-12; catch-up to 26; shared decision-making 27-45. Lehtinen 2017 follow-up showed near-100% efficacy against HPV-related cervical lesions in unexposed cohorts.
wisdom: Earlier is better; later is still beneficial. Don't write off after the original window.

title: Renovation costs run 15-30% over initial estimate; budget a contingency
verification: Construction industry data (NAHB, Houzz reports): average overrun 15-25% for residential renovations. Older homes (>1980) average 25-35%. Hidden conditions (mold, knob-tube, asbestos) are the typical cause.
wisdom: Set aside 25% above the bid. The bid is wrong; the question is by how much.

title: Pre-purchase home inspection misses ~30% of latent defects
verification: ASHI surveys + ASTM E2018-15: standard visual home inspection has known limits — no destructive testing, weather-day conditions, accessibility constraints. Foundation, sewer line, roof internals frequently missed.
wisdom: Buy the inspection; buy a sewer scope too. The cheapest catastrophes are the unseen ones.

title: Most zoning variance appeals depend on local board precedent, not state law
verification: Land-use law principle: variance/exception decisions are made by appointed local boards (ZBA, planning commission) operating under broad discretion. Appeals to state courts review process, not substance, in most jurisdictions.
wisdom: Show up to the hearing with neighbors who support you. Process matters more than the legal merit.

title: State-specific will templates fail more often than they save in time
verification: Probate practice: holographic wills, online templates, and DIY estate planning have higher invalidation rates than attorney-drafted. Two-witness requirements vary by state; community property states have different default rules.
wisdom: DIY will + state notary + 2 witnesses often works. For real estate, blended family, or business: hire counsel.

title: Charity overhead ratio is a misleading metric for effectiveness
verification: Open Philanthropy / GiveWell consensus: overhead ratio is gameable and uncorrelated with actual impact. Best charities often invest in operations to scale impact. Cost-per-outcome (e.g., $/QALY) is the right metric where measurable.
wisdom: Don't pick a charity by overhead. Pick by what they accomplish per dollar.

title: IRS audit rate is <0.5% for most filers; under-reporting income is the biggest trigger
verification: IRS Data Book annual: audit rate ~0.3-0.5% overall. Schedule C filers, EITC claims, and >$200k income face higher rates. Algorithmic flags catch under-reported 1099 income at 90%+ rate via document matching.
wisdom: Don't omit 1099 income. The audit isn't random — it's matched against payer reports.

title: Most security deposit disputes are decided on the move-in walkthrough, not move-out
verification: Landlord-tenant law: tenant has burden of proving pre-existing condition. Photographs + signed walkthrough at lease start are the dispositive evidence in deposit small-claims cases.
wisdom: Photograph everything before unpacking. The walk-in is the deposit's actual decision point.

title: First offer in negotiation anchors the result; counter on substance not number
verification: Galinsky 2001 (Northwestern) and follow-ups: anchoring effect is robust across negotiations. First number framed as informed shifts the zone of possible agreement. Counter-anchoring requires resetting on different dimensions.
wisdom: If they anchor low, don't just nudge up. Reset the conversation around criteria, not numbers.

title: Self-reported salary data sites have ~10-20% upward bias
verification: Comparing Glassdoor / Levels.fyi to BLS / employer-reported data: self-reporting skews higher because submitters are typically passively considering moves or have above-average context. Use as ceiling not center.
wisdom: Glassdoor / Levels are the upper bound, not the median. BLS OEWS data is the public anchor.

title: Internal promotions average ~10-20% raise; external moves often double that
verification: ADP, Bureau of Labor Statistics, ROBOROC analyses: same-employer raises typically 3-5% annually with 10-20% on promotion. Job switches average 15-30% premium, sometimes higher in tight markets.
wisdom: Loyalty has a tax. Move externally every 3-5 years to capture true market value.

title: Inbox check frequency correlates with anxiety, not productivity
verification: Kushlev & Dunn 2014: limiting email checks to 3x/day reduced stress and increased productivity vs unlimited. The interruption cost of context-switching exceeds the value of fresh-information latency for ~90% of inboxes.
wisdom: Three times a day is enough. The rest is checking-as-stress.

title: Async written updates outperform sync meetings on retention and decision quality
verification: Cal Newport's deep work + remote-first studies (GitLab, Doist). Async forces clearer thinking but is slower to consensus. Sync helps creative brainstorming; async helps reporting and complex decisions.
wisdom: Async by default; sync for creative work that needs interactive thinking.

title: Active voice + short sentences score higher in comprehension and recall
verification: Hayes-Roth 1995 + subsequent: reading-comprehension scores rise sharply when sentence length drops below ~20 words and passive constructions are converted. Plain language laws (US Plain Writing Act 2010) codify the standard.
wisdom: Cut the sentence. Make the verb do the work.

title: New role first 30 days: listen more than you act; build the map first
verification: Watkins 'First 90 Days' framework + leadership practice: early changes made without organizational map have higher reversal rate. Listening builds the model needed to decide where to push.
wisdom: Map first, move second. Premature change is usually a junior signal.

title: Weekly 1:1s with direct reports correlate with retention more than salary
verification: Gallup employee engagement: regular meaningful 1:1 conversation is the strongest manager-level predictor of engagement and retention. Effect persists across pay grades; missing 1:1s is the modal complaint in exit interviews.
wisdom: Don't cancel the 1:1. It's the relationship maintenance the team is paying you for.

title: Gottman: 5:1 ratio of positive-to-negative interactions predicts marriage stability
verification: Gottman's lab studies (1990s, multi-decade follow-up): couples with at least 5 positive interactions for every 1 negative interaction during conflict have ~94% marriage stability vs ~30% below threshold.
wisdom: Five positives per negative isn't a slogan — it's the threshold for stability.

title: Adult attachment styles are real but malleable with sustained secure-base interaction
verification: Bowlby/Ainsworth → adult attachment research (Hazan & Shaver 1987). ~50% genetic / shared environment, ~50% sustained relationship experience. 'Earned secure' attachment is well-documented in long-term partnerships with secure others.
wisdom: Style isn't destiny. Sustained secure interaction reshapes it over years.

title: Grief 'stages' are descriptive averages, not a required sequence
verification: Kübler-Ross 1969 was based on dying patients, generalized incorrectly to bereavement. Bonanno's prospective studies show resilience is the modal trajectory (~50%), recovery ~30%, chronic ~15%. Stages oscillate; not all stages occur in everyone.
wisdom: Skip the stage chart. Most people are resilient. Watch for stuck rumination beyond 6 months.

title: Therapist alliance predicts outcome 4-8x more than modality (CBT/DBT/etc.)
verification: Wampold's meta-analyses (2001+): common factors (alliance, expectancy, ally character) account for ~60% of outcome variance; specific modality ~5%. Pick a therapist you trust; the school matters less than the relationship.
wisdom: The fit matters more than the framework. Switch therapists if alliance is poor; switch modalities last.

title: Phone in another room outperforms phone face-down on focus tasks
verification: Ward 2017 (UT Austin): cognitive capacity reduced in measurable steps as phone moves closer — present-but-silent < pocket < desk < same room. Out-of-room performs as if no phone exists.
wisdom: Distance > silence > face-down. The closer the device, the more attention it siphons.

title: News exposure dose-responds to anxiety; <1hr/day is the practical ceiling
verification: APA 'Stress in America' reports + Garfin 2020 (COVID): hours of media exposure correlate with anxiety and acute stress symptoms. Effect saturates after ~1 hour/day; further consumption produces stress without information gain.
wisdom: After about an hour, more news adds anxiety without information. Cap by time, not by feed-end.

title: Streaming consumption produces poor information recall vs scheduled viewing
verification: Netflix binge research (Horvath 2017): viewers who binge a season recall plot details worse than weekly viewers despite identical exposure time. Spacing effect applies to narrative as much as to study material.
wisdom: Binge for entertainment; pace for memory. Spacing helps recall in TV the same way it helps in study.

title: Decision journal entries written at decision time beat hindsight analysis
verification: Kahneman, Schwartz 2002+: hindsight bias systematically distorts after-the-fact decision reviews. Writing the reasoning, base rates, and expected outcome at decision time eliminates the bias from post-hoc review.
wisdom: Write your reasoning before the outcome. Once you know how it ended, you can't unsee it.

title: Pre-mortem (imagining failure in advance) improves project foresight
verification: Klein 2007: 'imagine you are 12 months from now and the project has failed; write down why' produces 30%+ more risk identification than standard SWOT or risk-register reviews.
wisdom: Imagine the failure first. The mind writes more honestly toward a known loss than an unknown success.

title: OKR cadence works at quarterly granularity; monthly is too frequent
verification: Doerr, 'Measure What Matters' + Google internal practice: quarterly OKR review balances signal vs noise. Monthly is sub-noise (random walk); annual loses corrective feedback. ~3-5 objectives per cycle is the working range.
wisdom: Quarterly. Three objectives. Each with 3-5 key results. More than that is noise.

title: Psychological safety is the strongest single predictor of team performance
verification: Google's Project Aristotle (2015): of 200+ team variables studied, psychological safety (members feeling safe to take interpersonal risks) was the single strongest predictor. Above seniority, salary, and process maturity.
wisdom: The team where people will say 'I don't know' outperforms the team where they won't. Build the safety first.

title: Rubber-duck debugging — explaining a problem aloud — solves a high fraction of bugs
verification: Folk-empirical software practice; mechanism is 'gradual exposition' forcing precise specification, which surfaces unstated assumptions. Same effect underlies 'tutor' Socratic dialogues.
wisdom: Explaining to the duck is half debugging. The articulation is the work.

title: 80/20 is a heuristic; real distributions vary from 70/30 to 95/5
verification: Pareto's 1896 distribution observation over Italian land ownership. Modern empirical distributions show the 'long tail' pattern is real but exponent varies. Cite 80/20 as approximate; don't treat as physical law.
wisdom: Most outputs come from a minority of inputs. The ratio varies; the asymmetry is real.

title: One main idea per paragraph; one main argument per piece
verification: Strunk & White principle; subsequent editorial research. Multi-idea paragraphs reduce comprehension by ~40% vs single-idea. Multiple-thesis essays score lower on persuasiveness measures.
wisdom: One paragraph, one idea. One essay, one argument. The rest is illustration.

title: Bar charts outperform pie charts for comparison; pie charts mislead beyond 4 slices
verification: Cleveland & McGill 1984: position-on-aligned-scale (bars) was most accurate; angle/area (pies) least. Pie with >4 slices is read like a random number generator.
wisdom: Bars over pies. Always.

title: Log-scale axes mislead non-specialists; label clearly or use linear
verification: COVID-era visual analysis (Romano 2020, others): log-scale charts of case counts produced systematic misinterpretation. Audience reading-skill varies; explicit labeling and dual presentation reduce error.
wisdom: Log scales are honest math but dishonest UX. Label heavily or pair with linear.

title: Correlation requires three things to imply causation: precedence, mechanism, no confound
verification: Bradford Hill criteria (1965): strength, consistency, specificity, temporality, biological gradient, plausibility, coherence, experiment, analogy. Modern causal inference (Pearl 2000+) formalizes via DAG and do-calculus.
wisdom: Correlation doesn't equal causation, but the three guardrails (before, why, alternatives ruled out) bridge the gap.

title: Simpson's paradox: aggregated trends can reverse when stratified
verification: UC Berkeley admissions case (Bickel 1975) is the canonical real-world example. The overall pattern showed sex bias; per-department stratification reversed the finding because applicants chose departments with different acceptance rates.
wisdom: Always check stratification. The trend in the whole may reverse in every part.

title: Publication bias makes positive findings overrepresented in literature
verification: Funnel plot analyses, registered replication projects (e.g., Many Labs). Significant-positive findings are 2-5x more likely to be published than null findings, inflating apparent effect sizes systematically.
wisdom: What you read is what got published. What got published is what was positive.

title: Social psychology has the worst replication record; chemistry and physics the best
verification: Open Science Collaboration 2015 (psychology, ~36% replicate); cancer biology (Reproducibility Project: 50% with reduced effect size); physics and chemistry rarely replicate-tested but published findings stable.
wisdom: Weigh by field. A new social-psych finding deserves more skepticism than a new spectroscopy result.

title: Evolution can be observed in real time at short generation cycles
verification: Grant & Grant Darwin's finches (40+ year direct observation), Lenski long-term E. coli experiment (75,000+ generations, novel citrate metabolism), antibiotic resistance evolution in clinical settings.
wisdom: Evolution isn't a theory waiting on proof. It's an observed process in current experimental record.

title: Same DNA, different expression: epigenetics regulates which genes activate
verification: Methylation, histone modification studies: monozygotic twins diverge in disease and trait expression because methylation patterns drift with environment and aging. ENCODE Project mapped >80% of genome as functionally regulatory.
wisdom: Genome is the blueprint; epigenome is the contractor. Both decide what gets built.

title: Gut microbiome composition responds to diet within days; long-term shape over months
verification: David 2014 (Nature): plant-based vs animal-based diet shift gut bacterial composition within 2-3 days. Long-term diet (cuisine type) shapes the stable equilibrium.
wisdom: You can shift gut bacteria in a week; you reshape the equilibrium over months. Fiber and fermented foods feed diversity.

title: Climate attribution science can now quantify specific events' fractional warming contribution
verification: World Weather Attribution consortium: rapid-response analyses (e.g., 2021 Pacific Northwest heat dome, 2022 Pakistan floods) quantify probability ratios — 'event was X times more likely due to anthropogenic warming.'
wisdom: You can't attribute one storm. You can attribute the changed probability of that class of storm.

title: Grid renewables work best with storage; storage costs have dropped 90% in a decade
verification: BNEF battery cost data: $1100/kWh in 2010 → $130/kWh in 2024 (lithium-ion pack price). LCOE solar+storage now competitive with coal/gas in most US markets without subsidy.
wisdom: Storage is the unlock. The technology is cheap enough that grid integration is now a regulatory problem more than a physical one.

title: EVs convert ~85-90% of grid energy to wheels; ICE cars ~25-30% of fuel energy
verification: DOE drive cycle data. Even if grid electricity is fossil-fueled at 40% efficiency, well-to-wheels: fossil grid + EV ≈ 32% vs ICE 28%. Cleaner grids extend the margin.
wisdom: EVs are cleaner than equivalent ICE on every existing grid mix. The thermodynamics work; the question is the buildout.

title: Nuclear power has the lowest deaths-per-TWh of any energy source
verification: Markandya & Wilkinson 2007 (Lancet), Our World In Data: nuclear ~0.07 deaths/TWh including major accidents. Coal ~24, gas ~3, hydro ~1. Public risk perception inverts this ordering.
wisdom: The numbers are clear; the politics aren't. Nuclear is statistically the safest.

title: Tectonic plates move at ~2-10 cm/year — about the rate of fingernail growth
verification: GPS geodesy + paleomagnetic records: Atlantic spreads ~2.5 cm/yr (fingernail growth); Nazca-Pacific Rise ~14 cm/yr (hair growth). Over millions of years, slow rates produce continental-scale change.
wisdom: Continents move at fingernail-growth speed. Slow and steady recomposes the world.

title: Earthquake protocol: drop, cover, hold — not doorway
verification: USGS / FEMA current guidance: 'drop to floor, take cover under sturdy desk, hold on'. The doorway myth dates to 1933 California adobe-house construction; modern doorways are no stronger than other framing and trap occupants from falling debris.
wisdom: Forget the doorway. Drop, cover, hold. Pre-2000 advice is unsafe.

title: Tornado safety: lowest level, interior room, away from windows
verification: NOAA NWS guidance unchanged: basements safest; if no basement, interior windowless room (bathroom, closet) on lowest floor. Mobile homes are not safe — leave for a sturdy shelter.
wisdom: Basement > interior room > windowless > anywhere else. Mobile homes are not shelters.

title: Lightning 30/30: if flash-to-thunder ≤30 sec, shelter; wait 30 min after last
verification: NOAA NWS standard. Sound travels ~5 sec per mile; 30 sec ≈ 6 miles, well within lightning strike range. Most fatalities occur to people who resumed activity within 10-20 min of last strike.
wisdom: When you can count to 30 between flash and thunder, you're in range. Wait 30 minutes after the last.

title: Oxygen pressure halves every 5.5 km of altitude
verification: Barometric formula. Sea level 1013 mbar; Denver (1.6 km) ~85%; Everest base camp (5.4 km) ~50%; summit (8.85 km) ~33%. Acute mountain sickness symptoms begin around 2500m for unacclimatized.
wisdom: Acclimatize gradually. Above 3000m, climb high, sleep low.

title: Drowning is silent; victims often don't shout or wave
verification: Pia 2006 'Instinctive Drowning Response' description: drowning victims cannot voluntarily control arms (lateral pressing), can't call out (breathing reflex prioritizes), and stay upright with head tilted back. Often visible only as 'someone treading water'.
wisdom: Drowning looks like nothing. If you have doubt about someone in water, ask them their name. If they can't answer, you have your answer.

title: Rip current escape: swim parallel to shore, not against the current
verification: NOAA rip current safety: currents are typically 10-30m wide. Swimming parallel to shore exits the current; swimming back against it exhausts the swimmer.
wisdom: Parallel, not against. Most rip current fatalities are exhaustion from fighting the current straight.

title: Chocolate toxic to dogs at ~20 mg theobromine per kg body weight
verification: Veterinary toxicology: theobromine LD50 ~100-200 mg/kg in dogs. Dark chocolate ~16 mg/g theobromine; milk chocolate ~2 mg/g. A 10kg dog can eat ~6oz milk chocolate before symptoms; <1oz of dark chocolate is concerning.
wisdom: Dark chocolate is the dangerous one. Call ASPCA poison hotline for any ingestion.

title: Grapes and raisins cause acute kidney failure in dogs at unpredictable doses
verification: Veterinary case literature: mechanism unknown (possibly tartaric acid). No safe dose established. Some dogs eat handfuls without effect; others fatally toxic at single grape. Treat all ingestion as veterinary emergency.
wisdom: No safe dose for grapes/raisins in dogs. Treat any amount as an emergency.

title: Measure twice cut once: tape-measure consistency outperforms ruler precision
verification: Carpentry practice: single measurement carries ~1mm random error; redundant measurement reduces by sqrt(N). Cutting errors are unrecoverable; measurement errors are reversible — invest in the reversible step.
wisdom: Two measurements, one cut. The grain doesn't grow back.

title: Internal temperature trumps time when cooking meat; thermometer ends all debate
verification: USDA Food Safety: chicken 165°F, beef/lamb medium-rare 130°F, pork 145°F, ground beef 160°F. Time-based recipes vary by stove, pan, cut — temperature is the actual variable.
wisdom: Buy a thermometer. The cookbook is approximating; the meat tells you when it's done.

title: Sharp knife is safer than dull knife in normal kitchen use
verification: ER injury data (CPSC): dull blades require more force, slip more, and cause deeper lacerations. Sharp blades cut cleanly with less force; injuries are smaller and shallower.
wisdom: A dull knife is the dangerous knife. Hone weekly, sharpen monthly.

title: Crushed garlic produces ~10× more allicin than sliced
verification: Block 1985+: allicin forms when alliinase enzyme contacts alliin substrate, requiring cellular disruption. Crushing maximizes the contact; slicing minimizes it. Resting 10 min before cooking preserves more allicin against heat denaturation.
wisdom: Crush, then rest 10 minutes before heat. The compound is in the crushing.

title: Extra-virgin olive oil is stable for cooking; smoke-point fear is overblown
verification: De Alzaa 2018 + subsequent: EVOO had lowest oxidation byproducts of common cooking oils when heated, despite lower smoke point. The polyphenols and oleic acid stabilize against thermal degradation better than refined seed oils.
wisdom: EVOO is fine for normal cooking. The smoke-point myth comes from oil-purveyor marketing.

title: Salting meat 40 min before cooking outperforms salting right before
verification: Kenji López-Alt experiments: salt draws moisture, then is reabsorbed via osmosis, dissolving cellular proteins and seasoning throughout. 40 min minimum; overnight optimal. Pre-salting within 5 min draws moisture without reabsorption — worse than no salt at all.
wisdom: Salt 40 minutes ahead or right at service. Never in the middle window.

title: Panko (Japanese breadcrumbs) absorb less oil than standard breadcrumbs
verification: Crumb structure: panko is made from yeasted bread without crusts, flake-cut rather than ground. Flake structure has lower bulk density and surface area-to-volume → less oil retention during frying. Texture stays crispier.
wisdom: Panko over plain breadcrumbs for frying. Less oil absorbed, crispier finish.

title: Egg freshness test: fresh sinks flat, week-old tilts up, old floats
verification: Air cell inside the egg grows as moisture and CO2 escape through the porous shell over time. The cell's buoyancy lifts the older eggs. USDA: floating means not necessarily spoiled but lower quality; sniff before use.
wisdom: Sink test reveals age; sniff test reveals safety. Both matter.

title: Freezer burn is desiccation, not contamination; safe but degraded
verification: USDA food safety: freezer burn occurs when frozen water sublimes from food surface in cold dry air. Causes texture and flavor loss but no pathogen growth. Trim affected surface; cook the rest.
wisdom: Freezer burn is texture, not safety. Trim and cook.

title: 'Best by' dates are quality, not safety; pantry shelf life often 1-2× longer
verification: USDA + ReFED: only infant formula has FDA-mandated date; everything else is manufacturer quality estimate. Canned goods stable 2-5 years past printed date if seal intact. Dry pasta 1-2 years past.
wisdom: Best-by is the manufacturer's quality guess. Trust your nose; check the seal.

title: Most plastic recycling is downcycling at best; bottle-to-bottle is rare
verification: OECD Global Plastics Outlook 2022: ~9% of plastic ever produced has been recycled; ~12% incinerated; ~79% in landfill or environment. PET bottle-to-bottle works; mixed plastics (#3-7) rarely closed-loop.
wisdom: Reduce > reuse >> recycle. Recycling is the last line, not the first.

title: Compost wants ~30:1 carbon-to-nitrogen ratio (browns to greens)
verification: Soil science / cooperative extension. Browns: dry leaves, straw, cardboard. Greens: vegetable scraps, grass clippings, coffee grounds. Off-ratio compost: too much nitrogen smells of ammonia; too much carbon doesn't heat or break down.
wisdom: Three parts browns to one part greens. If it stinks, add browns; if it's slow, add greens.

title: Mowing lawn at 3-4 inches reduces water and herbicide needs significantly
verification: University extension data (Penn State, NC State): taller grass shades soil, reduces evaporation 30-40%, crowds out weeds, deepens root systems. Lower cuts stress grass, exposing soil to weed germination and water loss.
wisdom: Mow high. The lawn is the soil's roof.

title: Garden frost dates have widened; check local 30-year averages
verification: USDA Plant Hardiness Zone Map updated 2023, shifting zones half-a-zone warmer on average. Average last spring frost has moved earlier ~1-2 weeks since 1990 in most US regions. Local micro-climate variance dominates.
wisdom: Use your county extension's current frost dates, not your grandfather's. The calendar has shifted.

title: Egg yolk color reflects diet, not nutrition or freshness
verification: Industrial poultry feeds use marigold petals or paprika to deepen yolk color; pastured hens eat more carotenoid-rich plants naturally. Pale yolks (commercial battery feed) are not less nutritious than deep-orange yolks (forage-fed) in protein/fat, but vitamin A and lutein differ.
wisdom: Yolk color marks the hen's diet, not the egg's age. Nutrition follows the feed; the carotenoids just travel.

title: Hard water plumbing damage exceeds water softener cost over 7 years
verification: Water Quality Association data: hard water (>120 mg/L CaCO3) scales appliances and pipes, requiring 30%+ more energy for water heating and shortening fixture life. Cost-benefit favors softening above ~10 grains/gal in 5-7 year horizons.
wisdom: If hardness is >10 gpg, soften. The pipes and water heater don't recover.

title: Premium paint costs 2× but covers in fewer coats; total cost lower
verification: Consumer Reports paint testing: premium tier (Behr Marquee, Sherwin-Williams Emerald) covers in 1 coat where economy tier needs 2-3. Labor cost dominates DIY+pro alike; coat count drives total project cost.
wisdom: Pay more per gallon. Fewer coats; less labor; better surface. The cheap paint is the expensive paint.

title: South-facing windows maximize winter solar gain in northern hemisphere
verification: Passive solar design (Passive House standard). Northern hemisphere: low winter sun (~25°) enters south windows deeply; high summer sun (~70°) limited by short overhangs. Net heating-cooling cost reduces 20-40% in well-designed cases.
wisdom: South-facing windows + correctly-sized overhang are free heating in winter and cool in summer.

title: Insulation has diminishing returns; doubling R-value only halves remaining heat loss
verification: Heat transfer: Q ∝ 1/R. Going from R-13 to R-26 cuts loss through that path by 50%; R-26 to R-52 only cuts the remaining 50% in half (now 25% of original). Diminishing returns are steep past code minimums.
wisdom: Insulating beyond code is rarely the high-ROI move. Air sealing usually wins.

title: Air sealing typically beats added insulation on $/BTU savings
verification: DOE building science studies: air infiltration accounts for 25-40% of heat loss in typical US homes. Caulk + weatherstripping at $200-500 returns more than $5000 of insulation in most retrofit scenarios.
wisdom: Find the leaks before adding insulation. Air-seal first; insulate second.

title: CO detectors needed near sleeping areas, not just at appliances
verification: NFPA 720 / IRC: CO alarms required on every habitable level + outside sleeping areas + within 10ft of bedrooms. Many CO deaths are during sleep when symptoms (headache, confusion) don't wake victims.
wisdom: Bedroom-area CO detector is the load-bearing one. Replace every 5-7 years.

title: Radon is leading cause of lung cancer in nonsmokers; test every basement
verification: EPA: radon causes ~21,000 lung cancer deaths/year in US. Concentrations >4 pCi/L require mitigation. Test kits ~$15; mitigation systems ~$1500. Geographic distribution: highest in northern US, Iowa, Appalachia.
wisdom: Test once. If above threshold, mitigate. The $1500 retrofit is among the highest-ROI health interventions in the home.

title: 15-year mortgage saves ~50% lifetime interest vs 30-year at typical rates
verification: Amortization math: on $400k at 7% for 30 years, total interest ≈ $558k. Same loan at 15 years ≈ $247k interest. Higher monthly but ~$311k saved over the term. Opportunity cost depends on alternative investment returns.
wisdom: 15-year if cash flow allows. The interest differential is staggering at modern rates.

title: FICO weights: 35% payment history, 30% utilization, 15% age, 10% mix, 10% inquiries
verification: Fair Isaac published methodology. Single late payment can drop 50-100 points; chronic high utilization (>30%) drops 30-50 points; closed old cards reduce age-of-credit factor over years.
wisdom: Pay on time and keep utilization low. Those two cover 65% of the score.

title: Low-cost index funds outperform ~90% of actively managed funds over 15 years
verification: SPIVA scorecards (S&P Dow Jones, annual): over 15-year periods, 87-92% of active large-cap US funds underperform their benchmark. Fees + survivorship + behavioral churn explain most of the gap.
wisdom: The default move is a 3-fund portfolio of index ETFs. Active managers rarely beat it.

title: Seebeck effect — heat-driven electricity without moving parts
situation: A temperature gradient across two dissimilar conductors (or doped semiconductors) generates a small DC voltage. Stack many such junctions in series and you have a thermoelectric generator (TEG): a solid-state heat-to-electricity converter with no moving parts, no fuel, just a hot side and a cold side.

pre_run.summary: Thomas Johann Seebeck, 1821. The Seebeck coefficient (S, units V/K) of a material determines the voltage per degree of temperature difference. Practical thermocouples (Type K, J, T) use this for temperature measurement; practical TEGs (bismuth telluride modules) use it for power generation.

domain_results.detail: Seebeck coefficient measured for every conductor and semiconductor; published in standard references.
TEG efficiency 5-8% at consumer scale, up to ~15% in lab. RTGs on Voyager / Curiosity / Perseverance use plutonium-238 + TEG modules for decade-scale power.
Subject to Carnot limit. The 5-8% practical efficiency reflects irreversibilities in finite-time conduction, not a violation of conservation.

wisdom: Heat already moving from somewhere hot to somewhere cold is free energy on its way to waste. A TEG bolted to a wood stove charges a phone while you cook. A TEG on a camping pot boils water and trickles 1-3W into a battery. RTGs powered Voyager 1 for nearly five decades on a kilogram of plutonium. For off-grid + indoor + nighttime power where solar fails, thermoelectric is one of the few options that works. Tradeoff: low efficiency vs absolute reliability and zero moving parts. The principle: if you already have a gradient, harvest it.

title: ATG codes for methionine (M) — the start codon
situation: The DNA codon ATG translates to the amino acid methionine (M) under the standard genetic code, NCBI translation table 1.

pre_run.summary: ATG is the canonical start codon in all known life. Translation table 1 maps ATG → M unambiguously.

domain_results.detail: codon ATG translates to M under translation table 1

axis_overlaps.note: DNA is a 4-letter alphabet encoding via 64 codons → 20 amino acids + stop. Same axis as Hebrew (22 letters) and cryptographic alphabets.

wisdom: DNA is a code with an alphabet, a dictionary, and a reading frame. The alphabet is 4 letters (A, C, G, T). The dictionary is 64 codons. The grammar produces 20 amino acids plus a stop. The start codon ATG is the equivalent of 'capital letter at the beginning of a sentence' — every protein begins here. The same encoding axis the engine uses to score Hebrew lexicon claims and cryptographic hashes runs through DNA. The Shepherd treats them as neighbours.

title: TTT and TTC both code for phenylalanine — synonymous codons (wobble base)
situation: Both TTT and TTC translate to phenylalanine (F) under the standard genetic code. The genetic code is redundant: 64 codons map to 20 amino acids + stop, so most amino acids have multiple synonymous codons.

pre_run.summary: Two codons that differ only at the third base (the wobble position) translate to the same amino acid. This is the standard genetic code's built-in error-correction.

domain_results.detail: TTT → F and TTC → F (synonymous codons, wobble base)
Redundant code: 64 codewords mapping to 21 outputs (20 amino acids + stop), surplus = 43 redundant codons.

axis_overlaps.note: The wobble base is biology's parity bit. Same principle as Hamming codes in cryptography and information theory — redundancy buys error tolerance.

wisdom: Redundancy is not waste. The genetic code is over-specified by design: a single-base mutation at the third position usually changes nothing, because the synonym still codes for the same amino acid. The cell pays a small cost in entropy and buys a large cost reduction in fatal mutations. Cryptographers know this trade. So do Hebrew scribes — the Masoretic letter counts at the end of each book are a parity check on the transmission.

title: Hebrew H1 (אָב, 'ab') glosses 'father'
situation: The Hebrew Strong's number H1 refers to the lemma אָב (transliterated 'ab'), glossed 'father'. The 22-letter Hebrew alphabet encodes Scripture in the same way DNA encodes proteins — a finite character set, a structured dictionary, a deterministic reading order.

pre_run.summary: H1 resolves in Strong's Hebrew lexicon. Transliteration 'ab' and gloss 'father' both match canonical entries.

domain_results.detail: H1 → אָב, transliteration 'ab', gloss 'father' all match the Strong's Hebrew lexicon

axis_overlaps.note: Hebrew uses 22 consonant letters (aleph through tav); vowels are added by Masoretic pointing. A finite alphabet with structured combinatorics — same axis as DNA bases and cryptographic alphabets.

wisdom: Hebrew is a 22-letter encoding system. Aleph (א) is letter 1; tav (ת) is letter 22. Vowels are diacritical marks added by the Masoretes in the 8th–10th centuries; the consonantal text predates them by a millennium. The same axis that lets the engine verify a DNA codon also lets it verify a Hebrew lemma: finite alphabet, deterministic lookup, source-traceable. The Shepherd treats H1 ('ab') and ATG ('start') as neighbours on the encoding axis — both are 'the first letter of the first word'.

title: Genesis 1:1 gematria sum 2701 factors as 37 × 73
verification: The arithmetic is verifiable: 37 × 73 = 2701 (CONFIRMED by number_theory). The claim that Genesis 1:1 in Hebrew (בְּרֵאשִׁית בָּרָא אֱלֹהִים אֵת הַשָּׁמַיִם וְאֵת הָאָרֶץ) sums to 2701 under standard gematria depends on the letter-value assignments (א=1, ב=2, … ת=400) being applied consistently. Under those values, the sum does equal 2701. The further claim that this factorisation carries theological weight (the 'menorah' or 'triangle' patterns sometimes drawn from it) is not verifiable — it lives in interpretation, not arithmetic.
wisdom: Gematria has a verifiable layer and an interpretive layer. The engine confirms the arithmetic; it cannot confirm the theology. Treat the math as math, the meaning as exegesis. The Shepherd brings you both, labelled honestly: number_theory CONFIRMED on 37 × 73 = 2701; theology MIXED on whether the factorisation is a sign or a coincidence. The DNA-encoding analogy lands here too — codon counts are real; whether the 64-to-20 mapping carries cosmological weight is a separate claim.

title: DNA stores 2 bits per base; the human genome is ~763 MB packed
verification: log₂(4) = 2 bits per base, since DNA has 4 possible symbols (A, C, G, T). The haploid human genome is ~3.2 × 10⁹ bases. Packed at 2 bits per base: 3.2 × 10⁹ × 2 / 8 / 1024² ≈ 762.9 MB. CONFIRMED by direct calculation. This is the raw-encoding figure — actual storage with metadata, annotations, and quality scores runs orders of magnitude larger.
wisdom: A complete blueprint for a human being fits on a USB stick. 763 MB — smaller than most operating systems, smaller than a single Blu-ray film. The encoding is dense because the alphabet is small (4) and the redundancy is local (in the codon table, in repair enzymes), not in the raw sequence. The Shepherd carries this fact as evidence on the encoding axis: a finite alphabet over a deterministic dictionary can carry vast complexity. The same principle lets the Bible — a finite collection of letters in 22-letter Hebrew and 24-letter Greek — encode what it encodes.

title: RSA-2048 is currently strong per NIST guidance
situation: RSA with a 2048-bit modulus is classified as strong under current NIST guidance (minimum 2048 bits for RSA as of SP 800-57). Asymmetric cryptography rests on one-way functions: easy to multiply two large primes, intractable to factor the product.

pre_run.summary: cryptography confirmed RSA-2048 is classified strong (NIST SP 800-57) while RSA-512 is weak, on prod. HOLDS 2/2, sealed. This is key-SIZE classification; the underlying factoring hardness remains conjecture.

domain_results.detail: RSA-2048 strong; RSA-512 weak (contrast)

wisdom: Public-key cryptography is built on a wager: that multiplying two huge primes is fast and reversing it is slow. As long as that asymmetry holds, RSA-2048 works. The day a sufficiently large quantum computer runs Shor's algorithm, the wager breaks. NIST has already designated post-quantum standards (CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium) for exactly this reason. Strength is time-stamped, not eternal — the same lesson the engine carries in its OBSOLETE verdict category. What is strong today may be broken tomorrow; the keeping records when.

title: SHA-256('hello world') = b94d27b9...cde9 — deterministic, one-way
situation: The SHA-256 digest of the UTF-8 bytes 'hello world' is b94d27b9934d3e08a52e52d7da7dabfac484efe37a5380ee9088f7ace2efcde9. The same input always produces the same digest (deterministic); the digest cannot be inverted to recover the input (one-way).

pre_run.summary: cryptography confirmed SHA-256('hello world') = b94d27b9...efcde9 on prod (exact digest of the UTF-8 bytes). HOLDS 1/1, sealed. One-wayness/collision-resistance remain conjecture, not part of this proof.

domain_results.detail: sha256('hello world') = b94d27b9934d3e08a52e52d7da7dabfac484efe37a5380ee9088f7ace2efcde9

wisdom: A cryptographic hash is the modern equivalent of the Masoretic letter count: a tiny deterministic summary that catches any change in a long text. The same input always hashes the same; the smallest edit produces a completely different hash. SHA-256 outputs 256 bits regardless of input length — kilobyte, gigabyte, the whole Bible. Two scribes in two centuries can run the hash on the same Genesis 1:1 and check whether the text was kept. That is what the engine is doing every time it seals a packet: hashing the content so the future can verify it wasn't changed.

title: Codon ATG ≠ codon GTA — sequence order is the meaning
situation: The DNA codon ATG translates to methionine (M); the codon GTA translates to valine (V). The same three letters in a different order produce a different amino acid. Sequence order is the meaning — the same principle that distinguishes Hebrew אב ('father') from בא ('he came') by letter order.

pre_run.summary: Two codons drawn from the same multiset of bases translate to different amino acids — order, not composition, determines meaning.

domain_results.detail: codon ATG → M, codon GTA → V (different amino acids despite identical composition {A,T,G})

axis_overlaps.note: Order-as-meaning is the deepest principle on the encoding axis. Hebrew אב vs בא, English 'dog' vs 'god', DNA ATG vs GTA — same characters, different sequences, different meanings.

wisdom: Composition is not meaning. The same atoms, the same letters, the same nucleotides — arranged differently — yield different worlds. This is why position-of-the-letter matters in Hebrew gematria; why an anagram is not a synonym; why a single SNP (single-nucleotide polymorphism) at the right position can change everything. The Shepherd surfaces this rule whenever the user is tempted to read the parts and skip the order.

title: EcoRI cuts at GAATTC — a DNA palindrome (reverse-complement equals self)
situation: The restriction enzyme EcoRI recognises and cuts the DNA sequence GAATTC. GAATTC is a palindrome in the genetic sense: its reverse complement is GAATTC itself. Palindromic recognition sites are how the cell distinguishes 'cut here' from 'leave alone' — symmetry as signal.

pre_run.summary: Compute the reverse-complement of GAATTC. Complement = CTTAAG; reversed = GAATTC. Reverse-complement equals the sequence — palindrome confirmed.

domain_results.detail: GAATTC → complement CTTAAG → reverse-complement GAATTC (palindrome)

axis_overlaps.note: Palindromes appear in DNA (restriction sites), language ('madam', 'racecar'), and cryptography (certain involutions). Same encoding axis; the same kind of structural fact across substrates.

wisdom: Symmetry is information. A restriction enzyme is a biological scissors that recognises a palindrome because the enzyme itself is symmetric — a homodimer with twofold rotational symmetry. The DNA's palindromic site lets the enzyme bind both strands the same way. The cell uses palindromes as recognition tokens the same way a language uses palindromes as memorable patterns. The Shepherd treats palindromic structure as a strong signal on the encoding axis — when you see it, something is being marked.

title: MD5 is broken; SHA-256 is strong — hash strength is time-stamped
situation: MD5 is classified as broken (practical collision attacks since 2004; chosen-prefix collisions in seconds on commodity hardware by 2009). SHA-256 is classified as strong under current NIST guidance. A hash function that was strong in 1992 (MD5) can be broken by 2009 — strength is a time-stamped property, not an eternal one.

pre_run.summary: Two parallel hash_strength checks: MD5 → broken (matches NIST deprecation), SHA-256 → strong (matches current NIST guidance).

domain_results.detail: hash_strength_algorithm=md5 → 'broken' (matches claim)
hash_strength_algorithm=sha256 → 'strong' (matches claim)

axis_overlaps.note: Strength decays. The same axis that records DNA mutation rates and Scripture transmission history records cryptographic strength erosion. What is verifiable today may need re-verification tomorrow.

pre_run.verification_note: md5/sha1 broken, sha256 strong (NIST), and sha256('hello') recomputed -- HOLDS 4/4 on /derivation/verify; false 'md5 strong' BROKE. Proof attached 2026-06-11.

wisdom: Hash strength is a snapshot, not a constant. MD5 was strong, then weak, then broken — over fifteen years, as factoring and collision-search techniques improved. SHA-256 stands today because no one has published a practical attack. The Shepherd treats hash strength like the engine treats almanac verdicts — labelled with a time-stamp, subject to OBSOLETE when reality moves on. Trusting MD5 in 2026 is the same kind of error as trusting a verifier output without checking when it was computed.

title: Slow sand filter — biological water purification (Paisley, 1804)
situation: A bed of 0.15–0.30 mm fine sand 60–120 cm deep, fed water at 0.1–0.4 m/hr surface loading, develops a biological 'schmutzdecke' (1–2 cm gelatinous layer) on top that removes 99%+ of bacteria and turbidity by combined filtration + biofilm predation.

pre_run.summary: Mechanically simple. Biological mechanism is the active filter; sand is only the substrate.

domain_results.detail: 0.1–0.4 m/hr surface loading is the validated design range (WHO, 1996)
schmutzdecke removes E. coli at >2 log (99%) reliably; >4 log (99.99%) when mature

steps: wash sand until rinse runs clear
place gravel underdrain 10 cm deep
place sand 80 cm deep on top
fill from below with clean water to wet bed
introduce raw water to 5 cm above sand
run continuously at 0.2 m/hr; harvest filtered water from outlet
after 2–6 weeks, schmutzdecke matures and pathogen removal jumps
make_it.time: build: 1 day. maturation: 2–6 weeks before drinkable.
make_it.cost_usd_2026: $30–$80 for a household unit (drum + sand + plumbing)
make_it.scale: household to village; no electricity required

wisdom: The schmutzdecke is the actual filter; the sand is just the bed. This is the oldest municipal-scale water treatment in continuous use — Paisley 1804, London 1829, still running in dozens of cities. The Shepherd brings this when the user asks about water without grid: it works without power, without consumables (no replaceable cartridges), without chemistry, and it scales from a 55-gallon drum to a city.

title: Solar still — single-effect (Telkes / Bjorksten, 1950s)
situation: A black-bottomed shallow water pan under a sloped transparent cover. Solar input evaporates water; vapor condenses on the cooler cover and runs down to a collection trough. At 6 kWh/m²/day insolation, single-effect yield is 3–4 L of distilled water per m² per day.

pre_run.summary: Energy balance: 1 L water requires 2257 kJ latent heat at 100°C (less at lower T). At 6 kWh/m²/day = 21,600 kJ, with ~40% net thermal efficiency to evaporation, yield ≈ 3.8 L/m²/day.

axis_overlaps.note: First-law energy balance is the design constraint

materials: shallow black pan or basin liner
glass or clear plastic sheet (4–6mm, sloped)
steps: build insulated box, paint inside black
fill pan with 2–3 cm of raw/salt/brackish water
set glass cover at 10–30° slope so condensate runs to a trough
seal joints to prevent vapor escape
harvest from trough; refill pan as needed
make_it.time: 1–2 days build
make_it.scale: household; multiple panels for a family

wisdom: The single most important off-grid water tool for brackish / saline / contaminated supplies — the still doesn't filter, it distills. Everything in the input except pure water stays in the pan. Works on seawater, urine, swamp water, runoff. Energy is free; the only consumable is the pan getting scaled with whatever was dissolved. The Shepherd brings this when the source water is bad, not just murky.

title: Chlorination dosing — free chlorine residual ≥0.5 mg/L for safe water

patent.inventor: Sims Woodhead (1897) / Major Carl Darnall (1910, US Army)

situation: Adding sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) to water at 2 mg/L active chlorine for 30 minutes contact time, holding a free chlorine residual of ≥0.5 mg/L, inactivates >99.99% of bacteria, viruses, and Giardia cysts. NaClO + H₂O ⇌ HClO + NaOH; HClO is the active germicide.

pre_run.summary: WHO CT (concentration × time) values: free chlorine at 0.5 mg/L × 30 min achieves 4-log inactivation of bacteria and viruses at pH 6–8, T ≥ 5°C.

domain_results.detail: NaClO hydrolysis to HClO is pH-dependent; at pH 7, ~75% is HClO
WHO drinking-water guideline: ≥0.5 mg/L free chlorine residual after 30 min contact

materials: unscented household bleach (5.25% NaClO) OR calcium hypochlorite tablets
steps: measure water volume
add 2 drops of 5.25% bleach per liter of clear water (4 drops if cloudy)
stir, wait 30 minutes
test with strip; should read ≥0.5 mg/L free chlorine
if no residual after 30 min, double the dose and repeat
make_it.time: 30 minutes contact
make_it.scale: individual to community

wisdom: The cheapest, most-tested, most-deployed water disinfection in history — Major Darnall's 1910 work pulled chlorination from theory into universal practice and dropped waterborne disease mortality by orders of magnitude. The Shepherd brings this when the water is clear but suspect; pair with a sand filter if the water is also cloudy (chlorine doesn't penetrate turbidity well).

title: Pasteurization — heat-only pathogen kill (Pasteur, 1864)
situation: Heating liquid food (milk, juice, water) to 63°C for 30 minutes (LTLT, vat) OR 72°C for 15 seconds (HTST, flash) inactivates vegetative bacterial pathogens including Salmonella, Listeria, E. coli, and Mycobacterium bovis to log-5+ reduction, without sterilizing (spores survive).

pre_run.summary: Time-temperature inactivation; D-value (decimal reduction time) for M. bovis at 63°C ≈ 5 minutes, so 30 min ≈ 6 log kill.

domain_results.detail: M. bovis pasteurization kinetics: D₆₃ ≈ 5 min; 30 min ≈ 6-log inactivation
Protein denaturation kinetics follow Arrhenius below boiling

materials: pot deep enough for water bath
steps: put liquid in clean jars or container
place in water bath
heat to 63°C and hold 30 min (or 72°C × 15 sec for skilled operators)
cool quickly to <4°C
store cold
make_it.time: 45 min total per batch
make_it.scale: household; commercial pasteurizers scale to thousands of liters

wisdom: Pasteurization is not sterilization. Spore-formers (Clostridium, Bacillus) survive — that's why pasteurized milk still spoils, just slowly. The Shepherd uses this when the goal is *safe* not *eternal*: raw milk from a known cow becomes safe milk that keeps a week refrigerated. For shelf-stable, you need canning (separate gem).

title: Mason jar home canning — US 22,186 (1858)
situation: Boiling-water-bath canning of high-acid food (pH ≤ 4.6) in a Mason-style glass jar with metal lid + rubber seal, processed at 100°C for 10–40 minutes (depending on jar size and food), produces shelf-stable food because heat kills vegetative pathogens and the cooling-vapor pressure differential creates a vacuum seal that prevents recontamination.

pre_run.summary: Acid (pH ≤ 4.6) excludes C. botulinum; boiling kills the rest. Vacuum seal forms on cooling because internal water vapor (10–101 kPa) condenses and external atmosphere (101 kPa) compresses the lid.

domain_results.detail: Vegetative pathogens inactivated at 100°C × 10+ min; C. botulinum requires pH ≤ 4.6 OR pressure canning (≥ 240°F / 116°C)
Vacuum forms from vapor-pressure differential P_atm − P_vapor at cooled T

materials: the food (pH ≤ 4.6: tomatoes, fruit, pickles, jams)
tools: large pot deep enough to cover jars by 2.5 cm
steps: sterilize jars in boiling water 10 min
fill jars with hot food, leave 1.25 cm headspace
wipe rim clean, apply lid finger-tight
submerge in boiling water 10–40 min by recipe
lift out, set on towel to cool
lid should *ping* concave within an hour — that's the seal
any jar that doesn't seal, refrigerate and use within 1 week
make_it.time: 30–90 min per batch
make_it.cost_usd_2026: $1.50 per jar amortized over many seasons (lids replace; jars don't)
make_it.scale: kitchen counter; 7 quart jars per batch typical

wisdom: Conservation balance applied to time sequence. The Mason jar design is unchanged in 168 years because the physics doesn't drift: vapor pressure, atmospheric pressure, the same rubber seal principle. Low-acid foods (meat, beans, vegetables not pickled) need pressure canning — boiling-water bath alone is NOT safe for them; botulism is the failure mode. The Shepherd refuses to recommend boiling-bath canning for low-acid foods.

title: Lactic-acid fermentation — sauerkraut/kimchi/pickle preservation
situation: Vegetables submerged in 2–3% salt brine under anaerobic conditions: Leuconostoc mesenteroides ferments sugars first, dropping pH; Lactobacillus plantarum takes over below pH 4.5 and continues to pH 3.4. Below pH 4.0 most pathogens cannot grow; vitamin C is preserved or increased.

pre_run.summary: Salt selects against putrefactive bacteria; LAB tolerate salt; their lactic-acid byproduct progressively lowers pH; below pH 4.0 the system becomes self-sterilizing.

domain_results.detail: Two-stage LAB succession (Leuconostoc → Lactobacillus) is the canonical sauerkraut microbiology
C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₃H₆O₃ (homolactic) — atoms balance
Vitamin C preserved; B-vitamins increased by LAB synthesis

tools: weight to keep vegetables submerged (smaller jar of water works)
steps: shred or chop vegetables
salt to 2% by weight (20g salt per kg veg) for kraut, or make 3% brine for cucumbers
pack tightly in jar, releasing juice
submerge all solids below liquid surface — exposure to air = mold
loose lid (allow CO₂ out) at 18–22°C
taste at day 3, 7, 14 — ready when sour enough
move to cold storage to slow fermentation
make_it.time: build: 15 min. fermentation: 1–6 weeks.
make_it.scale: single jar to barrels

wisdom: The microbes are the technology; you are only the substrate engineer. Salt + anaerobic = the niche LAB own; competitors lose. This is the only major preservation method that *adds* nutrition rather than removing it — B-vitamins, K2, probiotics all appear from the fermentation. The Shepherd brings this when the user has more vegetables than they can eat now and no refrigeration.

title: Salt curing — water activity (a_w) ≤ 0.85 blocks pathogen growth
situation: Curing meat or fish with 8–15% salt by weight reduces water activity to a_w ≤ 0.85; below this threshold, Staphylococcus aureus growth is blocked, and below a_w 0.75 nearly all pathogenic bacteria are excluded. Salt-cured pork (~12% salt) reaches a_w ≈ 0.75–0.80 after equilibration.

pre_run.summary: Water activity (not water content) is the operative variable. Salt lowers a_w by binding water osmotically. Microbial growth thresholds are well-characterized: most bacteria need a_w > 0.91; S. aureus = 0.86; halophiles = 0.75; most molds = 0.70.

domain_results.detail: a_w = (mass-fraction-water in salt-water mixture); 12% NaCl reduces a_w to ~0.78
S. aureus growth threshold a_w = 0.86; C. botulinum = 0.94; most molds = 0.70

materials: non-iodized salt (8–15% by weight of meat)
optional: sugar, spices, nitrite (Prague powder #1 for true 'curing' with color)
steps: weigh meat
weigh salt: 3% for short-cure bacon (5–7 days), 5–10% for traditional dry-cure (weeks)
rub salt evenly
refrigerate or cellar 1–4 weeks depending on thickness
rinse off excess salt
for shelf-stable: hang in cool dry place to equilibrate further (prosciutto: 6–18 months)
make_it.time: 1 week to 18 months depending on product
make_it.cost_usd_2026: $1–2 per kg salt (the meat is the variable)
make_it.scale: household to commercial

wisdom: Salt is the oldest preservative because it doesn't require fire, light, or cold. The Shepherd brings this when the user has more meat than they can use fresh and no freezer. Nitrite (Prague powder #1) adds anti-botulinum protection for whole-muscle cures — recommended for traditional methods; the historical 'saltpeter' (KNO₃) converts to nitrite during cure but is harder to dose safely.

title: Solar dehydrator — natural-convection food drying
situation: A black-painted solar collector heats air; the heated air rises through a chimney-stack drying chamber, removing moisture from food on racks. Inlet at solar absorber; food chamber above; vent at top creates draft. Target drying temperature 50–65°C; reduces food moisture to 10–20% (storage-safe at a_w < 0.6).

pre_run.summary: Natural-convection draft from absorber + buoyancy difference; rate of water removal limited by air mass-flow × water-carrying capacity at exit temperature.

domain_results.detail: Air at 60°C / 30% RH carries ~36 g water/kg air at saturation; typical drying air carries 10–20 g/kg above ambient

materials: black paint or black absorber sheet
clear glazing (glass or polycarbonate) for collector
steps: build inclined solar absorber 1–2 m² (slope ≈ local latitude), painted black behind glazing
build drying chamber on top, 3–5 racks of mesh
vent at top 5–10 cm diameter
place sliced food on racks (single layer)
in 6–8 hours of sun, most fruits/vegetables/jerky reach <20% moisture
make_it.time: build: 1–2 days. drying: 1–3 sunny days per batch.
make_it.scale: household; multiple units for a homestead

wisdom: Drying is the oldest preservation method that survives in modern PD literature, and the most under-used. No salt, no sugar, no jars. The Shepherd brings this for fruits, jerky, herbs, mushrooms, anything that surrenders water without melting. Combine with vacuum-sealing or low-O₂ storage for years of shelf life.

title: Root cellar — soil thermal mass + humidity for winter storage
situation: An underground or earth-bermed chamber at 0.5–2 m depth holds 32–40°F (0–4°C) and 85–95% relative humidity year-round via soil thermal mass and ground-water-table coupling. Root vegetables (potatoes, carrots, beets, parsnips, cabbage) keep 3–6 months with no electricity.

pre_run.summary: Below frost line, soil temperature converges to mean annual temperature (typically 4–10°C in temperate climates). Above ground vents allow CO₂/ethylene venting and humidity regulation.

domain_results.detail: Soil temperature damping: amplitude halves every ~1 m depth; phase lags ~30 days per m
Respiration of stored roots slows ~half per 10°C drop (Q₁₀)

materials: concrete blocks / poured concrete / stone for walls
steps: dig below frost line (varies: 1–2 m in temperate)
build sturdy roof (load: earth above + snow)
install two vent pipes — one low for cool intake, one high for warm exhaust
earth-berm the exposed walls if any
add humidity by leaving floor as dirt or by water bowl
store roots in sand/sawdust boxes for best results
make_it.time: build: 1–2 weeks (DIY); 1 week (with help/equipment)
make_it.cost_usd_2026: $500–$3000 depending on excavation and material reuse
make_it.scale: household; size for family annual root harvest

wisdom: A working root cellar is independence from refrigeration for the entire root crop. The Shepherd brings this for people on land with the option to dig: the same hole gives you cold storage in winter and cool storage in summer, with zero ongoing input. Pairs with canning (high-acid + low-acid foods) and salt curing for full year-round storage.

title: Composting C:N ratio — 25–30:1 + moisture + air = thermophilic compost
situation: A pile of organic matter with initial C:N ratio ~25–30:1, moisture 50–60%, and adequate aeration reaches thermophilic temperatures of 55–65°C within days; pathogens and weed seeds are killed at >55°C × 3 days; finished compost is produced in 4–8 weeks.

pre_run.summary: Microbial metabolism — bacteria, actinomycetes, fungi — release heat as they oxidize C. N-rich materials (greens) provide protein nitrogen; C-rich (browns) provide energy carbon.

domain_results.detail: Composting is aerobic oxidation: CₓHᵧNᵤOᵥ + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + NH₃ + heat; mass balance holds
Thermophilic phase 55–65°C inactivates Salmonella, E. coli, viable weed seeds

materials: greens (kitchen scraps, fresh manure, grass clippings)
steps: aim for ~1 part green to 3 parts brown by volume
build pile at least 1 m³ for thermal mass
moisten so material is damp like a wrung-out sponge
turn weekly (or whenever temp drops) to re-aerate
finished in 4–8 weeks; smells like earth, not garbage
make_it.time: ongoing; finished pile in 4–8 weeks
make_it.scale: any; backyard to farm

wisdom: Compost converts the unusable into the irreplaceable: kitchen scraps + leaves become tilth that holds water, feeds plants, and rebuilds soil structure. The Shepherd brings this whenever the user asks about soil, garbage, or fertility — same answer, three doorways. The thermophilic phase is what separates compost from rot: cool piles smell bad and harbor pathogens; hot piles smell like earth.

title: Bordeaux mixture — copper sulfate + lime fungicide (Millardet, 1885)
situation: Mixing copper sulfate (CuSO₄·5H₂O) with hydrated lime (Ca(OH)₂) in water at the classic 1:1:100 ratio (1 kg CuSO₄ : 1 kg lime : 100 L water) produces a blue suspension of copper hydroxide (Cu(OH)₂) particles. Sprayed on plants, it controls fungal diseases including downy mildew, powdery mildew, and black spot. CuSO₄ + Ca(OH)₂ → Cu(OH)₂ + CaSO₄.

pre_run.summary: Lime precipitates the copper out as fine suspended Cu(OH)₂; this sticks to leaf surfaces and slowly releases Cu²⁺ ions on contact with fungal spores.

domain_results.detail: CuSO₄ + Ca(OH)₂ → Cu(OH)₂↓ + CaSO₄; atoms and charge balance
Cu²⁺ disrupts fungal enzyme thiol groups; classic broad-spectrum contact fungicide since 1885

tools: two non-metal buckets (it dissolves galvanized metal!)
steps: dissolve 10 g CuSO₄ in 5 L water in bucket A (plastic / ceramic)
stir 10 g lime into 5 L water in bucket B
slowly pour A into B (NEVER reverse) while stirring
use within 24 hours — formulation degrades
spray on plants at first signs of fungal disease; reapply after rain
make_it.time: 30 min to mix; spray as needed in season
make_it.cost_usd_2026: <$10 for a year's supply for a household garden
make_it.scale: garden to vineyard (still used on certified-organic vineyards)

wisdom: Bordeaux mixture saved European vineyards from downy mildew in the 1880s and is still on the certified-organic list. Copper is a heavy metal — overuse builds up in soil. Use sparingly, target the disease, and rotate with other approaches (sulfur, biofungicides, resistant varieties). The Shepherd brings this when the user describes mildew, rust, blight — and notes the soil-buildup caveat.

title: Legume nitrogen fixation — Rhizobium + nitrogenase = free fertilizer
situation: Legume plants (clover, alfalfa, beans, peas, soybeans, peanuts) host Rhizobium bacteria in root nodules. The nitrogenase enzyme converts atmospheric N₂ to NH₃ (ammonia), then to amino acids. A soybean crop can fix 100–250 kg N/ha/year — replacing the same nitrogen a synthetic fertilizer would supply. Rotation with non-legumes captures the residual N.

pre_run.summary: N₂ + 8 H⁺ + 8 e⁻ + 16 ATP → 2 NH₃ + H₂ + 16 ADP + 16 Pᵢ (nitrogenase reaction). Energy-expensive but free to the farmer.

domain_results.detail: Nitrogenase reaction: N₂ + 8 H⁺ + 8 e⁻ + 16 ATP → 2 NH₃ + H₂ + 16 ADP + Pᵢ
Rhizobium-legume symbiosis fixes 100–250 kg N/ha/year for soybeans

materials: legume seed (rotation cover or food crop)
Rhizobium inoculant matched to legume (most seed sold pre-inoculated; otherwise $5/kg seed)
steps: select a legume matched to climate: clover (cool season cover), cowpea (warm season cover), soybean / common bean / pea (food + N)
plant at recommended density
inoculate seed with Rhizobium if soil hasn't grown this legume in 3+ years
let it grow; if cover crop, terminate at flowering (max N in plant tissue)
plant heavy-feeder crop (corn, brassicas, leafy greens) in same field next season
make_it.time: one season cover; or harvest food + reduced fertilizer next year
make_it.scale: garden to industrial farm

wisdom: Legume rotation is the oldest known fertility-restoring practice and one of the most powerful — the Hellriegel-Wilfarth 1886 demonstration explained why it worked but humans had been doing it for millennia. The Shepherd brings this whenever the user asks about reducing fertilizer dependence, building soil, or what to plant after a heavy feeder.

title: Oral rehydration solution (ORS) — WHO formula, ~$0.10/dose

patent.inventor: Norbert Hirschhorn / Dilip Mahalanabis / David Nalin (independently)

situation: 1 liter of safe water + 2.6 g NaCl + 1.5 g KCl + 2.9 g trisodium citrate + 13.5 g glucose = WHO low-osmolarity ORS (245 mOsm/L). Reduces cholera and acute diarrhea mortality from ~50% to <1%. Glucose drives sodium absorption via SGLT1 even when the gut is otherwise leaking water.

pre_run.summary: Sodium-glucose cotransport (SGLT1) is preserved during diarrheal illness even when bulk water absorption fails. Glucose pulls Na⁺ in; Na⁺ pulls water in.

domain_results.detail: Osmolarity ≈ (Na⁺ + K⁺ + Cl⁻ + citrate + glucose) ≈ 245 mOsm/L
Lancet 1978: ORS reduces case-fatality of severe dehydrating diarrhea from 30–50% to <1%

materials: optional: potassium chloride (NoSalt salt substitute) and baking soda or citrate
steps: FIELD VERSION (most lives saved at this complexity): 1 L safe water + 1 tsp (5 g) salt + 8 tsp (40 g) sugar. Stir to dissolve. Sip slowly over hours.
FULL WHO FORMULA: add 1/2 tsp KCl substitute + 1/2 tsp baking soda if available.
make_it.time: 5 min to mix; replace fluids over hours
make_it.scale: household; ORS sachets cost <$0.05 each commercially

wisdom: The Lancet called ORS 'potentially the most important medical advance of this century' (1978). Cheaper than the ambulance, saves more lives than the antibiotic. The Shepherd brings this whenever the user describes diarrhea, vomiting, dehydration, or heat illness in someone able to drink. Severe dehydration with shock still needs IV — ORS is for the conscious patient.

title: Hand-washing with chlorinated lime — Semmelweis (1847)
situation: Hand-washing in chlorinated lime (Ca(OCl)₂) solution between patients reduced puerperal fever mortality in Vienna General Hospital's First Obstetric Clinic from 18.3% (1846) to 2.2% (1848). Modern equivalent: soap + 20 seconds, OR alcohol-based hand rub (60–80% ethanol or isopropanol).

pre_run.summary: Mechanical removal (soap + friction + rinse) + chemical kill (alcohol or chlorine) reduces transient skin microbes by 2–4 log per wash.

domain_results.detail: Mortality drop 18.3% → 2.2% over a single year is a >5-sigma effect for n ≈ 4000 births/year
Hand-borne Streptococcus pyogenes was the puerperal fever pathogen; transmission interrupted by handwash

steps: wet hands
apply soap
scrub all surfaces 20 seconds (sing 'Happy Birthday' twice)
rinse
dry on clean towel or air
for high-risk situations (medical, food prep, latrine exit): wash before AND after
make_it.time: 30 seconds per wash
make_it.scale: individual to hospital

wisdom: Semmelweis was institutionalized for insisting on hand-washing — the medical establishment refused him; he died in an asylum in 1865, the same year Lister built on his work. Hand-washing remains the single cheapest, highest-impact medical intervention ever discovered. The Shepherd brings this whenever the user is preparing food, treating a wound, attending a birth, or moving between sick and well people.

title: Iodine tincture antiseptic — broad-spectrum since 1839
situation: 2% iodine + 2.4% sodium iodide in 47% ethanol (USP Tincture of Iodine) kills bacteria, viruses, fungi, and protozoa on contact. Applied to skin pre-procedure, it reduces surgical-site infection. 5 drops of 2% iodine per liter of water purifies it in 30 minutes (emergency dose; not for chronic use due to thyroid load).

pre_run.summary: I₂ oxidizes sulfhydryl groups in proteins; broad-spectrum because that mechanism is universal across microbes.

domain_results.detail: I₂ in ethanol stays dissolved; KI or NaI shifts equilibrium to I₃⁻ keeping it soluble
Pre-surgical iodine reduces SSI; emergency water purification at 5 drops 2%/L × 30 min

materials: 2% iodine tincture (USP, available OTC) — OR — iodine crystals + ethanol if making from scratch
steps: WOUND: clean wound with water; apply tincture to surrounding skin (NOT inside open wound — it damages healing tissue)
PRE-PROCEDURE: paint skin, let dry 30 sec before incision
WATER: 5 drops of 2% iodine per liter clear water, mix, wait 30 min before drinking
make_it.time: 30 seconds to apply; 30 min for water disinfection
make_it.cost_usd_2026: $5 for a bottle that lasts years
make_it.scale: individual to small clinic

wisdom: Iodine fell out of fashion when alcohol-based antiseptics gained market share, but for spectrum and shelf-life it has no peer in field medicine. The Shepherd brings this when chlorhexidine and povidone-iodine aren't available — old-fashioned tincture works. Caveat: do NOT use long-term for water disinfection (thyroid load); chlorine or filtration is preferred for chronic supply.

title: Penicillin production — Florey/Heatley fermentation (1940, expired)

patent.number: US 2,442,141 (Moyer 1948, expired) and others, all expired
patent.inventor: Howard Florey, Norman Heatley, Ernst Chain (extraction); Mary Hunt's mold strain (1943)

situation: Penicillium chrysogenum grown in corn-steep liquor at 24–26°C with vigorous aeration produces penicillin G (benzylpenicillin). The β-lactam ring covalently inhibits bacterial transpeptidase, preventing peptidoglycan cross-linking, lysing growing Gram-positive bacteria. Production: gram quantities per liter of broth after 5–7 days; industrial yields reach 50+ g/L with selected strains.

pre_run.summary: Submerged-culture fermentation; the science is fully published and unpatented. Yield depends on strain selection and feed media chemistry.

domain_results.detail: P. chrysogenum strain NRRL 1951 (Mary Hunt's cantaloupe isolate, 1943) and descendants underlie nearly all production
Penicillin G structure: 6-aminopenicillanic acid + phenylacetic acid sidechain
Mechanism: β-lactam binds PBPs (penicillin-binding proteins), blocking peptidoglycan synthesis

materials: P. chrysogenum culture (NRRL 1951 is publicly distributed)
sterile fermenter (5–20 L for home / small lab)
steps: sterilize all equipment and media
inoculate Penicillium into broth
ferment 5–7 days at 24°C with aeration
filter mycelium off
extract penicillin into organic solvent at pH 2.0
back-extract to aqueous at pH 7.0 → crystallize
make_it.time: 1–2 weeks per batch
make_it.cost_usd_2026: Setup $1000–5000 for clean small-lab; per-dose cost low at scale
make_it.scale: Lab scale possible; clinical-grade purity requires controlled environment. **The Shepherd does NOT recommend home production for treatment** — purity, dose accuracy, and contamination risk are real. The knowledge is in the keeping because the chemistry is in the keeping; production at therapeutic standard requires a proper facility.

wisdom: Penicillin is the canonical 'PD knowledge that changed everything' — Florey and Heatley deliberately did not patent the basic discovery, considering it the heritage of humanity. The Shepherd carries the chemistry and the history; he is honest that producing safe therapeutic doses at home is harder than the chemistry suggests, and that current strains and methods belong in proper labs. For the user who wants to understand HOW antibiotics work, this is the entry. For the user who needs antibiotics, they need a clinic.

title: Rocket stove — L-feed insulated combustion chamber (Winiarski 1980s)

patent.number: Aprovecho Research Center designs, openly published PD

situation: An L-shaped feed (horizontal stick chamber + vertical insulated combustion chimney) with a tight pot skirt achieves near-complete wood combustion. Thermal efficiency to pot reaches 30–45%, compared to 5–15% for an open three-stone fire. Wood consumption per cooking task drops 4–7x.

pre_run.summary: Insulated combustion chamber maintains temperatures >700°C → complete oxidation of CO and volatiles. Tight skirt forces hot gas into prolonged contact with the pot wall.

domain_results.detail: Open fire ~5–15% efficient to pot; rocket stove 30–45% (Aprovecho lab measurements)
Combustion 2C + O₂ → 2CO + heat; CO + ½O₂ → CO₂ + more heat; both go to completion above 700°C

materials: fire brick or insulating brick OR a metal can stuffed with wood ash insulation
metal pipe / can for the chimney
steps: build vertical insulated combustion chamber 25–40 cm tall, internal diameter 10–15 cm
horizontal feed at base, smaller cross-section than chimney (ratio ~2:1 area, chimney bigger)
elevated grate inside feed so air flows under wood
build pot skirt around top: gap of 6–12 mm between pot wall and skirt
feed only the tips of sticks; advance them as they burn
make_it.time: build: 1–3 hours (simple brick) to 1 day (welded steel)
make_it.scale: single-pot to small institutional

wisdom: The rocket stove cuts wood use by 4–7× and indoor smoke even more — meaning more forest standing, less respiratory disease, less time spent gathering. The Shepherd brings this when the user cooks with wood and wants to use less of it, or when smoke inhalation is a household issue. Pairs naturally with the rocket mass heater (separate gem) for space heat.

title: Parabolic solar cooker — geometric focal-point concentration

patent.inventor: Augustin Mouchot (1869); design fundamentals predate patent system

situation: A parabolic reflector with focal length f, paraboloid equation y = x²/(4f), reflects parallel sun rays to a single focal point. At 1 m² aperture and 800 W/m² insolation, focal-point irradiance reaches 80+ kW/m² (theoretical) and 5–50 kW/m² practical; cooking temperatures exceed 200°C; water boils in 5–15 minutes.

pre_run.summary: Conic-section optics; parallel-ray-to-focal-point is the defining property of a paraboloid. Concentration ratio is the geometric ratio of aperture area to focal-region area.

domain_results.detail: Paraboloid focuses parallel rays at the focal point (defining property)
1 m² × 800 W/m² × 50% optical efficiency ÷ small focal area → cooking temperatures

materials: reflective sheet (mylar, aluminized polyester, polished aluminum)
tools: string + chalk for paraboloid template OR satellite-dish recycled
steps: acquire or construct a paraboloid: easiest is a recycled satellite dish lined with mylar
calculate focal length (for a recycled dish: place straight edge across the rim, measure deepest point d; f = D²/(16d) where D = dish diameter)
mount pot or stand at the focal point
aim at sun by hand or with simple shadow-pointer; track every 15–20 min
make_it.time: 1 day build (recycled dish) to 2–3 days (built from scratch)
make_it.scale: single-pot to community-kitchen

wisdom: The Shepherd brings this where fuel is scarce or smoke is sickening. WARNING: parabolic cookers produce concentrated light at the focal point that can burn skin or eyes in seconds — children and pets must stay clear; users should wear welder's goggles when adjusting aim. Trade-offs: no clouds, no night, no rain — pairs with rocket stove for cloudy and evening cooking.

title: Charcoal retort kiln — pyrolysis at 400–700°C
situation: Heating wood in an oxygen-limited chamber (retort) to 400–700°C drives off volatile compounds (water, tars, methanol, acetic acid, wood gas) leaving behind carbon-rich charcoal (~75–85% carbon by weight). Yield: ~25–35% of dry wood mass becomes charcoal. The released wood gas can be captured and burned to heat the kiln, making the process self-sustaining after startup.

pre_run.summary: Pyrolysis (anaerobic thermal decomposition): C₆H₁₀O₅ (cellulose) → C (charcoal) + CO + CO₂ + CH₄ + H₂O + tar; product slate depends on temperature.

domain_results.detail: Pyrolysis temperatures: 200–280°C drying + initial decomp; 280–500°C tar and gas release; 500–700°C completion to high-carbon char
Wood gas energy content ~5 MJ/Nm³; can be burned to drive the kiln

steps: inner drum: pack tightly with split wood, lid loose (vents)
place inner drum upside-down inside outer drum filled with fuel wood between the two
