The engine checked the numbers: twelve fifths (3/2 to the twelfth) come to 531441/524288 of seven octaves -- not 1, but a hair over. The crookedness is real and measurable.
wisdom: The fifth and the octave are each perfect, yet they will not agree; pile up the one and you always miss the other by a hair. Music rests on an honest compromise, not a flaw to be ashamed of. "That which is crooked cannot be made straight: and that which is wanting cannot be numbered" (Ecclesiastes 1:15).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_pythagorean_comma. The sealed proof shows (3/2)^12 / 2^7 = 531441/524288, not 1 -- twelve pure fifths overshoot seven octaves by the Pythagorean comma.

title: A piano is a little wrong everywhere, on purpose
verification: Since no tuning can be perfectly pure, the modern keyboard shares the unavoidable error evenly. Each of the twelve semitones is made the exact same ratio -- the twelfth root of two -- so the octave closes perfectly and every key, from C to F-sharp, is equally usable, at the cost of every fifth being a whisker flat. That is why you can play a song in ANY key on a piano, and why a choir or a string quartet, free of fixed frets, drifts back toward the pure ratios a piano can never quite reach. The compromise is what makes the instrument universal.
wisdom: Rather than let one note be perfect and another unplayable, the tuner spreads the small unavoidable error across them all, so that each bears a little and none is ruined. Fairness is often a shared and gentle imperfection. "Bear ye one another's burdens, and so fulfil the law of Christ" (Galatians 6:2).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_pythagorean_comma. Equal temperament hides the sealed Pythagorean comma by making every semitone 2^(1/12), so every key is usable.

title: A chord is hiding inside every single note
verification: Pluck one string and it does not sound one pure tone -- it quietly sings a whole stack of higher notes called overtones, at two times, three times, four times its frequency, and so on. The fourth, fifth, and sixth of those overtones are exactly a major chord. So the major triad is not something people invented; it is folded inside every note nature makes, which is why a valveless bugle (it can only play the overtones) sounds fanfares built from this very chord. The engine checked the frequencies: harmonics 4, 5, and 6 of a 100 Hz string are 400, 500, and 600 Hz -- a pure major third, minor third, and fifth.
wisdom: Strike one string and it sings not a single note but a quiet chord, the harmony folded inside it before any musician arrived. Order is not imposed on the world but discovered in it. "When the morning stars sang together, and all the sons of God shouted for joy" (Job 38:7).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_major_triad_is_overtone_ratios. The sealed proof shows harmonics 4:5:6 of a fundamental are a pure major triad.

title: Why some notes just sound good together
verification: Two notes sound sweet together when their frequencies form a simple whole-number ratio -- an octave is 2 to 1, a perfect fifth is 3 to 2, a major third is 5 to 4. The simpler the ratio, the more overtones the two notes share, and the more our ears hear them blending instead of clashing. That is the whole secret of consonance: not taste or training, but small numbers. The engine confirmed these exact ratios against the harmonic series.
wisdom: What pleases the ear is agreement in small, honest numbers -- voices that share much sound as one, and the more they hold in common the sweeter the blend. So it is with people. "Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity!" (Psalm 133:1).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_major_triad_is_overtone_ratios. The sealed proof shows consonant intervals are simple ratios (octave 2:1, fifth 3:2, third 5:4).

title: Your DNA carries its own backup copy
verification: DNA is a double strand, and the two halves are not the same -- they are mirror partners: wherever one strand has an A the other has a T, wherever one has a G the other has a C. That means each strand holds the complete recipe of the other. If one strand is damaged, the cell can rewrite the missing letters from the intact partner -- the second strand is a full backup, not just a check. The engine confirmed the pairing is exactly reversible: flip one strand to its partner and back, and you return to the original, every time.
wisdom: Each thread of the double helix holds the whole of the other, so nothing written there is lost while one strand survives to speak it back. The strength is in the pairing -- as it is with people. "Two are better than one... For if they fall, the one will lift up his fellow" (Ecclesiastes 4:9-10).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_watson_crick_is_reversible_code. The sealed proof shows complementation is a reversible involution, so each strand fully determines the other.

title: Why life can copy itself without losing a word
verification: When a cell divides, the two strands of its DNA unzip, and each one acts as a template to build a fresh partner -- so one double strand becomes two, each an exact copy of the original. Because the pairing rule (A with T, G with C) is exact and reversible, the message is handed down letter for letter, generation after generation, with no drift built in. The engine confirmed the copy is faithful: the rebuilt strand carries the identical information.
wisdom: A true message, rightly paired, can be passed down a thousand times and still read the same -- the medium wears out and is replaced, but the word endures. So the deepest things are kept. "The grass withereth, the flower fadeth: but the word of our God shall stand for ever" (Isaiah 40:8).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_watson_crick_is_reversible_code. The sealed proof shows each strand templates an exact copy via reversible complementarity.

title: Life's recipe is written to forgive a typo
verification: The instructions in your cells are written in three-letter words from a four-letter alphabet, which gives 64 possible words -- but there are only about 20 things to say (the amino acids). So most meanings have several spellings: six different words all mean Leucine, for instance. That spare room is not waste; it is forgiveness. Because synonyms differ mostly in their last letter, a single mis-copied letter usually lands on another word for the SAME thing, and the body builds the right part anyway. The engine confirmed it: change the third letter of those words and the meaning often does not move.
wisdom: A wise language leaves room to be misheard and still understood; it does not break at the first slip of the pen, but carries the meaning through. There is a mercy built into how the small mistakes are passed over. "The discretion of a man deferreth his anger; and it is his glory to pass over a transgression" (Proverbs 19:11).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_genetic_code_is_redundant. The sealed proof shows 64 codons map to ~21 meanings (degenerate), so many single-letter changes are silent.

title: Why most changes to your DNA do nothing at all
verification: Every time a cell copies its DNA, a few letters get mis-copied -- yet most of those changes never show. Two reasons, both real: the code has spare spellings (many words mean the same amino acid), so a wrong letter often still spells the right part; and where it does change the part, similar words tend to make similar parts. Only rarely does a single change matter. The engine confirmed the spare-spelling half of this: distinct codons that share a meaning.
wisdom: If every small fault were counted against us, none could stand; but most are quietly absorbed, never reckoned, and life goes on. So great is the patience woven into things. "If thou, LORD, shouldest mark iniquities, O Lord, who shall stand?" (Psalm 130:3).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_genetic_code_is_redundant. The sealed proof shows the degenerate code makes many copying errors silent.

title: The last digit of a card number is a built-in typo-catcher
verification: The final digit of a credit-card number, an ISBN on a book, or a barcode is not part of the count -- it is a CHECK DIGIT, figured from all the others. Mistype any single digit and a quick weighted sum no longer comes out even, so the mistake is caught the instant you enter it, with no database and no lookup. That is why a fat-fingered card number is rejected before it ever reaches the bank. The engine confirmed it: a valid ISBN weighted digit-sum lands exactly on a multiple of eleven -- remainder zero -- and a tampered digit cannot.
wisdom: A trustworthy number is built to expose its own corruption: alter one mark and the whole refuses to balance, so the lie cannot pass unseen. Truth has a way of carrying its own evidence. "Lying lips are abomination to the LORD: but they that deal truly are his delight" (Proverbs 12:22).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_checksum_is_modular_arithmetic. The sealed proof shows checksum validity is a weighted sum landing on a remainder of zero.

title: Some numbers are made to catch their own forgery
verification: It is one quiet trick wearing many badges: tack on a single extra digit, computed so the whole always lands on a round number, and any error in any part throws off the balance and is caught. Book numbers use it (counting by elevens), credit cards use it (the Luhn rule, by tens), barcodes use it (by tens). None of them understands the data; they only ask whether the sum comes out even. The engine confirmed two of them at once -- an ISBN by elevens, a card number by tens -- both valid exactly when the remainder is zero.
wisdom: Build a small honest witness into a thing and it will guard the rest, balancing only when nothing has been falsified -- the oldest law of the marketplace, made into arithmetic. "A false balance is an abomination to the LORD: but a just weight is his delight" (Proverbs 11:1).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_checksum_is_modular_arithmetic. The sealed proof shows ISBN (mod 11) and Luhn (mod 10) validity are both modular-zero conditions.

title: Two carriers, and a one-in-four chance
verification: Some traits are recessive -- they only show when a child gets the same copy from BOTH parents. If a mother and father each quietly carry one copy (they do not show it themselves), then for each child the chances fall out exactly: one in four will show the trait, two in four will be silent carriers like the parents, and one in four will be free of it altogether. This 1-in-4 is the heart of genetic counseling, and it resets fresh with every child -- the dice do not remember the last roll. The engine confirmed the arithmetic.
wisdom: What a child receives is not earned and not in our hands to dictate; we are given them as they come, each one a fresh and weighty gift, and we are stewards, not makers, of what they carry. "Lo, children are an heritage of the LORD: and the fruit of the womb is his reward" (Psalm 127:3).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_mendelian_ratio_is_probability. The sealed proof shows a monohybrid cross gives 3/4 dominant, 1/4 recessive (the 1-in-4).

title: Why a trait skips a generation and comes back
verification: A recessive trait can hide for a whole generation. A person can carry it without showing it, because a single dominant copy masks it; pass through such a carrier and the trait seems to vanish -- until two carriers meet and a child inherits two copies, and grandpa's blue eyes or great-aunt's red hair suddenly reappear. Nothing was lost; it was only hidden, waiting for the right pairing. The engine confirmed the underlying counts: the visible three-to-one masks a hidden one-two-one.
wisdom: What is concealed is not destroyed; it waits, and in its season it is brought to light again, for nothing is hidden that shall not be made manifest. "For there is nothing hid, which shall not be manifested; neither was any thing kept secret, but that it should come abroad" (Mark 4:22).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_mendelian_ratio_is_probability. The sealed proof shows the 3:1 phenotype ratio hides a 1:2:1 genotype ratio.

title: A single gram of anything holds 90 trillion joules
verification: Einstein E equals m c-squared means matter is frozen energy, and the exchange rate is staggering: multiply by the speed of light squared and one gram of ANY substance -- a paperclip, a raisin -- carries about 90 trillion joules, enough to power a town for hours. That is why nuclear reactions, which release a tiny fraction of a percent of mass as energy, utterly dwarf burning fuel, which only rearranges the outer skin of atoms. The engine checked it two ways: the formula truly comes out as energy (the units), and the number is that enormous (the arithmetic).
wisdom: The greatest powers are folded up small and unseen, and the solid world we touch is more concentrated fire than it ever lets on. What is visible was made from what is not. "Through faith we understand that the worlds were framed by the word of God, so that things which are seen were not made of things which do appear" (Hebrews 11:3).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_emc2_mass_is_bound_energy. The sealed proof shows E=mc^2 is dimensionally an energy and 1 g ~ 9x10^13 J.

title: The sun is quietly turning itself into light
verification: Sunlight is not free; the sun pays for it out of its own body. By Einstein famous rule, every burst of light carries away a little mass, and the sun sheds roughly four million tonnes of itself every second to keep shining -- and has for billions of years, with billions more to go, because its store is so vast. The warmth on your face this morning is matter that was, until eight minutes ago, part of a star. The engine confirmed the mass-to-energy conversion behind it.
wisdom: Light that gives life is bought by the spending of a body; the sun pours out its own substance, year upon year, that the earth may live -- a faint shadow of a greater giving. "Except a corn of wheat fall into the ground and die, it abideth alone: but if it die, it bringeth forth much fruit" (John 12:24).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_emc2_mass_is_bound_energy. The sealed proof shows mass converts to energy via E=mc^2; the Sun radiates ~4 million tonnes/second as light.

title: A moving clock runs slow, and it is only Pythagoras
verification: Time really does stretch for things in motion, and the reason is the humble carpenter triangle. Picture a clock that ticks by bouncing light up and down; set it moving sideways and, to a watcher, the light must travel a longer slanted path -- but light cannot go any faster, so each tick simply takes more time. Work the triangle and at 60 percent of light-speed a moving clock runs at exactly 80 percent (the old 3-4-5 right triangle). This is not theory: fast-moving particles and the clocks in GPS satellites are measured running slow by just this amount. The engine confirmed the triangle behind it.
wisdom: Even time, which we treat as the one fixed thing, bends and stretches, and is no master but a creature -- a reminder that our measures are small beside the One who made them. "Beloved, be not ignorant of this one thing, that one day is with the Lord as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day" (2 Peter 3:8).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_time_dilation_is_pythagoras. The sealed proof shows the time-dilation factor is the hypotenuse-over-leg of the light-clock triangle (3-4-5 at v=0.6c).

title: The faster you travel, the slower you age
verification: Because a moving clock runs slow, a fast traveler ages slightly less than the people he left behind. At everyday speeds the gap is far too tiny to notice -- a lifetime of jet travel buys back only a fraction of a second -- but it is real and measured: atomic clocks flown around the world come home a hair behind, and GPS satellites must correct for it constantly or your map would drift miles off. The engine confirmed the geometry that sets the exact amount.
wisdom: Our days run at different rates and none of them are firm; only One stands outside the current, unmoved while all our clocks drift. "For I am the LORD, I change not" (Malachi 3:6).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_time_dilation_is_pythagoras. The sealed proof shows the time-dilation factor gamma = 1/sqrt(1-(v/c)^2), tiny at low speed, real and measured.

title: In a room of 23, two of you probably share a birthday
verification: It feels like you would need 183 people (half of 365) before two share a birthday -- but it is only 23, where the odds pass even. The trick is that you are not matching one person to the rest; you are comparing every PAIR, and 23 people make 253 pairs. Matches show up at roughly the SQUARE ROOT of the possibilities, far sooner than gut sense says, and once you reach 366 people a shared birthday is guaranteed. The engine confirmed both halves of this rule: the square-root arrival and the certain match past the number of days.
wisdom: Our guesses about likelihood run shallow, and what feels like a rare wonder is often just arithmetic we did not do; better to count than to marvel or to fear. Yet nothing falls out merely by chance. "The lot is cast into the lap; but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD" (Proverbs 16:33).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_birthday_bound_halves_hash_security. The sealed proof shows collisions arrive at ~sqrt of the space and are guaranteed past the count (pigeonhole).

title: Coincidences happen far more often than they feel like they should
verification: The same math behind the birthday surprise explains why odd coincidences keep cropping up -- two friends with the same rare name, the same number twice in a day, a dream that seems to come true. With enough people and enough chances, the number of possible PAIRINGS explodes, so SOME striking match becomes nearly certain even when each single one is unlikely. A surprising coincidence is usually not a sign; it is just the law of large numbers doing its quiet work. The engine confirmed the rule: matches arrive at about the square root of the possibilities.
wisdom: Do not read too much into a startling coincidence, nor too little into the ordinary mercies; the rare event was always lurking in the arithmetic, while the daily bread was the real gift. "I returned, and saw under the sun, that... time and chance happeneth to them all" (Ecclesiastes 9:11).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_birthday_bound_halves_hash_security. The sealed proof shows matches arrive at ~sqrt of the possibility space (birthday bound).

title: Any number up to a million can be guessed in twenty yes-or-no questions
verification: Think of a number between one and a million and let me ask only yes-or-no questions -- is it higher than 500,000? -- and I will name it in about twenty guesses, because each good question cuts the field of what is left exactly in half. A million halves down to nothing in twenty steps (a thousand in ten); a billion in only thirty. This is the high-low game, and it is the same reason you do not read a phone book front to back. The engine checked the count: it really is about twenty questions for a million, not a hundred.
wisdom: The right question is worth more than a hundred guesses; one that splits the unknown in two does more than a sack of random tries. Ask better, not more. "It is the glory of God to conceal a thing: but the honour of kings is to search out a matter" (Proverbs 25:2).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_binary_search_is_information_bound. The sealed proof shows locating one of n items takes about log2(n) yes/no questions: 20 for a million.

title: To find a word, open the book to the middle -- not the front
verification: Looking up a word in the dictionary, you do not start at A and turn every page; you open near the middle, see whether your word falls before or after, and keep halving the part that is left. A thousand-page book gives up any word in about ten such steps. It is how a card catalog, a phone book, and a librarian all work, and there is no faster way by comparing -- the only quicker trick is to know the page outright. The engine confirmed the math: each halving is one honest step toward the answer.
wisdom: Diligent searching is not the same as frantic searching; the wise seeker does not scan everything, but narrows the field by half and half again, and so the lost is found without exhaustion. "If thou seekest her as silver, and searchest for her as for hid treasures; Then shalt thou understand" (Proverbs 2:4-5).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_binary_search_is_information_bound. The sealed proof shows halving search locates any item in about log2(n) steps.

title: You cannot outrun a sunbeam -- light passes everyone at the same speed
verification: Run toward a beam of light or flee from it as fast as you can, and it still rushes past you at exactly the same speed -- the famous speed of light. Stranger still, you cannot add your way up to it: a rocket launching a faster rocket launching a faster rocket never reaches that speed, only creeps closer. Light is the one speed that, added to itself, gives itself back unchanged. The engine confirmed the exact arithmetic: light plus light still equals light, while the everyday rule that would let you pass it simply breaks.
wisdom: Some things are not finish lines we sprint past but fixed points we only approach; the wise stop racing what was never meant to be overtaken and learn to walk in its light instead. "Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, and cometh down from the Father of lights, with whom is no variableness, neither shadow of turning" (James 1:17).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas located_velocity_addition_light_invariant. The sealed proof shows light is the fixed point of velocity addition: 1+1=1 in units of c.

title: Near the limit, half plus half does not make a whole
verification: Common sense says two half-speeds add up to full speed -- but near the edge of what is possible, that arithmetic fails. Combine half the speed of light with half again and you do not get the whole; you get four-fifths. Three-fifths and three-fifths make only fifteen-seventeenths. The closer you push toward the true ceiling, the less each addition buys you, and you never quite arrive. The engine checked these exact fractions and confirmed them; the naive half-plus-half-equals-whole answer broke.
wisdom: There are real ceilings in this life, and past a point each extra effort yields less, not more; the humble accept the boundary set for them rather than wearing themselves out trying to break it. "Hitherto shalt thou come, but no further: and here shall thy proud waves be stayed" (Job 38:11).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas located_velocity_addition_light_invariant. The sealed proof shows relativistic speeds add sub-linearly: 1/2+1/2=4/5, 3/5+3/5=15/17.

title: Two strangers can agree on a secret out loud, and no listener can carry it away
verification: Picture two people who never met, each with a private color of paint. They share one common color openly, each stirs in their own secret, and they swap the muddy mixtures where everyone can watch. Then each stirs in their private color once more -- and both end holding the exact same final shade, though no onlooker can un-mix the paint to find it. This is how your browser and a website agree on a key before any private data flows; only the public mixtures travel, never the secrets. The engine confirmed both sides reach the very same number, and a naive shortcut to guess it fails. (That a listener cannot work it out rests on a math problem no one has yet cracked.)
wisdom: Some things held in common were never handed across; they are arrived at, each party doing honest work in private, meeting in a place no thief can reach. "The secret things belong unto the LORD our God: but those things which are revealed belong unto us" (Deuteronomy 29:29).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_diffie_hellman_shared_secret. The sealed proof shows both parties reach the same shared value g^(ab) mod p; the security rests on the discrete-log problem (left honestly as conjecture).

title: When the same work reaches the same place in any order, you can meet without showing your hand
verification: The reason two strangers can reach a common secret is that the steps can be taken in either order and still land in the same spot -- yours-then-mine arrives exactly where mine-then-yours does. So neither has to reveal their private part; the order does not matter, only that each did the work. The engine confirmed the two paths meet at the identical value. It is agreement without exposure: common ground reached, nothing surrendered.
wisdom: True agreement is not always one side giving up its ground to the other; sometimes it is two doing the same faithful work and discovering they have arrived together. "Fulfil ye my joy, that ye be likeminded, having the same love, being of one accord, of one mind" (Philippians 2:2).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_diffie_hellman_shared_secret. The sealed proof shows (g^a)^b = (g^b)^a: the two orderings reach the same shared value.

title: A road can be easy to walk one way and all but impossible to walk back
verification: There are sums a child could do in a moment going forward that the cleverest machines cannot undo going backward. Locking a number up with the trick behind secure messaging takes the blink of an eye; trying to reverse it -- to recover what went in -- is a labor no one has found a shortcut for. The engine confirmed the easy direction is genuinely quick; the hard direction is only believed to be hard, a wall no one has yet found a door through. That lopsidedness is the whole secret of the age: the work is one-way on purpose.
wisdom: Many of life's doors swing freely one way and not the other -- a word said, a trust spent, a season passed -- so weigh the step forward, for the road back may have no shortcut. "That which is crooked cannot be made straight: and that which is wanting cannot be numbered" (Ecclesiastes 1:15).

keywords: easy to do hard to undo

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas located_modular_exponentiation_trapdoor. The sealed proof shows modular exponentiation is fast forward (O(log a)); reversing it (discrete log) is believed hard -- conjecture, not theorem.

title: Multiplying two big numbers is instant; pulling them back apart can defeat the world
verification: Take two large prime numbers and multiply them -- done in an instant. Now hand someone only the answer and ask which two primes made it: for big enough primes, no one alive knows how to do it in any reasonable time. Nearly all of the world's banking and private messaging rests on exactly this lopsidedness, a lock that shuts with a whisper and opens only to the one who kept the two halves. The engine confirmed the lock truly opens for the right key and refuses the wrong one; that unmaking the product is genuinely hard remains a belief no one has proven or broken.
wisdom: What is joined in a moment can take a lifetime to part, and some bonds were never meant to be undone by force but only opened by the one who holds the key. "A brother offended is harder to be won than a strong city: and their contentions are like the bars of a castle" (Proverbs 18:19).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_rsa_is_modular_inverse_of_primes. The sealed proof shows the RSA encrypt/decrypt round-trip is correct; factoring n into its primes is left honestly as conjecture, not theorem.

title: To find what two numbers share, keep only the remainder -- again and again
verification: Here is the trick for finding the largest number that divides two others evenly -- the same skill behind reducing a fraction to its simplest form. Divide the big by the small and throw the big away, keeping only what is left over; then do it again with that leftover; and again, until nothing remains. The last leftover before zero was the shared measure all along. For 48 and 18: 48 leaves 12, 18 leaves 6, 12 leaves nothing -- so 6 is the answer. The engine checked every step; the common measure really is the last remainder standing.
wisdom: The answer is often hiding in the small part you were tempted to throw away; keep the remainder, do not despise what is left over, for the least of it may turn out to be the measure of the whole. "For who hath despised the day of small things?" (Zechariah 4:10).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_euclid_gcd_is_remainder_recursion. The sealed proof shows the gcd is the last nonzero remainder of the Euclidean recursion (gcd(48,18)=6).

title: The oldest recipe in the book still runs every single day
verification: A method written down more than two thousand years ago, by Euclid the geometer, is still doing quiet work every time you open a secure website -- it is part of how the keys that guard your banking are made. It has never needed replacing because it was right: keep the remainder until none is left, and what stands at the end is the shared measure. The engine confirmed it holds exactly today as it did then. A true method does not age; it outlasts the empires that came after it.
wisdom: Not everything old is outworn; some of the oldest ways are the soundest, tested by centuries and found faithful, and the wise still walk in them. "Stand ye in the ways, and see, and ask for the old paths, where is the good way, and walk therein, and ye shall find rest for your souls" (Jeremiah 6:16).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_euclid_gcd_is_remainder_recursion. The sealed proof shows Euclid two-thousand-year-old remainder method computes the gcd, the engine of modern RSA key generation.

title: A good seal is either whole or broken -- there is no almost
verification: Modern files carry a kind of wax seal -- a string of characters called a fingerprint -- and it has a startling property: change a single letter in the file and about half the fingerprint changes, into something with no resemblance to the old. There is no near-miss, no smooth dial; two almost-identical files have wildly different seals. The engine measured it on real digests: changing one letter of the word hello flipped 131 of the 256 marks. (That no two different files anywhere could ever share a seal is believed but not yet proven.)
wisdom: Some things do not come in degrees; a seal is whole or it is broken, and a single fault unmakes the whole. So it is with integrity: it is not nine-tenths kept. "For whosoever shall keep the whole law, and yet offend in one point, he is guilty of all" (James 2:10).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_sha256_avalanche_is_measurable. The sealed proof measures the avalanche effect: a one-letter change flips ~51 percent (131/256) of the hash bits; collision-resistance left as conjecture.

title: You cannot quietly change one letter and keep the same fingerprint
verification: This is why a downloaded file can be checked for tampering: it carries a fingerprint, and if anyone alters even one character, the fingerprint no longer matches -- not slightly off, but unrecognizably changed. A single corrupted byte is caught at once; a forger cannot nudge a document into matching a seal by small edits. The engine confirmed that a one-letter change scatters about half the fingerprint. The smallest secret edit cannot stay hidden.
wisdom: What is altered in the dark does not stay dark; the small change you hoped no one would notice leaves its mark, and is found out in time. "Be sure your sin will find you out" (Numbers 32:23).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_sha256_avalanche_is_measurable. The sealed proof shows a one-letter change flips ~half the hash bits, so altered files fail their fingerprint check.

title: Life is written in three-letter words with no spaces -- drop one letter and the rest turns to nonsense
verification: The instructions inside every living cell are written in an alphabet of four letters, read three at a time, with no gaps between the words -- only a mark for where to begin and where to stop. Because there are no spaces, dropping or adding a single letter does not spoil one word; it shoves every word after it out of step, and the rest becomes gibberish, usually stopping short. It is like reading THEBIGOLDDOG in threes -- lose the first letter and all that follows is scrambled. The engine confirmed the reading really does march in threes, and refused a passage whose length was not a whole number of three-letter words.
wisdom: Where the text is sacred and tightly set, a single letter added or lost is no small thing; reverence keeps the words exactly as given, neither padding nor trimming. "Every word of God is pure... Add thou not unto his words, lest he reprove thee, and thou be found a liar" (Proverbs 30:5-6).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_reading_frame_is_triplet_code. The sealed proof shows genes are read as non-overlapping triplets from start to stop; a length not divisible by three is refused.

title: A letter swapped mars one word; a letter dropped throws off every word that follows
verification: Not all mistakes weigh the same. In the code of life, replacing one letter with another damages at most a single word -- and often not even that, because the language keeps spare spellings for the same meaning. But removing or inserting a letter is far worse: it knocks the whole sentence out of alignment, so every word after the slip is misread. The engine confirmed both: a swap touches one piece; a drop shifts the entire frame. The size of an error matters less than whether it throws off all that comes after.
wisdom: A fault that stays in its place may be borne, but a small slip that knocks everything after it out of step can ruin a life or a labor; guard most against the little errors that misalign all the rest. "Dead flies cause the ointment of the apothecary to send forth a stinking savour: so doth a little folly him that is in reputation for wisdom and honour" (Ecclesiastes 10:1).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_reading_frame_is_triplet_code. The sealed proof shows a substitution hits one codon while an insertion/deletion shifts the whole reading frame.

title: Keep the master safe; send out a true copy to do the work
verification: The living cell does not hand out its precious master plan to be worn and torn in daily labor. It keeps the original DNA archived and safe, and makes a disposable working copy -- letter for letter the same -- to carry to the workshop where proteins are built. The engine confirmed the copy spells out exactly the same result as the master; a claim that the copy changed the message simply broke. It is the oldest filing wisdom there is: protect the original, work from a faithful copy.
wisdom: What is precious and irreplaceable is not spent in the rough work of every day; a careful copy is made and sent out, while the original is kept whole. So even kings were charged to copy the law by their own hand and keep it near. "And it shall be, when he sitteth upon the throne of his kingdom, that he shall write him a copy of this law in a book" (Deuteronomy 17:18).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_transcription_is_complementary_copy. The sealed proof shows DNA-to-mRNA transcription is a faithful copy spelling the same protein.

title: A true copy in humbler ink still says exactly what the original says
verification: When the cell copies a gene into its working message, it writes in a slightly different ink -- one letter is swapped throughout for a cheaper substitute -- yet the meaning is untouched: the copy spells the very same protein, word for word. The engine confirmed it: change the ink, keep the message. What matters is not the costliness of the page or the pen, but that the words are faithfully the same.
wisdom: Do not mistake the vessel for the treasure; fine paper and plain may carry the very same truth, and the humblest copy of a faithful word is worth more than a gilded lie. The page fades; the message endures. "For all flesh is as grass... but the word of the Lord endureth for ever" (1 Peter 1:24-25).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_transcription_is_complementary_copy. The sealed proof shows mRNA (U for T) spells the identical protein -- the medium changes, the message does not.

title: Nature writes palindromes too -- and that is exactly where the scissors cut
verification: A palindrome is a word that reads the same forwards and backwards -- level, racecar, noon. It turns out the ladder of DNA has its own palindromes, places where the two strands read the same when you flip the molecule end for end. And here is the wonder: those mirror-spots are exactly where the cell's cutting tools take hold and snip -- the very basis of genetic engineering. The engine checked it: GAATTC is such a palindrome, while a near-twin like GAATTG is not, and reads back as something else entirely.
wisdom: There are hidden symmetries woven through creation, words that read the same from either end, and it is often at such places of order that a thing can be taken hold of and worked. The One who is first and last leaves His signature in what reads the same both ways. "I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end, the first and the last" (Revelation 22:13).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_dna_palindrome_is_restriction_site. The sealed proof shows restriction sites (GAATTC, GGATCC, AAGCTT) are reverse-complement palindromes; GAATTG is not.

title: Where a thing is symmetric, there is a place to take hold of it
verification: The tools that cut DNA are themselves built in mirror-image pairs, so they can only grip a stretch that looks the same from both sides -- which is why they fasten precisely onto the palindrome spots and nowhere else. The symmetry is not decoration; it is the handle. The engine confirmed that the cut sites are exactly the balanced, same-both-ways sequences. Order is what makes a thing graspable; where there is hidden balance, there is a way to work with it.
wisdom: A life or a labor that is well-ordered offers a place to take hold; what is balanced and fitly made can be built upon, while the lopsided slips through the hands. God made each thing fitting in its season, and that fitness is a gift to be used. "He hath made every thing beautiful in his time" (Ecclesiastes 3:11).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_dna_palindrome_is_restriction_site. The sealed proof shows restriction enzymes (2-fold symmetric dimers) grip reverse-complement palindromes -- symmetry is the binding site.

title: You can trim the fat from a message, but not the meat
verification: Every tool that shrinks a file -- the ZIP on your computer, the MP3 in your music, the squeezing of a photo -- runs into the same wall. You can strip out every wasted, repeated bit, but beneath the padding lies the real content, and that cannot be made any smaller without throwing some of it away. The engine confirmed the floor exactly, and a claim to squeeze below it simply broke. That is also why a page of pure random letters, or already-scrambled data, will not compress at all -- there is no fat left to trim.
wisdom: Say much in few words, by all means -- cut the padding, the repetition, the showing-off -- but there is a core of truth beneath that you must not cut, for to say less than the matter requires is not brevity but loss. "Till heaven and earth pass, one jot or one tittle shall in no wise pass from the law" (Matthew 5:18).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_entropy_is_compression_floor. The sealed proof shows entropy (H=1.75 bits) is the floor on lossless compression; a claim below it breaks.

title: Give the common thing a short name and the rare thing a long one
verification: Here is the whole secret of saying much in little: spend your shortest words on whatever you use most. Morse code gave the letter E -- the commonest in English -- a single dot, and saved the long signals for rare letters. Every good compressor does the same, handing brief codes to frequent things and longer ones to the unusual. The engine confirmed this is not just clever but optimal -- you cannot do better on average. Fit the effort to how often it is needed.
wisdom: The wise steward spends least on what recurs most and reserves the long labor for the rare occasion; so too in speech, give the everyday its plain short word and save your breath for what truly needs it. "A word fitly spoken is like apples of gold in pictures of silver" (Proverbs 25:11).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_entropy_is_compression_floor. The sealed proof shows short codes for frequent symbols reach the entropy floor (Huffman avg = H exactly).

title: Pluck one string and the chord is already inside it
verification: Strike a single string and you do not hear just one note. Quietly woven into it is a whole ladder of fainter tones -- and the first rungs of that ladder are the octave and the fifth, the very notes that make a chord sound sweet. The scale we sing is not something laid over the sound by custom; it is read straight off the way a string shakes. The engine confirmed it both ways: a string tuned to 220 also rings at 440 (the octave) and 660 (a fifth higher), and a false overtone was refused.
wisdom: There is more hidden in a simple thing than first meets the ear; one plain string, rightly struck, already holds a harmony it took men ages to name. Strike thanks, and find the chord was always there. "Praise the LORD with harp: sing unto him with the psaltery and an instrument of ten strings. Sing unto him a new song; play skilfully with a loud noise" (Psalm 33:2-3).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_harmonics_are_intervals_two_witnesses. The sealed proof shows the overtone series (440, 660 from 220) gives the octave and fifth -- the scale read off physics.

title: Music was discovered, not invented -- the scale only names what the string already sings
verification: Why do certain notes sound lovely together and others clash? Not by fashion, but because the pleasing pairs are the simple ones: two notes ring sweetly when their pitches stand in plain whole-number step -- two-to-one for the octave, three-to-two for the fifth. Those same ratios show up in the bare physics of a vibrating string and in the named intervals of music alike. The engine confirmed the agreement in both. The sweetness was built into the world before any musician found it; we did not compose the harmony, only learn to hear it.
wisdom: The deepest beauty is not our invention but our inheritance; the order was singing in creation long before we wrote it down, and to make music is to join a song already begun. "The heavens declare the glory of God... There is no speech nor language, where their voice is not heard" (Psalm 19:1,3).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_harmonics_are_intervals_two_witnesses. The sealed proof confirms the same small-integer ratios as both physics (overtones) and music (named intervals).

title: Skip-count by a step that shares no factor, and you touch every place before coming home
verification: Stand at the top of a clock face and hop around it by sevens -- seven, then two, then nine -- and you will land on every single hour once before you arrive back at the top. But hop by threes and you only ever touch four spots before circling back, missing the rest. The difference is whether your step and the twelve share a common factor: if they share none, you reach everything; if they share one, you close early in a smaller ring. The engine confirmed it -- by sevens you visit all twelve, by threes only four.
wisdom: To touch every part of a thing, sometimes you must step through it by a measure that does not divide it neatly; the journey that reaches all and returns home is the one whose stride is foreign to the circle. Round and round it goes, and comes again to its place. "The wind whirleth about continually, and the wind returneth again according to his circuits" (Ecclesiastes 1:6).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_circle_of_fifths_is_coprimality. The sealed proof shows a step coprime to n visits all n positions (7 mod 12 hits all; 3 mod 12 closes after 4).

title: Climb by fifths and you pass through all twelve keys before you come home
verification: Here is a quiet wonder of the keyboard: start on any note and keep stepping up by a perfect fifth, and you will pass through all twelve keys of music, each exactly once, and arrive right back where you began -- the famous circle of fifths. It forms a closed circle only because seven (the size of that step) and twelve (the notes in an octave) have no common measure. Step instead by a size that shares a factor, and the path shuts early without visiting them all. The engine confirmed the full round closes at the twelfth step, and a too-early closing was refused. The same hidden fact that orders locks and keys, now heard as harmony.
wisdom: The harmony of a whole depends on parts that, though they seem at odds, share no smallness that would cut the journey short; the music comes full circle because of a fitness woven beneath the notes. "And even things without life giving sound, whether pipe or harp, except they give a distinction in the sounds, how shall it be known what is piped or harped?" (1 Corinthians 14:7).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_circle_of_fifths_is_coprimality. The sealed proof shows the fifth (7 semitones) generates all 12 pitch classes because gcd(7,12)=1.

title: Putting things in order is not free -- you buy it one question at a time
verification: To sort a handful of things into order, you compare two at a time -- this before that? -- and each such question can at best cut in half the number of arrangements still possible. Four items can be jumbled twenty-four different ways, and four yes-or-no questions can only tell apart sixteen, so four is not enough; you need at least five. The engine confirmed the exact toll for three, four, and five items, and refused the claim that four would do for four. The cost of order is set by how many ways things could be in disorder -- not by how clever the sorter is.
wisdom: Order is bought, not found; before you set a thing right you do well to reckon what it will cost in patient labor, for the price is fixed by the size of the tangle and no cleverness escapes it. "For which of you, intending to build a tower, sitteth not down first, and counteth the cost?" (Luke 14:28).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_sorting_is_information_bound. The sealed proof shows a comparison sort of n items needs at least log2(n!) comparisons (5 for 4 items; 4 is too few).

title: The only way to skip the sorting is to already know where each thing goes
verification: If you must discover the order by comparing pairs, there is no escaping the toll -- so many questions and no fewer. But there is one way around it: if you already know something about the items -- that they are numbered one to a hundred, say -- you can drop each straight into its labeled bin without comparing anything, the way a postman sorts by zip code. The engine confirmed the comparison price is unavoidable; the only shortcut is to use what you knew in advance. Foreknowledge, not cleverness, is what spares the labor.
wisdom: When the work is heavy, do not merely strain harder; wisdom that knows the matter beforehand will direct the effort and save the back. Knowing where things belong is worth more than brute sorting. "If the iron be blunt... then must he put to more strength: but wisdom is profitable to direct" (Ecclesiastes 10:10).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_sorting_is_information_bound. The sealed proof shows the log2(n!) bound is for COMPARISON sorts; you beat it only by not comparing (counting/radix sort uses the values).

title: The ear multiplies, but the page adds -- and that is why a guitar frets crowd together
verification: Climb one octave and the pitch does not go up by some fixed amount -- it DOUBLES. Climb another and it doubles again. Yet on the page music counts intervals by plain addition: a fifth and a fourth stacked together make exactly an octave. How? Because doubling-upon-doubling, when written as musical distance, turns into simple sums (a fifth is 700 little units, a fourth 500, and 700 plus 500 is 1200, one octave). It is why guitar frets sit closer and closer as you climb -- each takes the same FRACTION of the string that is left, not the same length. The engine confirmed the sums, and refused a wrong one.
wisdom: What the senses receive by multiplying, a gracious order lets us count by adding, so that the soaring climb can be walked in even steps. Step by step the faithful ascend, each stage built on the last. "They go from strength to strength, every one of them in Zion appeareth before God" (Psalm 84:7).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_cents_are_logarithmic. The sealed proof shows pitch ratios multiply while cents add (fifth 700 + fourth 500 = octave 1200).

title: Before you measure a thing, learn whether it grows by adding or by doubling
verification: Some things grow by steady addition -- a row of fence posts, a stack of coins set down one by one. Others grow by multiplying, folding over on themselves: a sum at interest, a population, the pitch of a rising note that doubles every octave. Measure a doubling thing with an adding ruler and you will badly misjudge it. Musicians learned to lay a multiplying world onto an evenly-marked page, and the engine confirmed the ruler works exactly. The first task of honest measuring is to ask which kind of growth you are facing.
wisdom: Weigh a matter by its true nature, not by a convenient yardstick; what compounds quietly -- a debt, a habit, a small daily faithfulness -- outruns what merely adds, for good or ill. "A just weight and balance are the LORD's: all the weights of the bag are his work" (Proverbs 16:11).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_cents_are_logarithmic. The sealed proof shows a multiplying quantity (pitch) is measured on a logarithmic (adding) scale -- ratios multiply, cents add.

title: Every weight carries an invisible boundary, fixed by how much there is and nothing else
verification: Every lump of matter in the universe -- a star, a stone, you -- has a hidden boundary around it called its horizon: crush the thing inside that radius and not even light could climb back out. The astonishing part is how simple the rule is. The size of that boundary depends on one thing only: how much mass there is. Double the mass and you exactly double the boundary, and the very same constant of nature governs the Sun and a marble alike. The engine confirmed the law is a true length and that the horizon grows in exact step with the mass.
wisdom: The Maker set a fixed bound to every created thing, great and small under one law; what governs a matter is its true substance, not its show. He who decreed the limit of the sea decreed the limit of the stars. "When he gave to the sea his decree, that the waters should not pass his commandment" (Proverbs 8:29).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_schwarzschild_horizon_set_by_mass. The sealed proof shows the Schwarzschild radius r=2GM/c^2 is a length set proportionally by mass alone.

title: Crush the whole Earth to the size of a marble and not even light could leave it
verification: How close is an ordinary thing to becoming a black hole? Almost unimaginably far. For the entire Earth to vanish behind its own horizon, you would have to squeeze it down to under a centimeter across -- a marble. The mighty Sun would have to be crushed to a ball only about three kilometers wide. The engine confirmed these exact sizes (Earth, nine millimeters; Sun, near three kilometers). That such collapse is so absurdly far from everyday things is why the ground holds steady beneath your feet.
wisdom: How small the vast can be made in the hand that holds it, and how gently we are kept from the abyss that the bare numbers allow; mercy is the wide margin we never notice. "Behold, the nations are as a drop of a bucket... he taketh up the isles as a very little thing" (Isaiah 40:15).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_schwarzschild_horizon_set_by_mass. The sealed proof gives the Schwarzschild radii: Earth 8.87 mm, Sun ~2954 m.

title: The siren that rises and falls is the same law that colors the stars
verification: You have heard an ambulance climb in pitch as it races toward you and drop as it speeds away. The very same thing happens to light: a star rushing toward us is shifted bluer, one fleeing away is shifted redder, and the size of the shift tells its speed. This is how astronomers weigh the motion of the heavens -- and how they learned the distant galaxies are racing away, their light reddened. The engine confirmed the exact numbers for light, and even caught that fast sources shift a touch MORE than the old simple rule said, the extra coming from time itself stretching.
wisdom: The lights of heaven were set not only to shine but to speak -- to mark the seasons and tell the watching their motions; the same God who tuned a siren tuned the stars. "And God said, Let there be lights in the firmament of the heaven... and let them be for signs, and for seasons" (Genesis 1:14).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas located_relativistic_doppler_reciprocal. The sealed proof confirms the relativistic Doppler factor: approaching light blueshifts, receding redshifts, with an extra time-dilation factor.

title: The signal is unchanged; only your vantage bends it -- and the two bendings undo each other
verification: Here is a quiet marvel of perspective. The very same beam of light, from the very same source, reads twice as fast to someone racing toward it and half as fast to someone racing away -- and those two numbers, doubled and halved, multiply back to exactly one. The light never changed; where you stand bent your reading of it, and the opposite vantages cancel out perfectly. The engine confirmed the pair at three-fifths of light speed: times two coming, times one-half going, product exactly one.
wisdom: Two honest people may read the same thing oppositely because they stand in opposite places; the matter itself is unbent, and humility remembers that my vantage colors my view. The Lord alone reads without distortion. "For the LORD seeth not as man seeth; for man looketh on the outward appearance, but the LORD looketh on the heart" (1 Samuel 16:7).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas located_relativistic_doppler_reciprocal. The sealed proof shows the approach factor (x2) and recede factor (x1/2) multiply to exactly 1 at b=3/5.

title: At the bottom of every machine there are only two words: yes and no
verification: Strip a computer down to its foundation and you find no magic -- only two answers, on and off, one and zero, repeated past counting. And even these obey plain arithmetic: to require BOTH of two things true is simply to multiply (one times one is one; anything times zero is zero), and to ask whether they DIFFER is to add and drop the overflow. Every gate in the machine is a little sum or product. The engine confirmed the arithmetic and the logic agree exactly, and refused the case where they were confused. The whole tower stands on answers that are only ever one or the other.
wisdom: Great things are built on plain choices faithfully made; before the towering machine, before the tangled life, there stands a simple yes or no set before you. "I have set before thee life and death, blessing and cursing: therefore choose life" (Deuteronomy 30:19).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_boolean_is_arithmetic_gf2. The sealed proof shows Boolean logic is arithmetic in GF(2): AND=multiply, XOR=add mod 2, NOT=1-x.

title: The same secret that hides a message will, applied again, bring it back
verification: There is a beautifully simple way to scramble a message: blend it, letter by letter, with a secret. The wonder is the undoing -- blend it with the very SAME secret a second time, and the original returns untouched, because each piece combined with itself cancels to nothing and falls away. The engine confirmed this exactly: do the operation twice with the same key and you are back where you began. It is the heart of the simplest cipher there is, and the same trick guards data against errors. One act both conceals and restores.
wisdom: The hand that shuts a door is the hand that can open it; what binds may also loose, and the authority that hides a thing is the authority that brings it to light. Trust the key, not the lock. "And the key of the house of David will I lay upon his shoulder; so he shall open, and none shall shut; and he shall shut, and none shall open" (Isaiah 22:22).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_boolean_is_arithmetic_gf2. The sealed proof shows XOR is its own inverse in GF(2): x XOR k XOR k = x (k XOR k = 0).

title: Say a thing three times, and one garble cannot overturn it
verification: This is how your photos survive a scratched disc, how a QR code reads through a coffee stain, how a whisper from a distant spacecraft arrives intact through the static. The trick is repetition with room to spare: send a message threefold, and if noise corrupts one copy, the true version still stands closer than the false, so the right answer can be recovered. The engine confirmed it -- a single flipped bit still decodes correctly. (Honest limit: threefold guards against ONE error; let TWO slip in and even this can be fooled, so deeper protection needs more spread.)
wisdom: Truth confirmed by more than one mouth withstands a single corruption; what is established by several witnesses is not undone by one error or one liar. "But if he will not hear thee, then take with thee one or two more, that in the mouth of two or three witnesses every word may be established" (Matthew 18:16).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_error_correction_minimum_distance. The sealed proof shows threefold repetition (distance 3) corrects one error: floor((d-1)/2)=1.

title: Keep your yes and your no far enough apart that no slip can blur them
verification: Reliable messages are not just repeated -- they are spread far apart, so that a little noise can never carry one meaning across into another. The further apart two valid signals sit, the more corruption it takes to mistake one for the other; the single number measuring that gap decides how many errors can be undone. The engine confirmed the rule exactly: widen the distance and you can correct more mistakes. Leave a clear margin between what you mean and what you do not, and confusion finds no foothold.
wisdom: Make a plain difference between your meanings, and between good and evil, light and darkness; where the line is kept wide the deceiver cannot blur it, but a narrow margin invites the lie to pass for truth. "Woe unto them that call evil good, and good evil; that put darkness for light, and light for darkness" (Isaiah 5:20).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_error_correction_minimum_distance. The sealed proof shows the minimum Hamming distance d governs capacity: more distance corrects more errors (t=floor((d-1)/2)).

title: Wet ground does not prove it rained
verification: Here is a trap that has fooled clever people for ages. Rain makes the ground wet; the ground IS wet; so -- it rained? It feels airtight, yet it is false: the sprinkler, a burst pipe, the morning dew could have done it. An effect rarely points back to one cause only. The engine drew the exact line: the sound step (if rain, then wet; it rained; so it is wet) always holds, while its mirror-image (it is wet, so it rained) is a counterfeit and was rejected. The conclusion that merely COULD follow is not the conclusion that MUST.
wisdom: Do not be carried off by an argument just because it sounds forceful; the prudent weigh whether the conclusion truly must follow, or only happens to fit. Many a plausible tale has another explanation. "The simple believeth every word: but the prudent man looketh well to his going" (Proverbs 14:15).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas located_inference_rules_verified. The sealed proof confirms modus ponens is valid while affirming the consequent (effect implies a particular cause) is a fallacy and breaks.

title: If a claim were true, its promise would come to pass -- when it fails, the claim is false
verification: There is an honest way to test any belief: ask what it must lead to if it is true, then look. If a remedy truly cured, the sick would mend; if they do not, the remedy is empty. If a forecast were right, the sign would appear; when it does not, the forecast was wrong. This is the backbone of all real testing -- a guess is overturned the moment its promised result fails to arrive. The engine confirmed this step always holds. To follow where a claim leads, and find nothing there, is to know it was not true.
wisdom: Weigh the prophet and the promise by what actually comes; a word that does not bear out the thing it foretold has condemned itself, however confidently it was spoken. "When a prophet speaketh in the name of the LORD, if the thing follow not, nor come to pass... the prophet hath spoken it presumptuously" (Deuteronomy 18:22).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas located_inference_rules_verified. The sealed proof confirms modus tollens (a failed prediction refutes the hypothesis) is a valid rule of inference.

title: What speed steals from length it pays back in time -- the trade is exact
verification: Race fast enough and strange things happen: your length shrinks and your clock slows. The marvel is that these are not two separate quirks but one fact seen from two sides, and they balance perfectly. At three-fifths the speed of light a thing runs to four-fifths its length while its clock runs five-fourths as slow -- and four-fifths times five-fourths is exactly one. A speeding particle from space reaches the ground precisely because the distance it sees shrunk by the same factor our clocks say its time stretched. The engine confirmed the two factors are exact reciprocals, and broke a false triangle that would have unbalanced them.
wisdom: There is a deep economy in things: what is taken in one measure is restored in another, and the honest accounting always comes back to one. So even loss, rightly borne, is not pure loss. "For whosoever will save his life shall lose it: and whosoever will lose his life for my sake shall find it" (Matthew 16:25).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_length_contraction_is_reciprocal_of_dilation. The sealed proof shows length contraction (x4/5) and time dilation (x5/4) are exact reciprocals: product = 1.

title: Two honest accounts of one event can differ and still both be right
verification: A particle streaking down from the sky and the watchers on the ground tell the story differently. The particle would say the journey was short; the ground says its clock ran slow. Both are telling the truth -- and the two descriptions reconcile exactly, agreeing that it reaches the surface. Neither account is the whole, yet neither is false; they are one event measured from different motions. The engine confirmed the numbers fit together perfectly. Before you call a differing witness a liar, ask whether he simply stood somewhere else.
wisdom: Each of us sees in part, from where we stand, and two faithful witnesses may frame the same truth in unlike words; humility holds both until the fuller view reconciles them. "For now we see through a glass, darkly... now I know in part; but then shall I know even as also I am known" (1 Corinthians 13:12).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_length_contraction_is_reciprocal_of_dilation. The sealed proof shows the moving and resting frames give reciprocal factors that reconcile to the same outcome.

title: What a thing is at rest and what it gains in motion are two sides of one triangle
verification: A moving object carries its energy in two parts: the energy it holds simply by EXISTING, and the energy it holds from MOVING. The surprise is how they add -- not straight together, but the oldest way a carpenter knows, as the two short sides of a right triangle, with the total as the long side. In the cleanest case the parts are three and four and the whole is five, exactly the carpenter triangle. So rest-energy and motion-energy are not rivals; they are the two legs of one figure, and the total is always longer than either. The engine confirmed the triangle and refused a false total.
wisdom: What we are in stillness and what we become in striving are not at war; joined rightly, they make one fuller life, and the whole is greater than either alone. "Two are better than one; because they have a good reward for their labour" (Ecclesiastes 4:9).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_energy_momentum_is_pythagoras. The sealed proof shows E^2=(pc)^2+(mc^2)^2 -- rest energy and momentum are legs of a right triangle (3-4-5).

title: The strangest physics obeys the same rule a carpenter uses -- the deep laws are few
verification: You might expect the energy of something racing near the speed of light to need some exotic new mathematics. Instead it obeys the plain right-triangle rule that a carpenter uses to square a corner -- the square on the long side equals the sum of the squares on the other two. The same humble theorem governs a roof, a staircase, and the energy of a speeding particle. The engine confirmed it exactly. That so much of the deep world runs on so few, so simple rules is a quiet wonder -- as if one hand wrote them all.
wisdom: The order beneath all things is not a chaos of separate laws but a deep and simple unity; the more we learn, the fewer and plainer the foundations prove to be -- pointing back to One. "Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God is one LORD" (Deuteronomy 6:4).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_energy_momentum_is_pythagoras. The sealed proof shows relativistic energy obeys the Pythagorean theorem -- the same rule used to square a corner.

title: When many things must all go right, their chances multiply -- and shrink fast
verification: Each step of a plan may look likely on its own, yet if several unrelated things must ALL come off, you do not add their odds -- you multiply them, and the total shrinks quickly. Two coin-flips both landing heads is not one-in-two but one-in-four. Even nine-in-ten odds, demanded five times over, fall to about three-in-five. The engine confirmed that independent chances combine by multiplying. This is why a chain with many fragile links so often parts, and why the wise keep their plans few-stepped or well-guarded.
wisdom: Boast not of a plan that needs a dozen things to fall out just so; the more hinges, the more likely one gives way, and tomorrow is not yours to command. "Go to now, ye that say, To day or to morrow we will go into such a city... whereas ye know not what shall be on the morrow" (James 4:13-14).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_independence_multiplies_information_adds. The sealed proof shows independent probabilities multiply (0.5x0.5=0.25).

title: Chance shrinks by multiplying; knowledge grows by adding
verification: Here are two opposite climbs governed by one rule. The odds of many things all happening multiply, sliding quietly toward zero -- but the KNOWLEDGE those things bring simply adds: learn one true fact, then another unrelated one, and you have twice the understanding, not a fraction of it. Two coin-flips are a one-in-four chance, yet they tell you two full pieces of news. The engine confirmed both -- the multiply in chance, the add in knowledge -- joined by the same quiet mathematics. So let chance discourage you less, and let learning compound.
wisdom: Gather understanding the way it is given -- a little at a time, each true thing laid upon the last -- and over years the small additions become great store, though no single day felt like much. "Line upon line, line upon line; here a little, and there a little" (Isaiah 28:10).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_independence_multiplies_information_adds. The sealed proof shows probability multiplies while information adds, joined by the logarithm (-log2(0.25)=2=1+1).

title: A machine counts on a wheel: one step past the top lands at the bottom
verification: The numbers inside a computer do not march off to infinity; they ride a wheel, like a car odometer that rolls from all-nines back to all-zeros, or a clock where one hour past twelve is one. So in a small machine the highest count plus one quietly becomes the lowest -- add a little and you suddenly grow smaller, which looks like a paradox but is only a circle seen edge-on. The engine confirmed the wheel exactly: step one past the top of an eight-bit counter and you land at the very bottom. Even subtracting is just walking the wheel the other way round.
wisdom: Nothing in this world runs on forever in a straight line; the seasons, the waters, the works of men all come round again to where they began. What seems to vanish at the top reappears at the foot. "All the rivers run into the sea... unto the place from whence the rivers come, thither they return again" (Ecclesiastes 1:7).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas located_twos_complement_is_mod_2n. The sealed proof shows fixed-width integers are arithmetic mod 2^n: the top wraps to the bottom (127+1 = -128).

title: Push a thing past its limit and it does not grow -- it flips to the opposite
verification: A computer counter does not warn you when you exceed it. Push one step past its highest value and it does not halt or cry out -- it silently rolls to its lowest, so a large positive total can flip, unnoticed, into a negative. This quiet wrap is behind many a costly bug, from miscounted money to dates that suddenly read long ago. The engine confirmed it: the most a small counter can hold, plus one, becomes the least. Past a true limit, MORE turns into its opposite, and the danger is that no bell rings.
wisdom: The one who strains ever higher without regard for the bound set him does not rise forever; at the peak of his climbing waits the fall, and the exalted is brought low when he least expects it. "For whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased; and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted" (Luke 14:11).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas located_twos_complement_is_mod_2n. The sealed proof shows signed overflow: the maximum plus one wraps to the minimum (127+1 = -128 mod 256).

title: Count the eggs by threes and fives, and the leftovers will tell you how many
verification: Here is a riddle older than a thousand years. A basket of eggs: counted out by threes, two remain; by fives, three remain; by sevens, two remain. How many eggs? You need not have watched the counting -- the leftovers alone pin the answer to twenty-three (and then again at one-twenty-eight, and so on, each a full hundred-and-five apart). The number is known completely by the remnants it leaves against each measure. The engine confirmed it, and refused a wrong leftover. (The honest catch: the counts you use must share no common factor, or the answer blurs.)
wisdom: Even the scraps and remainders are not wasted; rightly gathered, the little that is left over can tell the whole story, for nothing falls to the ground unaccounted. "And they did all eat, and were filled: and they took up of the fragments that remained twelve baskets full" (Matthew 14:20).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_chinese_remainder_is_residue_reconstruction. The sealed proof shows a number is uniquely fixed by its remainders against coprime measures (23 = (2,3,2) mod (3,5,7), unique mod 105).

title: A thing tried against several honest measures is known completely by how it answers each
verification: Break a number into how it stands against several yardsticks -- what it leaves over against this one, against that one -- and if the yardsticks share nothing in common, the whole number can be rebuilt from those answers without the slightest loss. The pieces, rightly chosen, remember the whole. The engine confirmed the reconstruction is exact and unique. So a matter measured honestly from enough independent angles is not partly known but wholly known -- provided the measures are truly independent, and not secretly the same.
wisdom: What is tested from every honest side is fully known; God does not judge by one glance but searches a life through and through, and by its answer to each trial reads the whole. "I the LORD search the heart, I try the reins, even to give every man according to his ways" (Jeremiah 17:10).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_chinese_remainder_is_residue_reconstruction. The sealed proof shows a number is uniquely reconstructable from its residues against pairwise-coprime moduli.

title: A perfect number is exactly the sum of its own parts: six is one plus two plus three
verification: The ancients called a number perfect when it equals the sum of all the smaller numbers that divide it evenly. Six is the first: its parts are one, two, and three, and one plus two plus three is six exactly -- owing nothing, hoarding nothing. The next is twenty-eight (one plus two plus four plus seven plus fourteen), then four hundred ninety-six. The engine confirmed these and refused a pretender like twelve. (An honest mystery remains: every perfect number ever found is even; whether a single ODD one exists, no one in all of history has been able to settle.)
wisdom: To be whole is to render exactly what is due to each part -- no debt unpaid, no surplus clutched back; and it is no accident the first such number is six, the measure of the days in which all was made and called good. "And God saw every thing that he had made, and, behold, it was very good. And the evening and the morning were the sixth day" (Genesis 1:31).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_euclid_euler_perfect_numbers. The sealed proof confirms 6, 28, 496 are perfect (sum of their divisors); whether an odd perfect number exists is an open problem.

title: Perfection is never cheap -- it is rare, and comes only paired with what is rare
verification: You might think perfect numbers would grow common as you count higher. They do the opposite: they are vanishingly rare. In all of mathematical history only about fifty are known, and each one appears only when a certain rare kind of prime is found -- the two discoveries are a single hunt, so a new perfect number waits on a new rare prime. The engine confirmed the exact pairing, and showed why an ordinary prime will not do. Perfection is not mass-produced; it arrives seldom, and always at the price of something else rare.
wisdom: The truly whole and the truly precious are not found lying about cheap; they are sought long, come rarely, and are worth the selling of all else to obtain. "The kingdom of heaven is like unto a merchant man, seeking goodly pearls: Who, when he had found one pearl of great price, went and sold all that he had, and bought it" (Matthew 13:45-46).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas connection_euclid_euler_perfect_numbers. The sealed proof shows every even perfect number pairs with a rare Mersenne prime (2^(p-1)*(2^p-1)); only ~51 are known.

title: A pattern, however beautiful, is not a proof
verification: Fermat, one of the greatest minds in the history of numbers, noticed that a certain string of numbers came out prime -- three, five, seventeen, two hundred fifty-seven, sixty-five thousand five hundred thirty-seven -- and concluded that all of them, forever, would be prime. It was a lovely pattern. It was also wrong: the very next one, over four billion, quietly factors into 641 times 6,700,417. Nearly a century passed before Euler simply checked and found the crack. The engine recomputed it too -- five confirmed prime, the sixth refused. A run of examples, however elegant, never proves the rule.
wisdom: Do not be swept away by how neat a thing looks; the eye loves a tidy pattern, but truth is found by testing, not by admiring. Look past the surface before you trust it. "Judge not according to the appearance, but judge righteous judgment" (John 7:24).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas located_fermat_numbers_verified. The sealed proof confirms the first five Fermat numbers prime and refuses the sixth (composite), reproducing Euler 1732.

title: Check the claim, however great the one who made it
verification: Here is the whole discipline of honest verification in one story. A brilliant man made a confident claim; it was false; and it stood unchallenged for decades simply because of who had said it. The fix was not a cleverer argument but a plain recomputation -- someone who refused to take it on trust, and checked. The engine does the same: it recomputed the numbers itself and rejected the false one, caring nothing for the great name attached. Greatness is no guard against a wrong conclusion, and reputation is no substitute for proof.
wisdom: Weigh every claim by the truth and not by the rank of the one who speaks it; the humble fact outranks the famous opinion, for the Judge of all shows no favoritism. "Of a truth I perceive that God is no respecter of persons" (Acts 10:34).

pre_run.summary: Human-facing translation of Atlas located_fermat_numbers_verified. The sealed proof recomputes primality (not by authority) and refuses Fermat famous false claim that F_5 is prime.

title: You cannot make every word short -- brevity is a purse with a fixed bottom
verification: When you build a set of code-names so that none is mistaken for the start of another, there is a strict budget: every short name you grant spends a big slice of what is left. The very shortest name uses up fully HALF the room at once. So you simply cannot make every word short -- try to give three things the shortest possible name and the ledger demands more than the whole, which is impossible. The engine confirmed the budget exactly, and refused an over-spent set. The plain lesson: brevity, like money, runs out.
wisdom: Treat your short words, your easy slots, your front-row places as the limited store they are; the wise keep something in reserve, while the fool spends his whole purse on the first few and has nothing left for the rest. "There is treasure to be desired and oil in the dwelling of the wise; but a foolish man spendeth it up" (Proverbs 21:20).
