title: The Galton board makes the bell curve out of falling beads -- chance piles into a predictable shape
situation: Drop hundreds of beads through a triangle of evenly spaced pegs, each bead bouncing left or right by chance at every row, and they do not scatter at random -- they pile up into the smooth, symmetric BELL CURVE, every time. The reason is counting: the number of paths leading to each slot is a binomial coefficient. For four rows the slots receive 1, 4, 6, 4, 1 paths -- the center has the most (C(4,2)=6), the edges the fewest, and the pattern is symmetric (C(4,1)=C(4,3)=4). The total number of paths is 2 to the n (here 2^4 = 16). The average bead lands at the center (mean = n times p = 2) with a definite spread (variance = n p (1-p) = 1). As the rows increase, this binomial pile smooths into the normal curve -- the Central Limit Theorem made of wood and beads. The board proves that the sum of many small, independent chances is not chaos but a fixed, knowable shape.
use: Whenever many small independent effects add up -- measurement errors, heights in a population, the noise on a signal, the scatter of repeated trials -- expect the bell curve, and expect it to tighten and smooth as more effects combine. The Galton board is the visible proof; its reliable bell is among the strongest demonstrations that the laws of chance are laws, not guesses. HONEST NOTE: the verified facts here are the binomial path-counts, the mean, and the variance; the smoothing of the binomial into the exact normal curve as the rows grow is the Central Limit Theorem, named here -- itself a proven theorem.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 6/6. The slot path-counts are binomial coefficients: C(4,2)=6 (center, most paths), C(4,1)=4, symmetric C(4,1)=C(4,3), total paths C(4,0..4)=2^4=16. The mean lands at center, n p = 4 times 1/2 = 2; the variance is n p (1-p) = 1. A control C(4,2)=5 MISMATCHED. The bell shape is the path-counting made visible.

domain_results.note: the center slot receives the most paths, C(4,2)=6 -- the peak of the bell
the next slots receive C(4,1)=4
the pile is symmetric: C(4,1)=C(4,3)
the total number of paths is 2^n = 16
the average bead lands at the center: mean = n p = 2
the spread is fixed: variance = n p (1-p) = 1

wisdom: What looks like blind chance is held in a steady hand and falls into an appointed shape. 'The lot is cast into the lap; but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD' (Proverbs 16:33). Each bead bounces freely, yet the whole pile is ordered -- a small parable that randomness and providence are not enemies.

title: Even pure chance piles up into a steady shape -- the bell curve
verification: Drop a thousand beads through a board of pegs, each bouncing this way or that at random, and they do not make a mess -- they build the same gentle hill every time, tall in the middle and low at the edges. It happens because there are simply more ways to land near the middle than at the extremes. So it is with many small random things added together: errors, heights, the scatter of repeated tries -- they settle into the bell curve. Chance, in the large, keeps a shape. The engine confirmed the path-counting behind it.
wisdom: Are not two sparrows sold for a farthing? ...But the very hairs of your head are all numbered (Matthew 10:29-30). What seems most random is yet counted and known; the falling bead and the falling sparrow are both within a reckoning.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. The Galton board's bell shape is the binomial path-counts made visible; many small chances sum to the normal curve (CLT).

title: The hanging chain finds the perfect arch by itself -- the catenary is cosh, and upside down it carries pure compression
situation: Hold a chain or rope loosely by its two ends and let it hang. The curve it settles into is not a parabola, as Galileo first guessed, but the CATENARY -- the hyperbolic cosine, cosh -- the shape that balances the chain's weight against its tension at every point. Cosh and its partner sinh both solve the equation y'' = y (curvature proportional to height); they obey the hyperbolic identity cosh^2 - sinh^2 = 1 (the unit hyperbola, twin of the circle's cos^2 + sin^2 = 1); the derivative of cosh is sinh; and cosh is built from exponentials, (e^x + e^-x)/2, with its lowest point cosh(0) = 1. Now turn that hanging shape UPSIDE DOWN and you have the ideal masonry arch: inverted, the chain's pure tension becomes pure compression in the stone, with no bending anywhere -- the strongest arch there is. This is why builders from antiquity to Gaudi designed arches and domes by hanging chains and weighted strings and flipping the result. The hanging chain is an analog computer that solves a hard equation of equilibrium simply by obeying gravity. The chain works because the mathematics is true.
use: To find the strongest arch or dome for a given span, hang a chain (or model it as a*cosh(x/a)) and invert it -- the inverted catenary carries the load in pure compression, which stone and brick bear best. More broadly: a physical system left to settle often solves its governing equation for free, so read the answer off the settled shape. HONEST NOTE: the hanging-chain shape is exact for an arch whose own weight is the load; arches carrying other loads use a related weighted curve.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 6/6. cosh and sinh both solve y'' = y (a control, sin x, MISMATCHED). The hyperbolic identity cosh^2 - sinh^2 = 1 holds (the unit-hyperbola twin of cos^2+sin^2=1); d/dx cosh = sinh; cosh = (e^x + e^-x)/2; cosh(0) = 1 (the chain's lowest point). Inverted, this curve is the pure-compression arch.

domain_results.note: cosh solves y''=y -- the hanging-chain balance
sinh is its partner solution
cosh^2 - sinh^2 = 1: the unit hyperbola, twin of the circle
d/dx cosh = sinh
cosh = (e^x + e^-x)/2 -- built from exponentials
cosh(0) = 1, the lowest point of the chain

wisdom: The surest strength is in the rightly-set stone that bears its load without breaking. 'Behold, I lay in Zion for a foundation a stone, a tried stone, a precious corner stone, a sure foundation' (Isaiah 28:16). The inverted chain gives the arch that stands in pure compression -- a small image of a load borne perfectly, without strain.

title: Hang a chain, turn it upside down, and you have the strongest arch there is
verification: It is one of the loveliest tricks in all of building: to find the perfect shape for a stone arch, you need not calculate -- you hang a chain from two points and copy its curve upside down. A hanging chain settles into exactly the shape (the catenary) that, flipped over, lets stone press on stone with no bending and no breaking -- pure squeeze, which is what stone loves best. Gaudi raised whole cathedrals this way. The chain solves the hard problem for free, just by hanging. The engine confirmed the mathematics the chain quietly obeys.
wisdom: Whosoever shall humble himself shall be exalted (Matthew 23:12). The chain hangs lowest and slack, yet inverted it stands as the strongest of arches -- the low way made, in the end, the lifted and unbreakable one.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. A hanging chain forms a catenary (cosh); inverted it is the pure-compression arch -- the tool solves the equilibrium equation by hanging.

title: The nomogram solves an equation with a straightedge -- parallel resistors, lenses, and springs all add by reciprocals
situation: A nomogram (alignment chart) is a piece of paper that computes: three marked scales arranged so that laying a straightedge across two known values crosses the third scale at the answer -- no arithmetic, just a ruler. Its most useful form encodes RECIPROCAL ADDITION, 1/R = 1/a + 1/b, the law that joins many things in nature. Two resistors in parallel, 3 ohms beside 6 ohms, give not 9 but 2 (because 1/(1/3 + 1/6) = 2); two equal ones always halve (4 with 4 gives 2); 2 with 3 gives 6/5. The same law sets a thin lens (1/f = 1/u + 1/v) and springs in series, and it is the harmonic relationship behind musical means. In general the combination is a*b/(a+b) -- the product over the sum, the reciprocal of the sum of reciprocals. The nomogram makes this law a straightedge's width of work, and that it gives the right resistor or the right lens every time is the proof the law holds.
use: Whenever two paths share a flow -- two resistors, two pipes, two lenses, two springs in series, two workers on one job -- combine them by reciprocals: 1/total = 1/a + 1/b, or total = a*b/(a+b). The combined value is always LESS than the smaller part, which is why two 1-ohm resistors in parallel make half an ohm. A nomogram (or this formula) gives the answer directly, and its reliable working is the demonstration of the law.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 4/4. Reciprocal addition: 3 || 6 = 1/(1/3+1/6) = 2 (a control claiming 9, the series value, MISMATCHED); 2 || 3 = 6/5; 4 || 4 = 2 (equals always halve). The general identity 1/(1/a+1/b) = a*b/(a+b) holds. The same law governs the thin lens (1/f=1/u+1/v) and springs in series.

domain_results.note: 3 and 6 in parallel make 2 -- not the series sum 9
2 and 3 in parallel make 6/5
the general law: 1/(1/a+1/b) = a*b/(a+b), product over sum
two equal parts in parallel always halve: 4 || 4 = 2

wisdom: A load shared between two is lighter on each than it was on one. 'Bear ye one another's burdens, and so fulfil the law of Christ' (Galatians 6:2). Reciprocal addition is that kindness in numbers -- two joined to carry a flow leave each with less than it bore alone.

title: Two paths sharing a load each carry less than one alone -- that is reciprocal addition
verification: Here is a small surprise that runs all through nature: set two equal resistors, or two pipes, or two helpers side by side on the same task, and together they handle more, so each faces less -- the combined 'resistance' falls BELOW either one alone. Two equal 4s make 2, not 8. The rule is to add the reciprocals: one over the total equals one over this plus one over that. Engineers read it straight off a nomogram, a ruler laid across three scales. Lenses, springs, and shared work all keep the same law. The engine confirmed it.
wisdom: Take my yoke upon you...for my yoke is easy, and my burden is light (Matthew 11:30). A burden truly shared grows lighter on each shoulder -- the arithmetic of mercy, written even into resistors and springs.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. Things sharing a flow combine by reciprocal addition (1/total=1/a+1/b), always less than the smaller part.

title: The sextant finds your place on the earth by trigonometry -- the law of cosines, which is Pythagoras for every triangle
situation: For centuries a sailor's life depended on a sextant -- a brass arc that measures the angle between a star (or the sun) and the horizon. From a few such angles and the time, trigonometry fixes the ship's position anywhere on the open ocean. The mathematics is the LAW OF COSINES, c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2 a b cos(C), which relates the three sides of ANY triangle to one of its angles. It is the great generalization of Pythagoras: when the angle C is a right angle, cos(C) is zero and the law becomes exactly c^2 = a^2 + b^2 -- the 3-4-5 triangle and all its kin. For other angles it bends gracefully: an equilateral triangle (C = 60 degrees) gives back its equal side; a wide, obtuse angle (120 degrees) gives a longer far side (here c^2 = 19). The sextant turns this law into found latitude and longitude, and that countless ships came safely home by it is the proof the trigonometry is true.
use: To find an unknown distance or angle in any triangle -- a ship's position, a surveyor's baseline, a rangefinder's target, a force diagram -- use the law of cosines; it falls back to Pythagoras whenever there is a right angle. A measured angle plus two known lengths gives the third side; three known sides give any angle. The sextant is this law made portable, and its safe landfalls are the demonstration. HONEST NOTE: on the real globe a navigator uses the SPHERICAL law of cosines; the planar law sealed here is its small-region, flat kin.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 5/5. The law of cosines c^2=a^2+b^2-2ab cos(C) reduces to Pythagoras at C=90deg (cos=0). Checks: 3^2+4^2=5^2 (right); equilateral at 60deg returns c=2=a; a=2,b=3,C=60deg gives c^2=7; obtuse C=120deg gives c^2=19. A control (3^2+4^2=24) MISMATCHED.

domain_results.note: at a right angle cos(C)=0, so the law becomes a^2+b^2 -- Pythagoras
the right-angle case: 3^2+4^2=5^2
a 60-degree triangle returns its equal side
a non-right triangle: c^2 = 7
an obtuse angle gives a longer far side: c^2 = 19

wisdom: By measure of the stars the lost are guided to the shore appointed for them. 'Then are they glad because they be quiet; so he bringeth them unto their desired haven' (Psalm 107:30). The sextant's safe landfalls -- ship after ship brought home -- are trigonometry's plainest testimony, and a picture of a surer Guide.

title: One rule measures every triangle -- a star and a sextant can find you anywhere
verification: With a brass sextant to measure the angle up to a star, and one rule of trigonometry, sailors found their exact place on a featureless ocean. That rule -- the law of cosines -- works for every triangle there is, and when the corner is square it becomes the famous a squared plus b squared equals c squared. It is how rangefinders, surveys, and ships alike find a distance no one can walk. The engine confirmed the rule across right, equal, and wide triangles. A star, an angle, and true measure will carry you home.
wisdom: And God said, Let there be lights in the firmament of the heaven...and let them be for signs, and for seasons (Genesis 1:14). The stars were set as signs to steer by; the sextant only reads the sign that was hung there from the beginning.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. The sextant finds position by the law of cosines, which generalizes Pythagoras to any triangle; its safe landfalls demonstrate it.

title: The abacus computes with beads because of place value -- the same idea that runs every computer
situation: The abacus is humanity's oldest calculator, and it works on one quiet idea: PLACE VALUE. A bead is worth not what it is but WHERE it sits. The same single bead means one in the ones column, ten in the next, a hundred in the next -- so 342 is 3 hundreds plus 4 tens plus 2, the leftmost bead worth 300, not 3. Adding is just sliding beads and CARRYING when a column fills: 7 + 5 overflows to one ten and a two; 9 + 1 rolls the column over; 999 + 1 cascades all the way to 1000. This positional idea is not tied to ten: a bead-frame in base two reads 1011 as 8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 11 -- exactly how every digital computer counts, with two beads (0 and 1) per wire. The humble abacus and the silicon processor run on the very same trick of position. That merchants and engineers reckoned rightly on beads for four thousand years is the proof that place value is true.
use: Place value lets a few symbols (ten digits, or just two) name any number however large, and turns arithmetic into simple column rules with carries -- which is why one idea powers mental math, the abacus, and the computer alike. To work in any base, weight each column by a power of that base and carry when it fills. Binary is base two, hexadecimal base sixteen; the rule is one. The tool's four thousand years of right answers is the demonstration.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 6/6. Place value: 342 = 3*100 + 4*10 + 2 (the leftmost bead worth 300); carrying 7+5 = one ten and two; column rollover 9+1 = 10; cascade carry 999+1 = 1000; and the same idea in base two, 1011 = 8+0+2+1 = 11. A control with the place values swapped (giving 432, not 342) MISMATCHED.

domain_results.note: 342 = 3 hundreds + 4 tens + 2 -- value set by position
the same bead is worth 300 in the hundreds column
carrying: 7+5 overflows to one ten and a two
9+1 rolls the column over to the next
999+1 cascades all the way to 1000
the same trick in base two: 1011 = 11 -- how a computer counts

wisdom: The worth of a thing is often in its place and its time. 'A man hath joy by the answer of his mouth: and a word spoken in due season, how good is it!' (Proverbs 15:23). As the bead's value waits on its column, so a word's value waits on its place -- rightly set, the small becomes great.

title: On the abacus, where a bead sits sets its worth -- that is how a few beads count to millions
verification: The abacus shows a deep and simple truth: the SAME bead can mean one, or ten, or a hundred, depending only on which rod it sits on. Position gives value. That is how a mere ten kinds of marks -- or even just two -- can name any number, however huge, and why adding is just sliding beads and carrying when a column fills. It is the same idea inside every computer, which counts with only two: on and off. The engine confirmed the place-value arithmetic the abacus has run for ages.
wisdom: But now hath God set the members every one of them in the body, as it hath pleased him (1 Corinthians 12:18). Each is given worth by the place appointed it; rightly set, the least is needful, and together they make the whole sum.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. The abacus works by place value -- a bead's worth is its column; the same idea (in base two) runs every computer.

title: The tide-predicting machine sums waves with gears -- Fourier synthesis made of brass, foretelling the sea
situation: In 1872 Lord Kelvin built a machine of pulleys, wires, and gears that could predict the tides at any harbor for years ahead. It worked by Fourier SYNTHESIS: the tide is a sum of simple cosine waves, each a steady harmonic driven by the moon and sun (the twice-daily lunar, the daily solar, and their overtones), and the machine physically ADDED these waves by summing the motions of its gears, tracing the rising and falling tide on a roll of paper. The mathematics is the inverse of the harmonic analyzer: where analysis finds the component waves (an integral, the inner products sealed in the Hilbert-space card), synthesis adds them back. At high water all the harmonics crest together and their amplitudes simply sum; out of phase they cancel (a high tide plus a low gives still water, cos(0)+cos(pi)=0); and as more harmonics are added the partial sums close on the true curve -- the way the alternating series 1 - 1/3 + 1/5 - ... sums exactly to pi/4. That this machine of gears foretold real tides, ship after ship and year after year, is the proof that any periodic thing is a sum of pure waves.
use: Anything that repeats -- a tide, a sound, an electrical signal, a heartbeat, a season -- can be built (synthesized) from a sum of pure sine and cosine waves, and taken apart (analyzed) into them. To predict the future of a periodic signal, find its harmonics once and keep adding them forward: that is what the tide machine did with gears and what every signal processor does with numbers. The tide's harmonics are fixed by astronomy, which is why the far future of the tide is knowable while the weather is not.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 4/4. Synthesis at high water (t=0): the harmonics' amplitudes sum, 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 = 11/6 (a control =2 MISMATCHED). Out of phase they cancel: cos(0)+cos(pi)=0. A square-wave partial sum of three terms at pi/2 equals 52/(15 pi), closing on 1; and the synthesis limit, the alternating series sum (-1)^k/(2k+1), is exactly pi/4.

domain_results.note: at high water the harmonics crest together: amplitudes sum to 11/6
out of phase they cancel: cos(0)+cos(pi)=0 -- still water
a 3-term partial sum at pi/2 = 52/(15 pi), nearing the true value
the synthesis limit: 1 - 1/3 + 1/5 - ... = pi/4 exactly

wisdom: The lights of heaven keep the times, and by them the seas are reckoned. 'He appointed the moon for seasons: the sun knoweth his going down' (Psalm 104:19). The tide machine only counts what the moon already governs -- a sum of appointed waves, foretold because it was first ordained.

title: Anything that repeats is a sum of simple waves -- which is why the tides can be foretold
verification: The rise and fall of the sea looks complicated, but it is really just a handful of simple, steady waves -- driven by the moon and sun -- added together. Over a century ago engineers built a machine of gears that added those waves and printed the tide tables years in advance, and ships have trusted them ever since. The same truth holds for any repeating thing -- a musical note, a heartbeat, a radio signal: each is pure waves stacked up. The engine confirmed the adding-up that makes it work, down to the long sum that lands exactly on pi/4.
wisdom: Hitherto shalt thou come, but no further: and here shall thy proud waves be stayed (Job 38:11). The sea keeps an appointed bound and an appointed rhythm; what is ordered can be foreseen, and the tide table is a small reading of that order.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. The tide is a sum of harmonic waves; Kelvin's machine added them with gears to predict tides -- Fourier synthesis.

title: The pantograph copies a drawing at any scale -- a linkage that keeps similar triangles, so every ratio is preserved
situation: A pantograph is a jointed frame of four rods making a parallelogram, pinned at one point. Trace a picture with one pointer and a pen at another point draws a perfect copy -- twice the size, or half, or any chosen scale. It works because the linkage forces three points to stay in a straight line at a FIXED ratio of distances; the moving arms always form SIMILAR TRIANGLES, and similar triangles keep every ratio the same (6:3 = 8:4 = 10:5). So a point at (x, y) is faithfully sent to (2x, 2y) -- shape preserved, only size changed. A subtle, important consequence follows: while lengths scale by the ratio, AREA scales by the ratio SQUARED -- a copy twice as wide covers four times the paper, three times as wide covers nine. The pantograph is the law of similar triangles -- the same proportion that underlies all analogy -- made into a drafting tool, and that it copies maps, engravings, and machine parts faithfully is the proof the law holds.
use: To enlarge or reduce any shape exactly, fix a ratio and scale every length by it -- a pantograph does this mechanically; a photocopier and a CAD 'scale' command do it in numbers. Remember that area grows as the SQUARE of the scale and volume as the CUBE: a model doubled in size weighs eight times as much, which is the heart of why a giant cannot be merely a big man and why small creatures are so strong for their size. The pantograph is analogy you can hold: same form, different size.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 5/5. A fixed scale keeps every ratio: 10/5 = 14/7 = 2 (scale 2), 12/4 = 15/5 = 3 (scale 3) -- similar triangles. A traced point doubles (14/7 = 2). Area scales as the ratio squared: a 2x copy has 4x the area (2^2=4), a 3x copy 9x (3^2=9). A control (2x copy area = 2) MISMATCHED.

domain_results.note: scale 2 keeps every ratio equal -- similar triangles
the copied point is exactly twice the original
area grows as the square of the scale: 2x -> 4x area
scale 3 triples every length
scale 3 gives nine times the area

wisdom: A faithful copy is made by keeping to the pattern shown. 'And look that thou make them after their pattern, which was shewed thee in the mount' (Exodus 25:40). The pantograph holds to the pattern in every proportion, changing the size but never the form -- a small craft of faithfulness.

title: A pantograph copies any picture bigger or smaller -- but area grows as the SQUARE of the size
verification: There is an old drafting tool, the pantograph -- a set of hinged arms -- that copies any drawing at twice the size, or half, just by tracing it. It keeps the shape exactly because it keeps every proportion. But here is the catch worth knowing: when you double the size, you quadruple the AREA, and eight-fold the bulk. That is why a recipe doubled needs a far bigger pot, why a true giant could not stand, and why small creatures are so strong for their size. The engine confirmed the scaling -- length by k, area by k squared.
wisdom: But God hath chosen the foolish things of the world to confound the wise; and...the weak things of the world to confound the things which are mighty (1 Corinthians 1:27). The small is stronger for its size than the great -- a square-cube mercy woven into the made world, that the least is not as feeble as it looks.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. The pantograph scales a drawing by a fixed ratio (similar triangles); length scales by k, area by k^2, volume by k^3.

title: The barometer weighs the sky -- a column of mercury that makes hydrostatic pressure P = rho g h visible
situation: In 1643 Torricelli sealed a tube of mercury and upended it in a dish; the mercury fell only so far, leaving a vacuum above and standing about 76 cm high, held up by the weight of the whole atmosphere pressing on the dish. This is the law of hydrostatic pressure: the pressure of a still fluid grows in simple proportion to depth, P = rho g h (density times gravity times height). Double the column and you double the pressure; halve the depth and you halve it. A mercury column of 76 cm balances one atmosphere (about 103360 pascals); and because water is 13.6 times lighter than mercury, the same pressure needs a water column 13.6 times taller -- over ten metres, which is exactly why a simple suction pump cannot draw water higher than about that. The barometer turns the invisible pressure of air and sea into a visible height, and that its readings forecast storms and fix altitudes is the proof the law is true.
use: The pressure in any still fluid is rho g h -- it depends only on depth (with density and gravity), never on the width or shape of the container, the famous hydrostatic paradox. To find a pressure, multiply density by gravity by depth; to read the air, watch the mercury -- falling air pressure warns of coming storms, rising of fair weather. The same law is why deep water crushes, why a dam is built thick at its base where the pressure is greatest, and why a straw or suction pump has a hard limit.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 4/4. P = rho g h is linear in depth: doubling the column doubles the pressure (a control claiming triple MISMATCHED), and half the depth gives half the pressure. Concretely 76 cm of mercury = 13600 * 10 * 0.76 = 103360 Pa (about one atmosphere). Water, 13.6 times lighter, needs a column 13.6 times taller for the same pressure (13600/1000 = 68/5).

domain_results.note: pressure doubles when the column doubles -- strictly proportional to depth
half the depth, half the pressure
76 cm of mercury balances about one atmosphere, 103360 Pa
water needs a 13.6x taller column -- why suction can't lift water past ~10 m

wisdom: Even the wind has a weight, set by the hand that made it. 'To make the weight for the winds; and he weigheth the waters by measure' (Job 28:25). The barometer only reads a weight long since appointed -- the press of the air that He balanced when He framed the world.

title: A column of mercury weighs the whole sky -- and its fall foretells the storm
verification: It is hard to believe air has weight, but it does -- the whole ocean of air above us presses down hard enough to hold up a column of mercury about as tall as your arm. That is a barometer. The deeper you go in any fluid, air or water, the greater the pressure, in plain proportion to the depth. When the mercury falls the air is lightening, and a storm is often near; when it rises, fair weather is coming. Sailors and farmers have read it for centuries. The engine confirmed the pressure law it runs on.
wisdom: When it is evening, ye say, It will be fair weather: for the sky is red...O ye hypocrites, ye can discern the face of the sky; but can ye not discern the signs of the times? (Matthew 16:2-3). We are given signs to read in the very air; the wise heed both the lesser signs and the greater.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. A barometer balances the atmosphere's weight against a mercury column (P=rho g h); its fall warns of storms.

title: A soap film finds the least surface by itself -- nature solving a minimization, and why bubbles are round
situation: Dip a bent wire frame in soapy water and lift it out: the film that spans it is never ragged but always the smallest surface that can fit the frame, drawn taut by surface tension pulling itself to least area. The film is an analog computer solving a hard problem of the calculus of variations -- find the surface of minimum area on this boundary -- and it solves it instantly, by simply being. Stretch the film between two parallel rings and it forms a catenoid: the surface made by spinning the catenary curve (the hyperbolic cosine) about an axis -- the same cosh the hanging chain draws. The same drive to least surface makes a free soap bubble a perfect SPHERE, because the sphere encloses the most volume for the least skin. Its cousin in the plane is the circle, which for a given perimeter encloses the most area -- a circle of perimeter 4 holds 4/pi (about 1.27), more than the square's 1. And the disk's area is itself an accumulation of thin rings: the integral of 2*pi*x is pi*x^2, so the area is pi*r^2. That films and bubbles unfailingly find the least surface is the proof these minimizing laws are true.
use: When nature is free to choose a shape under tension or pressure, it chooses the one of least surface (or, for a fixed boundary, least area) -- which is why drops and bubbles are round, why foams meet at the angles they do, and why the lightest, strongest spanning roofs follow soap-film shapes (architects model them with actual soap). To find an enclosed area, integrate thin pieces (a disk is its rings, pi*r^2); to enclose the most for the least edge, go round.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 5/5. The disk is built of rings: integral of 2*pi*x is pi*x^2, so area = pi*r^2 (radius 3 gives 9*pi). Isoperimetric: a circle of perimeter 4 encloses 4/pi (about 1.27), a square of the same perimeter only 1, and 4/pi > 1 -- the round shape wins (a control 4/pi > 2 MISMATCHED). The soap film/bubble minimizes surface, which is why it rounds.

domain_results.note: a disk is the integral of its rings: int 2*pi*x dx = pi*x^2 (area pi*r^2)
a disk of radius 3 has area 9*pi
a circle of perimeter 4 encloses area 4/pi
a square of the same perimeter encloses only 1
4/pi > 1: the round shape encloses the most for a given edge

wisdom: Creation is frugal, and gathers up what would be lost. 'Gather up the fragments that remain, that nothing be lost' (John 6:12). The soap film keeps the least surface and the bubble the least skin -- a small thrift written into matter, that nothing be spent in vain.

title: A soap bubble is round because nature wastes nothing -- the least skin for the most inside
verification: A soap bubble is a perfect ball not by accident but by economy: a sphere wraps the most air in the least skin, and the film, pulling itself tight, finds that shape on its own. Dip a wire loop and the film stretches to the smallest surface that fits the frame. The same thrift makes raindrops round and sets the angles where bubbles meet in a foam. It is nature solving 'use the least' without a single calculation. The engine confirmed that the round shape wins -- a circle holds more than a square of the same edge.
wisdom: There is treasure to be desired and oil in the dwelling of the wise; but a foolish man spendeth it up (Proverbs 21:20). The wise, like the bubble, keep much in little and waste nothing; thrift is a kind of wisdom written even into water and air.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. Soap films/bubbles minimize surface; the round shape encloses the most for the least boundary (isoperimetric).

title: The analog computer solves an equation by obeying it -- a circuit that IS the differential operator
situation: Before digital machines, hard differential equations were solved by ANALOG computers: circuits built so that their own physics obeys the very equation you want solved, then simply let run. A resistor-capacitor circuit discharging obeys dV/dt = -V/(RC), the law of exponential decay, so its falling voltage IS the solution V = V0 e^(-t/RC) -- and after one 'time constant' RC it has dropped to 1/e (about 37%) of its start, the same one-over-e that governs cooling, half-lives, and discounting. An op-amp INTEGRATOR has an output equal to the running integral of its input (feed it a cosine and out comes a sine) -- it embodies the Fundamental Theorem. Wire an inductor to a capacitor and the LC circuit obeys y'' + w^2 y = 0, so it RINGS as a sine wave, the eigenfunction of the second derivative. The machine does not calculate the answer step by step; it IS the equation, and its measured voltages are the solution. This is the Hilbert-space truth made of wire: a differential operator is a physical thing, and a circuit can be built to be it. That such circuits flew aircraft and guided rockets is the proof.
use: When you need the behaviour of a system governed by a differential equation -- a suspension, a control loop, a reactor -- build (or simulate) a circuit that obeys the same equation and read off its response, often faster than solving by formula. The deep lesson: equations are not merely marks on paper; a physical system can embody one exactly, which is why a model that shares your system's equation predicts it, and why a digital simulation that mimics the same equation in numbers works too.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 4/4. An RC circuit obeys dV/dt = -V/k, solved by exp(-x/k) (a control with growth exp(+x/k) MISMATCHED); after one time constant it is at 1/e of its start. An integrator accumulates: integral of cos is sin. An LC circuit obeys y'' + y = 0 and rings as cos -- the eigenfunction of the second derivative. The circuit's physics IS the equation.

domain_results.note: RC discharge obeys dV/dt=-V/k, solved by exp(-x/k) -- the circuit is the decay law
after one time constant the voltage is 1/e of its start
the integrator's output is the integral of its input: cos becomes sin
the LC circuit solves y''+y=0 and rings as a sine -- an eigenfunction

wisdom: The deepest law is not written on a tablet only but into the very nature of a thing. 'I will put my law in their inward parts, and write it in their hearts' (Jeremiah 31:33). The circuit obeys its equation because the equation is written into what it is -- a faint image of a law that becomes a nature.

title: A circuit can solve an equation just by obeying it -- the law written into the thing itself
verification: Here is a wonder from old engineering: instead of solving a hard equation with pencil and paper, you can build a little circuit whose natural behaviour IS that equation, switch it on, and read the answer off a meter. A simple capacitor draining through a resistor traces the exact curve of decay -- of cooling coffee, of fading medicine, of a settling sound. The thing does not compute the law; it lives it. The engine confirmed the equations these circuits obey. A law can be written not only on a page, but into the very nature of a thing.
wisdom: But be ye doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving your own selves (James 1:22). The circuit is a doer, not a hearer -- it does not merely hold the equation but lives it; so too the word is meant to become a life, not just a thought.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. An analog circuit solves a differential equation because its physics obeys the same equation -- the RC circuit IS exponential decay.

title: Coprimality is the hinge of clock arithmetic -- when numbers share no factor, the moduli cooperate
situation: Two whole numbers are COPRIME when they share no factor but 1 (their greatest common divisor is 1). This simple condition is the secret that makes MODULAR arithmetic -- the arithmetic of clocks and remainders -- work cleanly. The Chinese Remainder Theorem says that if you know a number's remainder modulo 3 and modulo 5 (which are coprime), you know it uniquely modulo 15: the value 8 is the only one below 15 that is 2 mod 3 and 3 mod 5. Euler's counting function is multiplicative across coprime parts: phi(15) = phi(3) times phi(5) = 2 times 4 = 8, the count of numbers below 15 sharing no factor with it. And a number has a modular INVERSE -- a partner that multiplies with it to give 1 on the clock -- exactly when it is coprime to the modulus: 8 times 2 is 1 modulo 15. This last fact is the engine of RSA and much of cryptography. Coprimality (a fact about factors) and modular arithmetic (a fact about remainders) are two faces of one structure.
use: To combine information modulo coprime numbers, use the Chinese Remainder Theorem -- it lets a big modulus be split into small coprime ones, the basis of fast arithmetic and error-checking. To find a modular inverse (for codes, calendars, or RSA keys), first ensure the number is coprime to the modulus; if it is, an inverse is guaranteed to exist. Coprimality is the green light for the modular wheel to turn cleanly.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 6/6. gcd(8,15)=1 (coprime; a control =3 MISMATCHED). CRT: 8 is 2 mod 3 and 3 mod 5, the unique residue mod 15. Euler phi is multiplicative over coprimes: phi(15)=phi(3)*phi(5)=8. A modular inverse exists because of coprimality: 8 times 2 = 1 mod 15.

domain_results.note: 8 and 15 are coprime: gcd = 1
8 = 2 mod 3
8 = 3 mod 5 -- with the above, unique mod 15 (CRT)
phi(15) = phi(3) * phi(5): multiplicative over coprime factors
phi(15) = 8 numbers below 15 are coprime to it
8 has an inverse mod 15 (8*2=1) because it is coprime -- the basis of RSA

wisdom: Cooperation begins in agreement, and what shares no division can journey as one. 'Can two walk together, except they be agreed?' (Amos 3:3). Coprime numbers share no common factor, and just so they walk together -- their remainders combining cleanly, their moduli never clashing.

title: Numbers that share no common factor cooperate -- the quiet secret behind codes and calendars
verification: Two numbers are 'coprime' when they have no factor in common, like 8 and 15. It seems a small thing, but it is the quiet key to a great deal: it is why knowing a date's day-of-week and its day-of-month together can pin it down, why the secret codes guarding a bank can be built (and only with great labour broken), and why some gears mesh smoothly without ever repeating the same pairing of teeth. When numbers share no factor, they fit together without clashing. The engine confirmed the arithmetic behind it.
wisdom: Behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity! (Psalm 133:1). Even numbers dwell together best when nothing divides them in common -- a small picture of a peace that comes of sharing no quarrel.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. Coprime numbers (gcd=1) make modular arithmetic work: the CRT, Euler phi, and modular inverses (the basis of RSA) all depend on it.

title: Do it twice and you are back where you started -- the involution recurs in every block of the map
situation: Some operations are their own undoing: apply them twice and you return to the start. This is an INVOLUTION (a thing of order two), one structure wearing many faces across the whole map. Negation: minus minus five is five. Reciprocal: one over one-over-seven is seven. Logical or genetic complement: the complement of the complement is the original (1 minus (1 minus x) is x) -- the same self-undoing that pairs a DNA strand with its mirror and reads back the original. Reflection and matrix transpose: flip a matrix across its diagonal twice and it is unchanged. The complex conjugate, applied twice, returns the number. Even a half-turn rotation, done again, is a full turn home. Each lives in a different block -- algebra, logic, geometry, number -- yet all are the same simple shape: an operation that is its own inverse, a switch that toggles. Recognizing it once is recognizing it everywhere.
use: When an operation undoes itself -- a toggle, a flip, a swap, a complement, a negation -- you have an involution, which means doing it an even number of times changes nothing and an odd number flips once. This is why XOR encrypts and decrypts with the same key, why two reflections make a rotation, why pairing and unpairing DNA strands recovers the message, and why 'not not true' is simply true. Find the toggle and the whole problem grows simpler.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 6/6. The same order-two structure across blocks: -(-5)=5 (negation); 1/(1/7)=7 (reciprocal); 1-(1-x)=x (complement); conjugate(conjugate(2+3i))=2+3i; and transpose round-trips ([[1,2],[3,4]] -> [[1,3],[2,4]] -> [[1,2],[3,4]]). A control (-(-5) = -5) MISMATCHED. Each is its own inverse.

domain_results.note: negation undoes negation: -(-5)=5
reciprocal undoes reciprocal: 1/(1/7)=7
complement of the complement is the original -- the DNA/logic toggle
complex conjugate twice returns the number
transpose flips a matrix across its diagonal
transpose again returns the original -- reflection is an involution

wisdom: What is undone can be made again as it was; some turnings lead straight home. 'He restoreth my soul: he leadeth me in the paths of righteousness for his name's sake' (Psalm 23:3). The involution is restoration in miniature -- the second turn that brings a thing back to what it was.

title: Some actions undo themselves -- do them twice and you are right back where you started
verification: A light switch flipped twice is as it was; turn a sock inside out twice and it is right again; negate a negative and you have a positive; the opposite of the opposite is the thing itself. Mathematicians call these 'involutions' -- operations that are their own undoing. They are everywhere once you notice: it is why the same key both locks and unlocks a simple cipher, and why a mirror's mirror shows you true. The engine confirmed the pattern across negation, reciprocal, reflection, and conjugation.
wisdom: That which hath been is now; and that which is to be hath already been; and God requireth that which is past (Ecclesiastes 3:15). Some roads, walked twice, return you home; there is comfort in the turning that restores what was.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. An involution is its own inverse (negation, reciprocal, transpose, complement, conjugate) -- do it twice and you return to the start.

title: The second derivative reads two things at once -- how fast a change is changing, and which way a curve bends
situation: Apply the derivative twice and you get the SECOND derivative, and it carries two meanings that are really one. In MOTION it is acceleration: the first derivative of position is velocity, and the second is acceleration -- the very quantity in Newton's F = ma. For a body moving as position t^3, the velocity is 3t^2 and the acceleration is 6t. In SHAPE it is concavity, telling which way a curve bends: where the second derivative is positive the curve is concave up, cupping like a valley (y = x^2 has second derivative +2); where negative it is concave down, doming like a hill (y = -x^2 has -2); and where it passes through zero the bend reverses -- an inflection point (x^3 changes concavity at 0). So a peak is marked by negative concavity, a trough by positive, which is exactly how one tells a true maximum from a minimum. The acceleration of a thing and the curvature of its graph are the same number: the rate at which a rate is changing.
use: To learn whether a critical point is a peak or a valley, check the second derivative -- negative means a maximum (concave down), positive a minimum (concave up); this is the second-derivative test used from economics to engineering. To find acceleration, differentiate position twice. And watch the inflection, where the second derivative crosses zero -- the moment a trend stops speeding up and begins to slow: the turning of the tide in a growth curve, an epidemic, or a market.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 7/7. The second derivative of x^3 is 6x (a control claiming 3x MISMATCHED). Acceleration is the second derivative of position: d/dt of 3t^2 is 6t. Concavity: x^2 has +2 (concave up, a valley), -x^2 has -2 (concave down, a peak), and -2 < 0 marks the peak; x^3 has an inflection where 6x = 0, at x = 0.

domain_results.note: first derivative of x^3 is 3x^2 (velocity)
second derivative of x^3 is 6x
acceleration = second derivative of position: 6t
x^2 has second derivative +2: concave up, a valley
-x^2 has second derivative -2: concave down, a hill
a peak is marked by negative concavity (-2 < 0)
the bend reverses where the second derivative is zero -- an inflection

wisdom: The way of the upright bends ever upward, brightening as it goes. 'But the path of the just is as the shining light, that shineth more and more unto the perfect day' (Proverbs 4:18). The second derivative is the measure of that 'more and more' -- not the brightness only, but its quickening.

title: Don't just watch how fast it's changing -- watch whether that is speeding up or slowing down
verification: The first thing to know about anything moving or growing is how fast it is changing. But the deeper thing is whether that change is itself speeding up or easing off -- and that is the 'second derivative', which is both acceleration (the push you feel pressed into a car seat) and the bend of the curve. When a rising trend stops accelerating and begins to level, that turning point -- the inflection -- is often the real news, whether in an illness spreading, a price climbing, or a child growing. The engine confirmed the math: a peak bends one way, a valley the other.
wisdom: Now learn a parable of the fig tree; When his branch is yet tender, and putteth forth leaves, ye know that summer is nigh (Matthew 24:32). Wisdom reads not just the state of a thing but the turning of it -- the tender branch that tells the season is changing.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. The second derivative is acceleration and concavity together: a peak bends down, a valley up, the inflection is where a trend's acceleration turns.

title: A string's overtones are the eigenfunctions of the wave equation -- music is Fourier you can hear
situation: Pluck a string fixed at both ends and it cannot vibrate just any way: only the standing waves that fit exactly between the ends survive -- shapes like sin(n pi x / L) with a whole number of half-waves, pinned to zero at both ends (sin of 3 pi is 0). These are the natural MODES of the string, and they are precisely the EIGENFUNCTIONS of the wave equation, each obeying y'' + n^2 y = 0 -- the same eigenfunctions that fill a Hilbert space and that Kelvin's tide machine summed with gears. Their frequencies are the HARMONIC SERIES: f, 2f, 3f, 4f, whole-number multiples of the fundamental. From these whole numbers come the musical intervals: the octave is 2:1 (second harmonic over first), the perfect fifth 3:2 (third over second), the perfect fourth 4:3 (fourth over third), and two octaves is 4f. A real instrument's TIMBRE -- what makes a violin unlike a flute on the same note -- is just the particular recipe of how much of each harmonic it mixes in: a Fourier sum made audible. The consonance we hear is the ear recognizing small whole-number ratios; the mathematics of a vibrating string and the feeling of a chord are one thing.
use: To understand any sustained sound, hear it as a fundamental plus its overtones (the harmonic series), each an eigenmode of the vibrating body; their blend is its tone colour. Tune by the small-integer ratios the harmonics give (octave 2:1, fifth 3:2); shape timbre by emphasizing or damping particular overtones -- which is what an equalizer, a mute, or the very body of a guitar does. The same eigenfunction-and-Fourier machinery describes a drumhead, a wind column, a bell, and a bridge swaying in the wind.

bonds: timbre<->Fourier sum (the tide machine, by ear)

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 6/6. The harmonic series gives the intervals: octave 2:1, fifth 3:2 (third harmonic over second), fourth 4:3, two octaves = 4f. A string pinned at both ends admits only integer modes (sin(3 pi)=0). And the overtone is an eigenfunction of the wave equation: y'' + 9y = 0 is solved by sin(3x) -- the third mode, eigenvalue -9. A control (fifth = 5/4) MISMATCHED.

domain_results.note: the octave is the second harmonic over the first, 2:1
the perfect fifth is harmonic 3 over harmonic 2: 3:2
the perfect fourth is harmonic 4 over harmonic 3: 4:3
two octaves up is four times the frequency
fixed ends force whole-number modes: sin(3 pi)=0 at the end
the overtone solves the wave equation -- an eigenfunction, eigenvalue -n^2

wisdom: Let the strings be tuned and the praise rise in fitting order. 'Praise him with stringed instruments and organs' (Psalm 150:4). The string sounds its maker's order -- whole numbers ringing together -- and the consonance we love is the print of that order on the ear.

title: A single plucked string is already a chord -- the overtones hiding inside one note
verification: Pluck one string and you think you hear a single note, but you are really hearing a whole stack: the main tone plus its 'overtones', quieter notes at twice, three times, four times the speed -- the harmonic series. Those overtones are why a fifth (a 3-to-2 ratio) and an octave (2-to-1) sound sweet: the ear delights in simple whole-number relationships. And the particular mix of overtones is what makes a violin sound unlike a flute on the very same note. The engine confirmed the whole-number ratios, and that each overtone is a natural mode of the string.
wisdom: Speaking to yourselves in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing and making melody in your heart to the Lord (Ephesians 5:19). As one string holds many tones in harmony, so one heart may hold a whole song; the richness is folded into the simple.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. A plucked string sounds a fundamental plus overtones (the harmonic series); the intervals are small-integer ratios; timbre is the mix.

title: Add up many random parts and the spread grows only as the square root -- why more samples bring more certainty
situation: When you add together n independent random quantities of equal size, their VARIANCES add, so the spread (standard deviation) of the total grows not as n but as the SQUARE ROOT of n. Add a hundred and the spread is ten times one, not a hundred times. The far-reaching consequence is for AVERAGES: the spread of the average shrinks as one over the square root of n, so four times as many measurements only halve the uncertainty, and a hundred measurements pin the average down ten times tighter than one. This single law is why a random walk wanders only about the square root of n steps from where it began (a hundred coin-flip steps, about ten from start); why a poll of more people is more trustworthy (the 'standard error' is sigma over root n); why spreading money across many independent bets steadies the whole; and why the Galton board's bell, built of many tiny chances, has a definite, predictable width. Aggregation tames randomness -- but only as the square root, so certainty is bought dearly: four-fold effort for two-fold precision.
use: To make an average more certain, gather more independent samples -- but remember the cost: precision improves only as the square root, so halving your error takes four times the data, and cutting it tenfold takes a hundredfold. Use it to size a survey (standard error = sigma / root n), to judge how far a drifting random process has likely strayed (about root n), and to see why diversification across independent risks shrinks the swings without removing them. One caution: it holds only when the parts are INDEPENDENT; shared causes break it, which is how crowds and markets grow their fat tails.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 5/5. The spread of a sum of 100 equal independent parts is sqrt(100)=10, not 100 (a control claiming 100 MISMATCHED). The average's spread shrinks as 1/sqrt(100)=1/10. A random walk drifts about sqrt(9)=3 in 9 steps. To halve an error needs four times the data: sqrt(400)/sqrt(100)=2. The root cause: variances add, so std = sqrt(n)*sigma (n=4 gives 2).

domain_results.note: the spread of a sum of 100 grows as sqrt(100)=10, not linearly
the average's spread shrinks as 1/sqrt(n) = 1/10 for n=100
a random walk wanders about sqrt(n) from the start
four times the data halves the error -- certainty bought dearly
variances add, so the standard deviation grows as sqrt(n)*sigma

wisdom: Many sound and independent witnesses make a matter sure. 'Where no counsel is, the people fall: but in the multitude of counsellors there is safety' (Proverbs 11:14). The square-root law is that safety counted out -- many honest voices, each erring on its own, average toward the truth.

title: Many independent measurements steady the truth -- but four times the effort buys only twice the certainty
verification: Ask one person the time and you might be off; average a hundred honest, independent guesses and you will be remarkably close, for the errors cancel. That is the quiet power of averaging. But there is a catch the wise remember: certainty grows only as the square root of the number. To be twice as sure you need four times the data; ten times as sure, a hundred times. It is why bigger polls are better (though not cheaply so), why spreading your bets steadies the ride, and why a wandering random path drifts only about the square root of its steps from home. The engine confirmed the law.
wisdom: In the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established (Matthew 18:16). Truth is steadied by many independent witnesses -- but they must be independent; voices that merely echo one another add no surety.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. The spread of a sum grows as sqrt(n); the average's error shrinks as 1/sqrt(n), so 4x the data halves the error.

title: The senses hear ratios, not differences -- perception grows as the logarithm of the stimulus (Weber-Fechner)
situation: Our senses measure the world not by absolute amounts but by RATIOS. To just notice a change, a stimulus must grow by a constant FRACTION of itself (Weber's law) -- you can feel one candle added to one, but not one added to a hundred. So sensation grows as the LOGARITHM of the stimulus (Fechner's law): equal MULTIPLICATIONS of the stimulus feel like equal additive STEPS of sensation. This is why the senses are measured on logarithmic scales. Loudness in DECIBELS is ten times the log of the power ratio: ten times the power is +10 dB, a hundred times only +20 dB -- a jet is not 'a hundred times louder' to the ear than a whisper, but a fixed number of steps up. Star MAGNITUDES run the same way: a hundredfold in brightness is just five steps of magnitude. Three doublings of a sound feel like three equal stairs, because log of 8 over log of 2 is exactly 3. The ear, the eye, and even the sense of weight all take the logarithm -- so the logarithm, that compressor of the vast into the manageable, is built into how we perceive at all.
use: When designing for human senses -- volume knobs, brightness controls, pain scales, prices that 'feel' fair -- move in RATIOS, not fixed amounts, because that is what we perceive evenly. It explains why a few dollars off feels like a bargain on a cheap item and nothing on a costly one, why dimmer switches and audio faders are logarithmic, and why adding the tenth instrument to an orchestra barely changes the felt loudness. The senses are logarithmic; speak to them in multiples.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 5/5. Decibels are logarithmic: 10x power = +10 dB, 100x power = +20 dB, not +100 (a control claiming 100 MISMATCHED). Three doublings of a stimulus are three equal perceived steps (log 8 / log 2 = 3). A hundredfold brightness is two decades (log 100 / log 10 = 2; the magnitude scale spans 5 steps). Multiplying the stimulus adds to the sensation (log 100 = 2 log 10).

domain_results.note: ten times the power feels like +10 dB
a hundred times the power feels like only +20 dB -- the ear compresses
three doublings = three equal perceived steps (log 8 / log 2 = 3)
a hundredfold in brightness is two log-decades (the magnitude scale)
multiplying the stimulus adds to the sensation -- the logarithm at work

wisdom: Worth is weighed by what one has, not by the bare amount. 'This poor widow hath cast more in, than all they which have cast into the treasury: For all they did cast in of their abundance; but she of her want did cast in all that she had' (Mark 12:43-44). The senses judge as Heaven does here -- by the fraction, not the sum; a small thing is great against a small store.

title: We feel changes as fractions, not amounts -- one candle shows in a dark room, not at noon
verification: Light one candle in a dark room and the change is striking; light the same candle at noon and you will never see it. Our senses judge by RATIO, not by absolute amount: to notice a change, a thing must grow by a certain fraction of what is already there. That is why loudness, brightness, and even prices 'feel' logarithmic -- a hundred times the sound is only a few steps louder to the ear, and the same few dollars off feels like a bargain on a cheap thing and nothing on a costly one. The engine confirmed the logarithmic law behind it.
wisdom: For if there be first a willing mind, it is accepted according to that a man hath, and not according to that he hath not (2 Corinthians 8:12). We, and the senses given us, weigh by what is there to compare against -- the small gift and the small light loom large where little is.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. Weber-Fechner: the senses respond to ratios, so sensation is the log of the stimulus (decibels, star magnitudes).

title: The determinant is how much a matrix stretches space -- area in two dimensions, volume in three
situation: A matrix transforms space, and its DETERMINANT is a single number telling exactly how much it scales AREA (in 2D) or VOLUME (in 3D), with a sign that records whether it flips orientation. Send the unit square through [[2,0],[0,3]] and it becomes a 2-by-3 rectangle of area 6 -- the determinant is 6. A SHEAR like [[1,1],[0,1]] slides the top of the square sideways but keeps its area, so its determinant is 1; the identity, which changes nothing, is also 1. A reflection that swaps the axes, [[0,1],[1,0]], has determinant -1: the same area, but mirror-flipped. Scale both sides by 2 and the area grows by 4 = 2 squared (determinant 4), the same square-law a pantograph obeys; in three dimensions, stretching by 2, 3, and 4 multiplies volume by 24. And when the determinant is ZERO, space has been crushed flat -- the transformation cannot be undone, which is exactly why a zero determinant means no inverse exists. The determinant binds algebra to geometry: a property of numbers in a grid that is really the swelling or shrinking of space itself.
use: Read a matrix's determinant as the volume-change it causes: greater than 1 expands, between 0 and 1 shrinks, negative flips orientation, zero collapses (and so cannot be reversed). It is how a change of variables rescales an integral (the Jacobian determinant), how a transformation's invertibility is judged at a glance, and how an area or volume is computed straight from coordinates. Sign and size in one number: orientation and scale together.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 5/5. det[[2,0],[0,3]]=6 (the unit square becomes a 2x3 box; a control claiming 5 MISMATCHED); det[[1,1],[0,1]]=1 (a shear preserves area); det[[0,1],[1,0]]=-1 (a reflection: same area, flipped); det[[2,0],[0,2]]=4 (scaling by 2 gives 4x area, the square law); and in 3D det diag(2,3,4)=24 (volume).

domain_results.note: determinant 6: the unit square stretched to a 2x3 rectangle
a shear keeps area: determinant 1
a reflection has determinant -1: same area, opposite orientation
doubling both sides gives 4x area (k^2) -- the square law
in 3D the determinant is the volume factor: 2*3*4 = 24

wisdom: To enlarge a border is a gift, and the measure of the enlarging can be known. 'And Jabez called on the God of Israel, saying, Oh that thou wouldest bless me indeed, and enlarge my coast...And God granted him that which he requested' (1 Chronicles 4:10). The determinant is the plain reckoning of such enlarging -- by how much a space is widened, or, being zero, lost.

title: One number tells how much a stretch grows an area -- and whether it flipped it over
verification: Take any flat shape and stretch or shear it, and a single number -- the 'determinant' -- tells you exactly how many times bigger or smaller its area became, plus a minus sign if you flipped it like a mirror. Double a shape's width and height and the area goes up four times, not two (that square-law again). Squash it perfectly flat and the number is zero -- and you can never un-squash it. The engine confirmed these: a 2-by-3 box gives 6, a shear keeps its area, a flip gives minus one.
wisdom: Enlarge the place of thy tent, and let them stretch forth the curtains of thine habitations: spare not, lengthen thy cords, and strengthen thy stakes (Isaiah 54:2). To widen a space is promised increase; and what was pressed flat to nothing cannot be made wide again by the same hand that pressed it.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. A matrix's determinant is the area (or volume) scale factor; negative flips orientation; zero collapses space (no inverse).

title: Two rotations make one -- turning then turning again simply adds the angles (the circle group)
situation: Rotate the plane by one angle, then by another, and the result is a single rotation by the SUM of the two: R(a) followed by R(b) is R(a+b). Turn 60 degrees then 30 more and you have turned a quarter, 90 degrees; two quarter-turns make a half-turn. This 'angles simply add' is the structure of a GROUP -- the rotations of the plane form the circle group, closed, with rotation-by-zero as the do-nothing identity and the opposite turn as each rotation's undo. The truth behind it is the addition formulas of trigonometry, cos(a+b) = cos a cos b - sin a sin b and its partner for sine, which are exactly the entries you get when you multiply two rotation matrices. And it has a breathtakingly simple form through Euler: a rotation by angle a is the complex number e^(i a), so composing rotations is just e^(i a) times e^(i b) = e^(i (a+b)) -- multiplication of these unit numbers IS addition of angles. This is why the Smith chart's motion down a transmission line is rotation, why phases combine by adding, and why anything that spins or cycles obeys one tidy law: turns add.
use: Whenever rotations, phases, or cyclic shifts combine, ADD their angles -- do not multiply the turns; the multiplication is hidden in the unit complex numbers e^(i theta). This is how gears, clock hands, AC phases, polarizers, and orbital positions compose; how a rotation is undone by its negative; and why repeating a turn n times gives angle n times theta. Phase and angle are additive: that is the gift of the circle group.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 5/5. The addition formulas hold: cos(a+b)=cos a cos b - sin a sin b, sin(a+b)=sin a cos b + cos a sin b (a control with the wrong sign, which is cos(a-b), MISMATCHED). Concretely 60deg then 30deg gives cos = 0 (a quarter turn); two quarter-turns give cos(pi)= -1. In Euler form, e^(i a) e^(i b) = e^(i (a+b)) -- multiplying unit complex numbers adds the angles.

domain_results.note: cos(a+b) = cos a cos b - sin a sin b -- the rotation-matrix product
sin(a+b) = sin a cos b + cos a sin b
60deg then 30deg = a quarter turn (cosine 0)
e^(i a) e^(i b) = e^(i (a+b)): multiplying unit numbers adds angles
two quarter-turns make a half-turn (cosine -1)

wisdom: The turning of the times is ordered, each in its season, and the turns come round in measure. 'To every thing there is a season, and a time to every purpose under the heaven' (Ecclesiastes 3:1). So the angles of a turning add and return; the circle keeps its seasons and comes home.

title: Turn, then turn again, and the turns just add -- a quarter and a quarter make a half
verification: Here is a simple truth about anything that spins: if you rotate something a bit, then rotate it some more, the total is just the two angles added together. A quarter-turn plus a quarter-turn is a half-turn; spin all the way round and you are back to the start. It sounds obvious, but it is the same neat law behind gears meshing, clock hands, the phases of alternating current, and how light's polarization combines. Angles add. The engine confirmed it, down to Euler's beautiful e-to-the-i-theta.
wisdom: While the earth remaineth, seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night shall not cease (Genesis 8:22). The great turnings come round and add up faithfully, season upon season; the wheel of the year keeps its promise.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. Rotations compose by adding angles (the circle group); e^(ia)e^(ib)=e^(i(a+b)).

title: The sum of squares is one form everywhere -- distance, the size of a complex number, and energy all add squares
situation: One small algebraic shape, a-squared plus b-squared, recurs across the whole of mathematics and physics. In GEOMETRY it is Pythagoras: legs 3 and 4 give hypotenuse 5, because 9 plus 16 is 25; the straight-line DISTANCE across a plane is the square root of the sum of the squared steps (6 and 8 give 10). In the COMPLEX numbers it is the modulus, the 'size' of a number: the magnitude of 3 + 4i is 5, and its square is exactly the real part squared plus the imaginary part squared. In PHYSICS it is energy: the energy or intensity of a wave goes as the square of its amplitude (double the amplitude, four times the energy), kinetic energy goes as the square of speed, and the spread of data (variance) is a mean of squares. Wherever a quantity is built from independent components and you need its true 'length', you add their squares and take the root. The sum of squares is the signature of Euclidean distance, and it appears wherever space, or something like space, is measured.
use: To find the size of anything made of independent parts -- a diagonal, the magnitude of a complex number or a vector, the total energy of combined waves, the overall error from independent sources -- square the parts, add them, and take the root. This is why independent errors add 'in quadrature', why a hypotenuse is never the plain sum of the legs, and why doubling a signal's amplitude quadruples its power. Independent magnitudes combine by the sum of squares.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 5/5. The same sum-of-squares across domains: Pythagoras 3^2+4^2=5^2 (a control claiming 7^2 MISMATCHED); plane distance sqrt(6^2+8^2)=10; complex modulus |3+4i|=5 and |3+4i|^2=25 (= real^2 + imag^2); energy as amplitude^2 (doubling amplitude gives 4x energy). One quadratic form is distance, magnitude, and energy alike.

domain_results.note: Pythagoras: 3^2 + 4^2 = 5^2
the size of 3+4i is 5 -- the modulus is Pythagoras
straight-line distance across a plane: sqrt(6^2+8^2)=10
|z|^2 = real^2 + imaginary^2 = 25
energy goes as amplitude squared: double it, quadruple the energy

wisdom: The holy city is measured with care, foursquare and exact, that nothing be amiss. 'And the city lieth foursquare...he measured the city with the reed' (Revelation 21:16). The sum of squares is the measuring reed of ordinary space -- the one true length of all that is built of separate parts.

title: To find the true size of something built of parts, add their squares and take the root
verification: How far is it straight across a field if you walk 6 one way and 8 the other? Not 14 -- it is 10, the square root of 6-squared plus 8-squared. That same little rule (square them, add, take the root) gives the size of a complex number, the energy stored in a wave, and the combined error of several independent measurements. It is Pythagoras, quietly at work far beyond triangles. The engine confirmed it across geometry, complex numbers, and energy.
wisdom: Just balances, just weights, a just ephah, and a just hin, shall ye have (Leviticus 19:36). A true measure is a righteous thing; the sum of squares is the just measure of distance, giving each part its honest weight in the whole.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. The sum of squares (square, add, root) gives distance, complex magnitude, and energy alike -- Pythagoras everywhere.

title: A gear train trades speed for force by the ratio of its teeth -- mechanical advantage you can count
situation: A gear is the simplest machine for trading speed against force, and the trade is set by a number you can literally count: the ratio of the teeth. A 12-tooth gear driving a 36-tooth one turns the big gear at one-third the speed but with three times the torque -- the gear ratio is 36 over 12, which is 3. Speed and torque trade INVERSELY, so their product, the power, is conserved: the speed-ratio times the torque-ratio is exactly 1, nothing created or lost (in the idealized, frictionless count). Stages MULTIPLY: two 3-to-1 pairs in series give 9 to 1. This is why a low gear climbs a hill (much torque, little speed) and a high gear cruises (much speed, little torque), why a hand drill spins the bit faster than the crank, and why a clock's wheels divide one turn of the escapement down to the slow sweep of the hour hand. The gear is the lever made to go round and round: counted teeth, conserved power.
use: To pick a gear ratio, count teeth: output speed is input speed times the ratio of input-teeth to output-teeth, and torque is the inverse -- you cannot gain both, because power is conserved. Chain stages to multiply the ratio. The same trade governs pulleys, levers, and bicycle sprockets: speed for force, always at constant power. To climb, gear DOWN (more teeth out); to run fast, gear UP. The reliable working of every gearbox, clock, and bicycle is the demonstration that the law is true.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 5/5. The gear ratio is the teeth ratio: 36/12 = 3 (a control claiming 4 MISMATCHED). The driven gear turns inversely, 12/36 = 1/3 as fast, and its torque is three times (10 Nm becomes 30 Nm). Power is conserved: speed-ratio times torque-ratio = 1. Stages multiply: two 3:1 pairs give 9:1.

domain_results.note: the gear ratio is simply the ratio of teeth: 36/12 = 3
the larger gear turns inversely -- one-third the speed
speed-ratio times torque-ratio = 1: power conserved, nothing for nothing
stages multiply: two 3:1 pairs make 9:1
torque is multiplied by the ratio: 10 Nm becomes 30 Nm

wisdom: Strength and swiftness are not given together, but each in its turn and measure. 'I returned, and saw under the sun, that the race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong' (Ecclesiastes 9:11). The gear teaches the same: gain in force is loss in speed, and the wise choose which they need.

title: Gears trade speed for strength by counting teeth -- you cannot have more of both
verification: A gear is the oldest trick for swapping speed and force. Pair a small gear with a big one and the big one turns slower but pushes harder, by exactly the ratio of their teeth -- a 12 driving a 36 turns a third as fast with triple the muscle. You never get both; what you gain in force you lose in speed, because the power stays the same. It is why a bicycle's low gear crawls you up a hill and the high gear flies you down the flat, and why a clock's gears turn one fast wheel into the slow creep of the hour hand. The engine confirmed the counting.
wisdom: And he said unto me, My grace is sufficient for thee: for my strength is made perfect in weakness (2 Corinthians 12:9). The low gear looks weak and slow, yet there its strength is greatest; what seems the lesser way often carries the heavier load.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. A gear train's ratio is its teeth ratio; speed and torque trade inversely at constant power.

title: A Markov chain forgets its past and settles to the eigenvector of its transition matrix
situation: A Markov chain is a process with no memory: the chance of the next state depends ONLY on the present state, not on the road that led there. Its entire behaviour lives in a TRANSITION MATRIX whose rows are probabilities summing to 1 (a 'stochastic' matrix) -- a linear map that pushes a probability distribution one step forward. Multiply by it again for two steps (the Chapman-Kolmogorov rule: the two-step matrix is the square of the one-step matrix). The deep result is EQUILIBRIUM: run the chain long enough and, whatever the start, the distribution settles to a single STATIONARY distribution -- and that distribution is exactly the EIGENVECTOR of the transition matrix with eigenvalue 1. Every stochastic matrix has 1 as an eigenvalue (the rows summing to 1 force it); the OTHER eigenvalues are smaller than 1 in size, so the parts of any starting state that differ from equilibrium DECAY away step by step, and the chain forgets where it began. This single idea -- a memoryless step, a matrix, an eigenvector at rest -- runs Google's PageRank, weather and queueing models, board games, genetics, and the text-prediction behind language tools. Randomness under a fixed rule has a destiny, and it is an eigenvector.
use: To model anything that hops between states with fixed odds -- customers between products, weather between days, a web-surfer between pages, a token between words -- write the transition matrix and find its eigenvector for eigenvalue 1; that is the long-run share of time in each state, no matter where you start. Square the matrix for two steps, raise it to the n-th power for n steps; the speed at which the chain forgets its start is set by the second-largest eigenvalue. (It holds for chains that can reach anywhere from anywhere -- 'ergodic'.)

bonds: memoryless<->the present screens off the past

The stationary distribution pi=(4/7,3/7) is fixed under P (a control with the wrong value MISMATCHED). The two-step transition is the matrix squared (Chapman-Kolmogorov: P^2[0][0] = 61/100).

'Forgetting those things which are behind, and reaching forth unto those things which are before, I press toward the mark' (Philippians 3:13-14). The memoryless chain is a faint image of it -- the past let go, a fixed end drawing all beginnings home.

title: For many things, where you go next depends only on where you are now -- and the long run has a fixed home
verification: A great deal of the world works without memory: tomorrow's weather leans on today's, not last month's; the next word in a sentence leans on the last few; a game piece's next square depends only on its current one. These are 'Markov chains.' The surprising gift is that if the rules stay fixed, the long-run pattern settles to the SAME steady mix no matter where you started -- a kind of destiny built quietly into the odds. The engine confirmed that steady mix, and that any departure from it fades away. It is how Google first ranked the whole web.
wisdom: Take therefore no thought for the morrow: for the morrow shall take thought for the things of itself. Sufficient unto the day is the evil thereof (Matthew 6:34). The memoryless chain lives in its present step; and even so the faithful are counselled to meet today, and trust the appointed end.
