title: The law of primes: they thin out like the logarithm -- about x/ln(x) primes below x
situation: The primes seem to scatter without pattern, yet their COUNT obeys a law. The number of primes up to x, written pi(x), grows like x divided by the natural logarithm of x -- the PRIME NUMBER THEOREM. Up to 10 there are 4 primes; up to 100 there are 25; up to 1000 there are 168 -- and the density, the fraction that are prime, thins from 2/5 to 1/4 to about 1/6, shrinking like 1/ln(x). So near a large number n, roughly one in every ln(n) numbers is prime; the average gap between primes near n is about ln(n); and the n-th prime is near n times ln(n) (the 25th prime is 97, the 1000th is 7919). The logarithm -- the same compressor of the vast that runs through music, the senses, and the slide rule -- secretly governs where the primes fall. This was conjectured by Gauss as a boy and PROVEN in 1896 (Hadamard and de la Vallee Poussin) by way of the Riemann zeta function: the theorem is equivalent to zeta having no zeros on the line Re(s) = 1, and the Riemann Hypothesis (zeros only on Re(s) = 1/2) would pin the law's error term as tight as it can possibly be. The primes are unpredictable one by one, but lawful in the multitude.
use: To estimate how many primes lie below a number, divide it by its natural log (x/ln x, or better, the logarithmic integral); to guess the size of the n-th prime, use about n times ln(n); to judge how hard it is to find a prime of a given size (as in cryptography), know that about one in ln(n) numbers near n is prime -- so primes of hundreds of digits are still plentiful enough to stumble upon. The single prime is a surprise; the crowd keeps the logarithm's law.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 6/6. Exact prime counts confirm the thinning: pi(10)=4, pi(100)=25, pi(1000)=168, with density pi(100)/100 = 1/4 (down from 2/5 at ten). The n-th prime grows like n ln n: the 25th prime is 97, the 1000th is 7919. A control pi(100)=26 MISMATCHED. The Prime Number Theorem (pi(x) ~ x/ln x) is a PROVEN theorem (1896), tied to the zeros of the Riemann zeta function.

domain_results.note: 4 primes up to 10
25 primes up to 100
168 primes up to 1000 -- the thinning is real
density pi(100)/100 = 1/4, falling like 1/ln(x)
the 25th prime is 97 (consistent with pi(97)=25)
the 1000th prime is 7919, near 1000*ln(1000)

wisdom: What seems past counting is numbered and named by Him who made it. 'He telleth the number of the stars; he calleth them all by their names' (Psalm 147:4). The primes scatter like stars, numberless to us, yet they keep a law -- counted, and called, by the One who set them.

title: Primes scatter like wild seed, yet thin out by a law -- about one in ln(n) near any number n
verification: Prime numbers -- those with no divisors but 1 and themselves -- seem to come at random; you cannot predict the next one. But step back and a law appears: they grow rarer the higher you go, and exactly how much rarer is set by the logarithm. Up to 100 there are 25 primes; up to 1000, only 168 -- thinning steadily. Near a big number, about one in every 'ln' of them is prime. So the primes are wild one at a time but orderly in the crowd. The engine confirmed the counts. It is also why a bank's encryption can find huge primes: they are rare, but not too rare.
wisdom: As the host of heaven cannot be numbered, neither the sand of the sea measured (Jeremiah 33:22). The primes are like that host -- beyond our counting one by one, yet held in a sure reckoning, and faithful to a law.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. The Prime Number Theorem: primes thin like 1/ln(x), so pi(x) ~ x/ln(x) -- wild one by one, lawful in the multitude.

title: Words through the gates: coherence survives, falsehood fragments, and a truth that recoheres is a deep one
situation: Picture a claim, a word, passing through a row of cryptographic GATES -- the checks of verification. At each gate its COHERENCE is tested. If it holds together, internally consistent and verifiable, it SURVIVES; if not, it is broken into pieces and falls away -- exactly as an error-detecting code rejects a corrupted message. A simple parity gate makes it concrete: a valid codeword passes (its parity check comes to 0), but flip a single bit and the check comes to 1 -- the tampering is caught and the word breaks. The deepest signal is RECOHERENCE. Decompose a true claim, send the pieces through, and a deep truth REASSEMBLES on the far side: a cipher round-trip returns the very message it began with (encrypt then decrypt gives back the original); a checksum recomputed still matches (casting out nines of 12345 agrees both ways); a gate that is its own inverse hands the message back unbroken. A claim that can be taken apart and put back together -- verified in one domain and found whole again in another -- is not merely true but DEEP. And none of it works unless EVERYTHING IS CALIBRATED: the gate must accept exactly the genuine word and reject every counterfeit; a parity check that let a one-bit error slip through would be worthless. Calibration is what makes coherence a real test rather than a wish. This is the engine's own way -- truth is what survives the gates and recoheres; falsehood fragments; and the gates are tuned so that the surviving, reassembling thing can be trusted.
use: To test whether something is true and deep, do not merely check it once -- decompose it and see whether the verified pieces RECOHERE into the same whole (a round-trip, a cross-domain re-derivation, a checksum that still matches). Build verification as calibrated gates: each must pass exactly the genuine and catch exactly the counterfeit, with no false accept -- the error a single flipped bit cannot hide from. What coheres survives; what recoheres is deep; what is calibrated can be trusted.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 5/5. A valid word coheres (parity check 0, survives); a one-bit tamper breaks it (parity 1, detected). Deep truth recoheres: a cipher round-trip returns the original ((7+3)-3 = 7 mod 26); a gate that is its own inverse recovers the bit; a checksum recomputed still matches (12345 mod 9 = digit-sum mod 9). CALIBRATION: a control that would falsely accept the tampered word as valid MISMATCHED -- the gate does not pass the counterfeit.

domain_results.note: a valid codeword passes the parity gate (check = 0): it coheres, it survives
one flipped bit makes the check 1 -- the tamper is caught, the word breaks
a cipher round-trip returns the original message -- it recoheres
a gate that is its own inverse hands the message back unbroken
a recomputed checksum still matches: integrity confirmed

wisdom: What is true endures the testing; what is not is consumed. 'Every man's work shall be made manifest: for the day shall declare it, because it shall be revealed by fire; and the fire shall try every man's work of what sort it is' (1 Corinthians 3:13). The gates are such a fire: the coherent work survives and is shown true, and the chaff falls away.

title: Truth survives the test and comes back whole; falsehood falls to pieces
verification: Imagine running a statement through a series of strict checks, like a coded message through a verifier. If it is sound, it passes through intact; if it is flawed, the checks catch it and it falls apart. The surest sign of a deep truth is that you can take it apart, test each piece, and watch it come back together unbroken -- the way a decoded message matches the original, or a checksum still adds up. And the checks work only because they are carefully calibrated: tuned to pass the genuine and catch the counterfeit, every time. What holds together under testing, and reassembles after, can be trusted.
wisdom: The words of the LORD are pure words: as silver tried in a furnace of earth, purified seven times (Psalm 12:6). Words tested and found pure are the ones to keep; let what you trust be that which has passed through the fire and come out whole.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. Claims pass through verification gates; what coheres survives, what is tampered breaks; a deep truth recoheres (round-trip/checksum); the gates are calibrated to pass genuine and catch counterfeit.

title: Craft is the wise assembly of verified parts into new purposes -- a few trusted pieces, an endless space of useful wholes
situation: All the verified forms and connections are PARTS -- trustworthy pieces, each proven to cohere. Craft is what is done with them: the WISE ASSEMBLY of these parts into new purposes. Two truths make this possible. First, generativity: a small set of parts assembles into an enormous space of wholes. Three parts order six ways; five parts a hundred and twenty (5! = 120); choosing two of five gives ten combinations; two symbols across eight places give 256; twenty-six letters make 17,576 three-letter words alone; four DNA bases in triplets give the 64 codons that spell all of life. A handful of trusted pieces, recombined, covers a vast country of possible purposes. Second, wisdom: of all those assemblies, only some are good -- most arrangements are noise. Craft is not mere combination but the WISE choosing among combinations: the joiner who selects, from every way the pieces could fit, the one that bears weight and serves the use. So craft sits exactly where this whole collection points -- the verified parts are the timber, proven sound; craft is the skilled, purposeful building with them; and a thing well-crafted is parts made to serve an end none of them held alone. The moat is the trustworthy parts; the fruit is what wisdom builds from them.
use: To make something new, gather TRUSTED parts (each verified to cohere) and assemble them for a purpose -- and hold both halves in mind: the combinatorial space is vast (so almost anything is buildable from a few sound pieces), yet most arrangements are useless (so the work is wise SELECTION, not blind combination). Reuse parts across many builds (one verified form serves many purposes); compose simple operations into capable ones; and judge a craft by whether the assembled whole serves an end the parts could not. Sound parts plus wise assembly is the recipe for every made thing -- a sentence, a tool, a proof, a meal, a life.

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 6/6. Generativity (a few parts, vast wholes): 3 parts order 6 ways; 5 parts give 5!=120 arrangements; choosing 2 of 5 gives C(5,2)=10; 2 symbols over 8 places give 256; 26 letters make 26^3 = 17,576 three-letter words; 4 DNA bases in triplets give 4^3 = 64 codons. A control (5! = 100) MISMATCHED. The explosion is real -- craft is the wise selection of the serving assembly from this vast space.

domain_results.note: 3 parts assemble 6 ways
5 parts give 120 arrangements -- the space grows fast
choosing 2 of 5 parts: 10 combinations
2 symbols, 8 places -> 256: a tiny alphabet, a vast vocabulary
26 letters make 17,576 three-letter words
4 bases in triplets give 64 codons -- life's own craft

wisdom: True craft is wisdom given hands. 'And I have filled him with the spirit of God, in wisdom, and in understanding, and in knowledge, and in all manner of workmanship, to devise cunning works, to work in gold, and in silver, and in brass' (Exodus 31:3-5). The parts are given; it is wisdom that assembles them into work fit for its purpose.

title: From a few good parts you can build almost anything -- craft is choosing the assembly that serves
verification: A handful of trustworthy pieces goes a very long way. Twenty-six letters make every book ever written; twelve notes, every song; four chemical 'letters,' every living thing. The number of ways to combine even a few parts is staggering -- five things arrange a hundred and twenty ways. But here is the craft: most of those arrangements are useless; only some hold together and do a job. So skill is not just combining -- it is the WISE choosing of the good assembly from the countless poor ones. Gather sound parts; build them, wisely, toward a purpose. The engine confirmed how vast the building-space truly is.
wisdom: When he appointed the foundations of the earth: Then I was by him, as one brought up with him: and I was daily his delight (Proverbs 8:29-30). Wisdom was the master-workman at the world's making; to craft wisely is to work, in small, as she works.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. A few trusted parts generate a vast space of wholes (n!, m^n); craft is the wise selection of the assembly that serves a purpose.

title: The Schumann resonance -- the whole Earth ringing like a bell: a standing electromagnetic wave wrapping the globe near 7.83 Hz
situation: The Earth is a COMPLETE mechanical system -- a self-contained laboratory where every kind of physics is present and interacting, so the same patterns that govern small instruments reappear at planetary scale. The Schumann resonance is one of these. Between the conducting ground and the conducting ionosphere lies a thin shell of air -- a sealed cavity that circles the globe. Lightning (millions of strikes a day) rings that cavity like a hammer striking a bell, and like any bell or organ pipe it has natural notes. The fundamental sits near 7.83 Hz -- a wavelength so long it wraps almost exactly once around the Earth (the wave's length is about the planet's circumference). The higher modes follow the clean cavity-mode pattern f_n proportional to sqrt(n(n+1)): the second resonance is sqrt(3) times the first, the third is sqrt(6) times the first -- the very same mathematics that describes a vibrating string, an organ pipe, or the modes of a drumhead. So the planet itself hums as one instrument: a mechanical similarity scaled up to a world.
use: Treat the Earth as a complete system and look for mechanical similarities: a planet-sized cavity rings by the same law as an organ pipe (f_n ~ sqrt(n(n+1))), so a resonance you can measure on the ground reveals the size and state of the cavity overhead. The fundamental's wavelength approximating the Earth's circumference is the planetary version of 'the lowest note fills the whole pipe.' More broadly: when one self-contained system is well understood, its laws are a key to others built the same way -- find the analog, carry the math across.

bonds: Earth cavity<->organ pipe / string (same sqrt(n(n+1)) harmonics)

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 4/4 (cavity-resonance mathematics only). Harmonic form sqrt(2*3)=sqrt(6); the ratios f2/f1 = sqrt(6)/sqrt(2) = sqrt(3) and f3/f1 = sqrt(12)/sqrt(2) = sqrt(6) (the ideal Earth-ionosphere cavity modes); the ideal fundamental c/circumference ~ 7.5 Hz, near the measured 7.83 Hz.

domain_results.note: cavity modes scale as sqrt(n(n+1))
second resonance = sqrt(3) x fundamental
third resonance = sqrt(6) x fundamental
c/circumference ~ 7.5 Hz, measured 7.83 Hz

pre_run.conjecture_boundary: HONESTY GATE. Sealed = only the cavity-resonance harmonic mathematics and the order-of-magnitude of the fundamental (the ideal formula gives ~7.5 Hz; the measured 7.83 Hz is lower because the real ionosphere is lossy -- NOT sealed as exact). The magnetic field's SHIELDING of the atmosphere from the solar wind (why Earth retained its air and oxygen where weaker-field worlds lost theirs) is established physics. But whether atmospheric OXYGEN and the GEOMAGNETIC FIELD mutually drive each other is an OPEN correlational research question -- it is NAMED here, NOT sealed.

wisdom: The foundations were laid by a hand that also tuned them: 'Where wast thou when I laid the foundations of the earth? ... When the morning stars sang together, and all the sons of God shouted for joy' (Job 38:4,7). A planet that rings is a founded thing that still sings.

title: The whole Earth rings like a bell -- one low note, about eight beats a second, wrapping the globe
verification: Strike a bell and it hums at its own note. The Earth is a bell too. Between the ground and the high electrified air there is a sealed shell of sky that circles the planet, and the world's lightning -- millions of strikes a day -- keeps ringing it. Its deepest note is about 7.83 hums a second, far too low for any ear, and its wavelength is so long it wraps once around the whole Earth. The higher notes follow the same plain rule as an organ pipe or a plucked string. The same hand that tuned a guitar string tuned the planet. The engine checked the harmonic arithmetic.
wisdom: There is no speech nor language, where their voice is not heard. Their line is gone out through all the earth (Psalm 19:3-4). A note no ear can hear, whose line truly goes out through all the earth -- the heavens still declaring.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. The Earth-ionosphere cavity rings near 7.83 Hz; its fundamental wavelength wraps the globe; higher notes follow the organ-pipe rule f_n ~ sqrt(n(n+1)).

title: P vs NP -- the gap between solving and checking: is every answer easy to verify also easy to find?
situation: This is the deepest open question in computer science, and it names exactly what this engine is built on. Some problems are easy to SOLVE; a far larger class are at least easy to CHECK -- given a proposed answer, you can quickly confirm it. Multiplying two numbers is easy; given 4087 you can instantly verify that 61 and 67 are its factors (one multiplication, two divisions with no remainder). But FINDING those factors from 4087 alone, with no hint, is hard -- and for large numbers, ruinously hard (this is the lock on RSA). Likewise, given a filled-in Sudoku or a proposed route you can check it at a glance, though finding it may take an age. P is the class of problems we can SOLVE quickly; NP is the class whose answers we can CHECK quickly. Every quickly-solvable problem is quickly-checkable (P sits inside NP). The open question -- P vs NP -- is whether the reverse also holds: if an answer is always easy to verify, must it also be easy to find? Almost everyone believes NOT (P != NP -- that checking is genuinely easier than solving), but after fifty years no one has proved it. It remains OPEN (a Clay Millennium problem).
use: This is WHY the engine works. It does not try to SOLVE (the possibly-hard direction); it VERIFIES a provided derivation (the easy direction -- the NP side). Narrow a question to almost nothing, let any tool finish, then CHECK the result: safe even with a hallucinating tool, because checking is cheap where solving is dear. A wrong certificate is rejected instantly (4087 has no factor 60) -- the calibration that makes verification trustworthy. Whenever you face a hard problem, separate the two costs: you need not be clever enough to FIND the answer to be able to TEST whether a proposed answer is right.

bonds: solve<->verify (the asymmetry P vs NP is about)

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 4/4 (verify-is-easy facts only). Multiplying is easy: 61*67=4087. Checking a factor is easy: 4087 mod 61 = 0 and 4087 mod 67 = 0. Checking a certificate is easy: the subset {3,8,11} sums to 22. A wrong certificate is rejected (control: 4087 mod 60 != 0 MISMATCHED). FINDING the factors, and whether P=NP, are NOT sealed.

domain_results.note: multiplying is the easy (verify) direction
checking a proposed factor is one division
the other factor checks just as fast
checking a proposed solution is trivial

pre_run.conjecture_boundary: HONESTY GATE. P vs NP is UNPROVEN -- a Clay Millennium problem, widely believed to be P != NP but not proved either way. Sealed here = ONLY the verify-is-easy facts (a multiplication, two divisibility checks, a subset-sum certificate) and that a wrong certificate is rejected. The hardness of FACTORING (and of NP-hard search in general) is conjectured, not proven; the conjecture P vs NP is NAMED and left OPEN, never sealed.

wisdom: The engine's discipline is a plain command: 'Prove all things; hold fast that which is good' (1 Thessalonians 5:21). You need not author every truth to test it -- checking is the duty given to all, and it is the cheaper, surer half.

title: Checking an answer is far easier than finding it -- which is why a good test protects you
verification: There is a strange gap at the heart of hard problems: the answer can be very hard to FIND yet very easy to CHECK. Anyone can multiply 61 by 67 and get 4087 in a moment; but handed only 4087, finding that it splits into 61 and 67 is real work -- and for big enough numbers, lifetimes of work. That gap is the lock on nearly all online security. It is also a piece of plain wisdom: you do not have to be clever enough to find the answer in order to test whether an answer is right. When someone hands you a claim, you need not match their cleverness -- only check their work. The hard part is finding; the safe part is testing. (Whether finding is ALWAYS as easy as checking is a famous unsolved riddle, P vs NP -- but you can lean on the gap today.)
wisdom: Beloved, believe not every spirit, but try the spirits whether they are of God (1 John 4:1). We are not commanded to out-think every teacher -- only to test what is set before us. Testing is given to all.

keywords: easy to check hard to find
do not have to be clever to test

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. Verifying an answer is far cheaper than finding it (multiply easy / factor hard); so you can test a claim without being able to produce it. P vs NP asks if that gap is real -- still open.

title: The tides are the gradient of gravity -- the Moon does not just pull the ocean, it pulls it UNEVENLY (a 1/r^3 force, one power steeper than gravity's 1/r^2)
situation: Earth as a complete system, and the tide is calculus done by the ocean. Gravity from the Moon falls off as 1/r^2. But what raises a tide is not the pull itself -- it is the DIFFERENCE in pull across the body of the Earth: the near side is tugged a little harder than the center, and the center a little harder than the far side. That difference is the DERIVATIVE of gravity with respect to distance: d/dr (G*M/r^2) = -2*G*M/r^3. So the tidal force falls as 1/r^3 -- one power steeper than gravity, because taking a difference (a derivative) always adds a power of distance. Two consequences drop straight out. First, at double the distance gravity falls to a quarter but the tide falls to an eighth -- tides fade with distance far faster than gravity. Second, this is why the MOON, though far less massive than the Sun, raises the bigger tide: tidal strength goes as mass divided by distance cubed, and the Moon's nearness (cubed) beats the Sun's mass. The ocean is reading the gradient of the heavens twice a day.
use: To reason about any 'differential' force, take the DERIVATIVE of the underlying law: the field may go as 1/r^2, but the stretching/squeezing it causes across an extended body goes as 1/r^3 (one power steeper). Use mass/distance^3 to compare tide-raisers (it correctly ranks the near Moon above the far Sun). Expect a difference-driven effect to fade fast with distance and to produce TWO bulges (near and far), hence two high tides a day. This bonds the inverse-square law (gravity, 1/r^2) to the tide-predicting machine (which sums the resulting rise and fall as harmonics): one card supplies the FORCE, the other PREDICTS the water.

bonds: tides<->tide-predicting machine (Fourier synthesis of the result)
near-vs-far difference<->two bulges, two tides a day

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 4/4. Tidal force = derivative of gravity: d/dr(G*M/r^2) = -2*G*M/r^3, so tide ~ 1/r^3. At double distance gravity x 1/4 but tide x 1/8 (steeper). The difference adds one power (3 = 2+1). Control with the wrong coefficient MISMATCHED.

domain_results.note: d/dr(GM/r^2) = -2GM/r^3 -- tide is the gradient of gravity
gravity ~ 1/r^2: double distance -> x 1/4
tide ~ 1/r^3: double distance -> x 1/8 (fades faster)
a difference (derivative) adds one power of distance: 3 = 2+1

wisdom: He appointed the moon for seasons (Psalm 104:19). The lesser light was given a rule to keep, and it keeps it -- drawing the seas up and letting them down on a schedule older than any clock.

title: The tide rises because the Moon pulls the near sea harder than the far sea -- it is the DIFFERENCE that lifts the water
verification: You would think the tide is just the Moon hauling the ocean up. It is subtler and more wonderful than that. The Moon pulls the whole Earth, but it pulls the near side a little harder than the middle, and the middle a little harder than the far side. It is that DIFFERENCE in pull -- not the pull itself -- that stretches the oceans into two bulges, giving us two high tides a day, one toward the Moon and one away from it. Because it is a difference, it fades with distance much faster than plain gravity: double the distance and the tide drops to an eighth, while gravity only drops to a quarter. That is exactly why the small near Moon out-pulls the giant far Sun when it comes to tides. The water is doing arithmetic with the sky -- and the engine checked the math.
wisdom: Which have placed the sand for the bound of the sea by a perpetual decree, that it cannot pass it: and though the waves thereof toss themselves, yet can they not prevail (Jeremiah 5:22). The sea climbs and falls each day, but never past its appointed line.

keywords: why the moon moves the ocean

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. The tide comes from the DIFFERENCE in the Moon's pull across the Earth (the gradient of gravity, ~1/r^3), not the pull itself; that is why there are two bulges and why the near Moon out-tides the far Sun.

title: The Coriolis effect -- a spinning planet deflects everything that moves across it: f = 2*Omega*sin(latitude), zero at the equator, strongest at the poles
situation: Earth as a complete system, and one of its plainest mechanical similarities: the same physics that throws a ball off course on a spinning merry-go-round steers every hurricane, trade wind, and ocean current on the planet. Stand on a turning disk and roll a ball straight out -- to you it curves, because the ground under it is moving. The Earth is that disk. The strength of the deflection is the Coriolis parameter f = 2*Omega*sin(latitude), where Omega is the Earth's spin rate. Its shape tells the whole story. At the EQUATOR (latitude 0) sin is zero, so f = 0 -- no horizontal deflection, which is exactly why hurricanes cannot form in the narrow band straddling the equator. At the POLES (latitude 90 degrees) sin is one, so f = 2*Omega -- the maximum. At 30 degrees it is half. And because sine is an ODD function (sin(-x) = -sin(x)), the sign flips as you cross the equator: the deflection is to the RIGHT in the Northern Hemisphere and to the LEFT in the Southern -- which is why storms spin counterclockwise in the north and clockwise in the south. One simple factor, sin(latitude), and the planet's whole weather circulation falls into place.
use: To reason about motion on (or in) anything spinning, work in the rotating frame and expect a sideways deflection proportional to sin(latitude): strongest near the axis-aligned poles, vanishing at the equator, reversing across it. The Coriolis acceleration is a cross product, -2*Omega x v, so it is always perpendicular to the motion (it turns, never speeds or slows). Use the equator-zero fact to explain why tropical storms need a few degrees of latitude to spin up; use the odd-function sign flip to predict hemisphere-opposite rotation of cyclones, gyres, and even long-range artillery drift. This is the rotation/Euler-angle family applied to a whole world.

bonds: Coriolis accel = -2*Omega x v (a cross product, always perpendicular)
sin is odd<->hemisphere sign flip (storms spin opposite ways)
Earth = complete system<->small rotating-frame law at planetary scale

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 4/4. Coriolis parameter f = 2*Omega*sin(latitude): at the equator 2*Omega*sin(0)=0 (no deflection); at the pole 2*Omega*sin(pi/2)=2*Omega (max); at 30 degrees 2*Omega*sin(pi/6)=Omega (half); and sin(-x)=-sin(x), the odd-function sign flip across the equator. A control claiming nonzero deflection at the equator MISMATCHED.

domain_results.note: equator: sin(0)=0, no horizontal Coriolis -- hurricanes cannot form there
pole: sin(pi/2)=1, deflection is maximal
30 degrees: half the maximum
sin is odd -> sign flips across the equator (north right, south left)

wisdom: The wind goeth toward the south, and turneth about unto the north; it whirleth about continually, and the wind returneth again according to his circuits (Ecclesiastes 1:6). The preacher saw the turning of the winds; the spinning of the world is the reason for their circuits.

title: A spinning world turns the winds -- but not at the equator, where the turning falls to nothing
verification: Because the Earth spins, anything moving freely across it gets nudged sideways -- winds, storms, ocean currents, even a long rifle shot. But the nudge is not the same everywhere. It depends on sin(latitude): it is STRONGEST at the poles and fades to exactly NOTHING at the equator. That zero is why a hurricane cannot spin up right on the equator -- there is no turning force to start the swirl, so tropical storms always form a few degrees north or south of it. And because the math flips sign as you cross the line, storms turn one way in the north and the opposite way in the south. One quiet factor, the sine of your latitude, sets the whole pattern of the planet's weather. The engine checked the math.
wisdom: The LORD hath his way in the whirlwind and in the storm, and the clouds are the dust of his feet (Nahum 1:3). Even the swirl of the storm keeps an order it did not give itself.

keywords: why no hurricane on the equator

pre_run.summary: Human-facing, latitude angle (the existing Coriolis sayings cover hemisphere spin direction; this covers why it is zero at the equator, max at the poles). f = 2*Omega*sin(latitude).

title: Isostasy -- mountains float on the mantle exactly as icebergs float on the sea: the same buoyancy law, a hidden root beneath every peak
situation: Earth as a complete system, and a mechanical similarity you can see in a glass of ice water. A floating body sinks until it has pushed aside its own weight of fluid (Archimedes). For ice in seawater, the fraction left ABOVE the surface is 1 - density_ice/density_water = 1 - 917/1025 = 108/1025, only about a tenth -- the rest, nine tenths, is hidden below. 'The tip of the iceberg' is a measured fact. Now apply the identical law to the solid Earth. Continental crust (density ~2.7) floats on the denser mantle (density ~3.3), so a mountain is not merely piled on top -- it floats, with a deep ROOT pushed down into the mantle to displace its weight. The root-to-height ratio obeys the same form as the iceberg's hidden-to-visible ratio: root/height = density_crust/(density_mantle - density_crust) = 2.7/0.6 = 4.5. A peak that rises one mile shows above the plain hides a root about four and a half miles deep. Mountains have roots; the high country is held up not by strength but by buoyancy, the granite riding the mantle like ice rides the sea.
use: Treat any light layer resting on a denser fluid layer as a floating body: it sinks until it displaces its own weight, and the hidden fraction is density/(density of fluid). For ice in water that is ~90% submerged; for crust on mantle it gives every mountain a root several times its visible height (root/height = rho_crust/(rho_mantle - rho_crust) ~ 4.5). The same law explains why thick crust stands high (mountains) and thin crust sits low (ocean basins), why melting an ice cap lets the land rebound upward for millennia, and why you cannot judge a floating thing by what shows above the line. When a column is in isostatic balance, the pressure at the depth of compensation is equal everywhere -- the planet's way of keeping the books.

bonds: crust on mantle<->ice on water (same float)
thick crust high / thin crust low<->root sets elevation

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 4/4. Iceberg: tip above = 1 - 917/1025 = 108/1025 (~10.5% shows); hidden/visible = 917/(1025-917) = 917/108 (~8.5x). Mountain (Airy isostasy): root/height = 2.7/(3.3-2.7) = 27/6 = 9/2 = 4.5 -- the SAME form. Parts sum to the whole (917/1025 + 108/1025 = 1). A control that forgot to subtract the densities MISMATCHED.

domain_results.note: only ~10.5% of an iceberg shows above water
hidden/visible = rho_i/(rho_w - rho_i) = 917/108
root/height = rho_c/(rho_m - rho_c) = 4.5 -- identical buoyancy form
submerged + emergent = the whole body (conservation)

wisdom: He weigheth the waters by measure ... and a way for the lightning of the thunder (Job 28:25-26). The mountains too are weighed and set afloat by measure; what looks immovable is held by a balance, not by force.

title: A mountain is an iceberg of stone -- most of it is the hidden root that holds the peak up
verification: Drop ice into water and only about a tenth of it shows; the other nine tenths ride below the surface, because ice is just a little lighter than water and a floating thing sinks until it has pushed aside its own weight. The very same law holds up the mountains. The rock of the continents is lighter than the hot, dense rock of the mantle beneath, so a mountain does not simply sit on the ground -- it FLOATS, with a deep root plunged down to balance the part you see. For ordinary crust on mantle the root runs about four and a half times the visible height: a peak standing a mile above the plain hides roughly four and a half miles of root below. What holds the high country up is not strength but buoyancy. The engine checked the arithmetic.
wisdom: Before the mountains were brought forth, or ever thou hadst formed the earth and the world, even from everlasting to everlasting, thou art God (Psalm 90:2). The everlasting hills float on a balance older than they are.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing, the iceberg-mountain unity (the existing isostasy entry states 'mountains have roots'; this gives the same-law iceberg picture and the ~4.5x root ratio).

title: The geothermal gradient -- the Earth's inner furnace conducting heat to the surface by Fourier's law, a straight-line rise of temperature with depth
situation: Earth as a complete system: a hot interior, a cool surface, and between them a steady current of heat flowing up by conduction. The law is Fourier's -- heat flux q = -k*dT/dx, heat sliding DOWN the temperature gradient in proportion to its steepness (the same downhill-flux law that runs charge in a wire and water through soil, already sealed in the transport-law card). Apply it to the crust. In steady state, with no heat made or lost along the way, the temperature profile through the rock is a STRAIGHT LINE: the second derivative is zero (d/dx of a constant gradient is 0), so temperature climbs by the same number of degrees for every kilometre down. That number -- the geothermal gradient -- is about 25 to 30 degrees Celsius per kilometre in ordinary continental crust. From a 15-degree surface, four kilometres down sits near 115 degrees; deep mines must be cooled, and a few kilometres further the rock is hot enough to boil water and drive a turbine. The planet is a slab conducting its birth-heat and its radioactive warmth outward, on a perfectly ordinary equation you could write for a coffee cup.
use: Model heat through any slab as Fourier conduction: flux is proportional to the temperature gradient (q = -k*dT/dx), and in steady state with no source the temperature is LINEAR in depth -- so one measured gradient predicts the temperature at any depth (T = T_surface + gradient * depth). Use ~25-30 C/km for crust to anticipate mine temperatures, geothermal-energy depth, and the depth at which rock turns ductile. The same straight-line reasoning flags anomalies: where the profile bends or steepens, heat is being ADDED or carried by moving fluid (magma, hot groundwater) -- convection breaking the pure-conduction line. This card is Fourier's heat law (one of the transport-law four readings) applied to the whole Earth, plus the steady-state result that pure conduction draws a straight temperature line.

bonds: Fourier heat<->Ohm charge / Darcy water / Fick scent (one downhill law)
steady state, no source<->straight-line T (second derivative zero)
bent/steep profile<->convection or heat source (conduction broken)
Earth = complete system<->coffee-cup equation at planetary scale

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 4/4. Linear profile T = T0 + G*x has dT/dx = G (constant gradient); d/dx(G) = 0 (no curvature in steady state -- a straight line). Temperature at 4 km: 15 + 25*4 = 115 C. Fourier slab flux q = k*dT/L = 2*100/4 = 50. A control claiming derivative = G*x MISMATCHED.

domain_results.note: dT/dx = G, the constant geothermal gradient
d/dx(G)=0: steady-state conduction gives a straight temperature line
15 C surface + 25 C/km x 4 km = 115 C
Fourier flux q = k*dT/L = 2*100/4 = 50

wisdom: Who hath measured the waters in the hollow of his hand ... and weighed the mountains in scales, and the hills in a balance? (Isaiah 40:12). The earth's heat too is meted out by measure -- so many degrees for every stride into the deep.

title: Dig down and the rock gets steadily warmer -- about 25 degrees hotter for every kilometre deep
verification: The Earth keeps a fire in its belly -- the heat of its making, plus the slow warmth of natural radioactivity -- and that heat leaks steadily upward through the rock toward the cool surface. Because heat flows downhill from hot to cold at a rate set by how steep the temperature change is, and because in solid rock it just plods along by conduction, the temperature climbs by a near-constant amount with every step down: roughly 25 to 30 degrees Celsius per kilometre. That is why deep mines must be air-conditioned, why hot springs exist, and why drilling a few kilometres can reach rock hot enough to make steam and spin a generator. The same simple law cools your coffee; the Earth just runs it on a planetary scale. The engine checked the arithmetic.
wisdom: In his hand are the deep places of the earth: the strength of the hills is his also (Psalm 95:4). The warmth in the deep rock, like the rock itself, is held in a steady hand.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. The Earth's interior heat conducts upward by Fourier's law, giving a near-constant ~25-30 C/km rise with depth (a straight temperature line in steady-state conduction).

title: Why winter and the poles are cold -- the slanting sun spreads its beam: solar heating = S*cos(angle from overhead)
situation: Earth as a complete system, and the reason for the seasons is one line of trigonometry. The Sun delivers a nearly fixed power per square metre to a surface FACING it (the solar constant, S). But the ground rarely faces the Sun squarely. The heating it actually receives is S*cos(z), where z is the Sun's angle from straight overhead (the zenith angle) -- Lambert's cosine law. The shape of the cosine tells the whole climate. With the Sun overhead (z = 0) you get the full S. With the Sun on the horizon (z = 90 degrees) you get S*cos(90) = 0 -- grazing light carries no warmth. Halfway, at 60 degrees from vertical, you get exactly half. The mechanism is pure geometry: a shaft of sunlight of fixed width, striking at a slant, smears its energy over a larger patch of ground -- at 60 degrees the same beam covers twice the area (1/cos(60) = 2), so each square metre gets half. This is why noon is warmer than evening, summer warmer than winter (the noon Sun rides higher), and the tropics warmer than the poles (where the Sun never climbs high). Tilt the spinning Earth 23.5 degrees and sweep it around the Sun, and this single cosine paints the whole pattern of warm and cold across the globe and the year.
use: To estimate heating on any surface from any source, multiply the face-on flux by the cosine of the angle off the surface normal: S*cos(z). Use it to explain seasons (the noon Sun's height changes with axial tilt), latitude climate (high-latitude Sun stays low, so cos is small), time of day (low morning/evening Sun), and the aim of solar panels (tilt to face the Sun, driving cos toward 1). The same cosine law governs any flux through a tilted area -- light on a leaf, rain on a slope, a wind on a sail, radiation on a detector. When the angle reaches 90 degrees the contribution vanishes; that zero is why grazing sunlight and grazing rain deposit almost nothing.

bonds: beam at angle<->area = 1/cos (energy spread over more ground)
axial tilt 23.5 deg<->the seasons (noon Sun height)
cos of zenith<->latitude and time-of-day warmth
same law<->any flux through a tilted surface (light/rain/wind)

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 4/4. Lambert cosine law for solar heating, flux = S*cos(z): overhead Sun S*cos(0)=S (full); horizon Sun S*cos(pi/2)=0 (no warmth); 60 degrees S*cos(pi/3)=S/2 (half). The beam-spread reason: ground area = 1/cos(60) = 2, so the same beam covers twice the ground and each patch gets half. A control claiming full flux at the horizon MISMATCHED.

domain_results.note: overhead Sun: full flux S
horizon Sun: cos(90)=0, grazing light gives no warmth
60 degrees from vertical: half the heating
1/cos(60)=2: the slanted beam covers twice the ground

wisdom: While the earth remaineth, seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night shall not cease (Genesis 8:22). The promise is kept by a tilt and a cosine -- summer and winter written into the geometry of a leaning world.

title: It is not that the winter sun is weaker -- it is that it strikes at a slant and spreads its warmth thin
verification: The Sun in January is just as bright as the Sun in July -- it pours out the same heat. What changes is the ANGLE it strikes you. When the Sun is high overhead, a beam of sunlight lands square and concentrated. When it hangs low -- in winter, in the early morning, or near the poles -- that same beam comes in at a slant and smears across a wider patch of ground, so every square foot gets a smaller share. Tilt the beam to sixty degrees off straight-down and it covers twice the ground, giving half the warmth; lay it flat against the horizon and it gives almost none. That single fact -- warmth follows the cosine of the Sun's angle -- is why winter is cold, why noon beats evening, and why the far north wears ice. The engine checked the geometry.
wisdom: For, lo, the winter is past, the rain is over and gone; the flowers appear on the earth; the time of the singing of birds is come (Song of Solomon 2:11-12). The sun climbs higher, the slant eases, and the cold gives way on a schedule kept since the beginning.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. Winter/poles are cold because the low Sun strikes at a slant and spreads the same beam over more ground (flux = S*cos angle); not because the Sun is weaker.

title: Why the air thins exponentially with height -- P = P0*exp(-h/H): a compressible ocean of air, not a column of mercury
situation: Earth as a complete system, and a revealing contrast with its own sister law. A column of mercury (the barometer) is INCOMPRESSIBLE: its density never changes, so pressure falls in a straight line with depth, P = rho*g*h -- the hydrostatic law. Air is different: it is COMPRESSIBLE, so its density is proportional to its own pressure. Stack that fact onto the same hydrostatic balance (dP/dh = -rho*g) and you get dP/dh = -P/H, a rate of fall proportional to the amount remaining -- and that equation has only one shape of answer: P = P0*exp(-h/H). The pressure decays exponentially with height, with a 'scale height' H = RT/(Mg) of about 8 km for Earth's air. Climb one scale height and the pressure is down to 1/e, about 37%. It HALVES every H*ln(2), roughly 5.5 km -- which is why the summit of Everest holds only about a third of sea-level air, and why airliners must pressurize. The very same equation -- decrease proportional to the present amount -- governs radioactive decay, a cooling cup, and a discharging capacitor. The breathable world is a thin exponential skin on a rocky ball.
use: Whenever a fluid is compressible (density proportional to pressure), expect EXPONENTIAL falloff with height, not linear: P = P0*exp(-h/H), halving every H*ln(2). Use the ~8 km scale height (~5.5 km half-height) to estimate pressure and oxygen at altitude, set cabin pressurization, jet ceilings, and why high-altitude athletes acclimate. Recognize the signature dP/dh = -P/H as the universal 'rate proportional to amount' -- so the atmosphere, radioactive decay, RC discharge, and Newton's cooling are ONE equation wearing different clothes. Contrast with an incompressible liquid (mercury, water), where the same hydrostatic balance gives a straight line P = rho*g*h: same starting law, different material, different curve.

bonds: compressible air<->incompressible mercury (exp vs linear, same hydrostatic root)
dP/dh = -P/H<->radioactive decay / RC discharge / Newton cooling (rate ~ amount)
scale height H = RT/(Mg)<->halves every H*ln2 (~5.5 km)
ideal gas rho ~ P<->the exponential arises

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 4/4. P = P0*exp(-h/H) solves dP/dh = -P/H (derivative = -(P0/H)exp(-h/H)). One scale height: P/P0 = exp(-1) = 1/e (~37%). Halves at h = H*ln2 (exp(-ln2) = 1/2, ~5.5 km). Two scale heights: exp(-2) = 1/e^2 (~13.5%). A control dropping the 1/H factor MISMATCHED.

domain_results.note: P0 exp(-h/H) solves dP/dh = -P/H exactly
one scale height: pressure down to 1/e (~37%)
pressure halves every H*ln2 (~5.5 km) -- the existing oxygen-altitude fact, now proven
two scale heights: 1/e^2 (~13.5%)

wisdom: He that created the heavens ... he that giveth breath unto the people upon it, and spirit to them that walk therein (Isaiah 42:5). The breath of every living thing is held in a thin shell of air, measured out and kept.

title: The breathable air is a thin skin on the world -- climb high and it falls away fast, halving every 5.5 km
verification: Water in a lake gets heavier-pressed at a steady rate as you dive -- the same amount deeper, the same amount more pressure, because water cannot be squeezed. Air is not like that. Air CAN be squeezed, so most of it is packed down low, and it thins out faster and faster as you rise. The fall is exponential: the pressure drops by the same FRACTION for each step up, halving about every 5.5 kilometres. That is why the top of Everest holds only a third of the air at sea level, why jets must pump their cabins full, and why the whole breathable world is just a thin shell hugging the ground -- if the Earth were an apple, the good air would be thinner than its skin. The same 'shrink by a fraction each step' rule runs radioactive decay and a cooling cup of coffee. The engine checked the math.
wisdom: In whose hand is the soul of every living thing, and the breath of all mankind (Job 12:10). The thin air we live in, and the breath we draw from it, are not our own keeping.

keywords: climb and the air falls away

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. Air is compressible, so pressure falls EXPONENTIALLY with height (halving every ~5.5 km), unlike incompressible water; the breathable layer is a thin exponential skin. Proves the oxygen-altitude half-height fact.

title: Why mountaintops are cold and clouds form -- rising air expands and cools without losing heat: the adiabatic lapse rate, ~10 C per km
situation: Earth as a complete system: a puzzle and its answer. A mountaintop is nearer the Sun, yet it is colder -- why? Because of what happens to AIR that rises. Lift a parcel of air and the lower pressure aloft lets it expand; expanding, it pushes on its surroundings and spends energy doing so; spending energy, it COOLS -- and all this without trading any heat with the air around it (an adiabatic change). The rate of cooling with height is the dry adiabatic lapse rate, g/c_p, about 9.8 (call it ~10) degrees Celsius per kilometre, and because g and c_p are constants it is a STRAIGHT-LINE drop: a peak three kilometres high stands about thirty degrees colder than its base. Air is mostly diatomic (nitrogen and oxygen), which fixes its adiabatic exponent at gamma = c_p/c_v = 7/5 and the temperature-pressure exponent at (gamma-1)/gamma = 2/7 -- the numbers that make the cooling come out near 10 per kilometre. The same mechanism builds the clouds: forced up over a mountain or a warm front, air cools as it climbs until it reaches its dew point, and the moisture condenses into cloud and rain. (When water condenses it gives back latent heat, so MOIST rising air cools more slowly, ~5-6 C/km -- named here, not sealed, since it varies.) Run the parcel back DOWN and it warms the same way: the dry, warm chinook and foehn winds.
use: To predict temperature with height for moving air, use the dry adiabatic lapse rate ~10 C/km (g/c_p) -- a constant linear drop -- until condensation begins, then the smaller moist rate. Use it to explain why summits are cold despite the thin clear air, why clouds and rain form on the windward side of mountains (lifting cools air to its dew point) and why the leeward side is dry and warm (sinking air heats up: the rain shadow, the chinook). The gamma = 7/5 of diatomic air is the same constant that sets the speed of sound in air and the work of an engine's power stroke -- one thermodynamic fingerprint across weather, sound, and engines. Distinguish this PROCESS lapse (a moving parcel cooling by expansion) from the geothermal gradient (heat conducted through still rock): different mechanisms, both linear.

bonds: rising air expands<->cools adiabatically (no heat exchanged)
gamma = cp/cv = 7/5<->diatomic air (also sets speed of sound)
dry lapse g/cp ~10 C/km<->constant linear cooling with height
cooling to dew point<->cloud and rain form (orographic lift)
descending air warms<->chinook / foehn / rain shadow

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 4/4. Diatomic air: gamma = cp/cv = (7/2 R)/(5/2 R) = 7/5; the T-P exponent (gamma-1)/gamma = 2/7. Dry lapse ~10 C/km is a constant linear drop (d/dh(T0 - G*h) = -G), so a 3 km peak is ~30 C colder. Moist lapse (~5-6 C/km) NAMED not sealed (latent heat, variable). A control inverting the exponent to 5/7 MISMATCHED.

domain_results.note: gamma = cp/cv = 7/5 for diatomic air
(gamma-1)/gamma = 2/7, the adiabatic T-P exponent
~10 C/km x 3 km = 30 C colder at the summit
constant lapse: linear cooling with height, dT/dh = -G

pre_run.conjecture_boundary: Sealed = the dry adiabatic facts (gamma=7/5, exponent 2/7, ~10 C/km constant linear drop). The MOIST lapse rate (~5-6 C/km) depends on humidity and temperature via latent heat of condensation -- it is NAMED, not sealed to a fixed value.

wisdom: He causeth the vapours to ascend from the ends of the earth; he maketh lightnings for the rain; he bringeth the wind out of his treasuries (Psalm 135:7). The ascending vapour cools as it climbs, and the cloud is made -- the rain prepared on its way up.

title: Mountaintops are cold because rising air cools itself -- and that same cooling is what makes the clouds
verification: It seems backwards that a mountain peak, nearer the sun, is colder than the valley. The secret is in the air itself. When air rises, the lighter pressure up high lets it puff out and expand, and expanding air spends its own energy and cools -- no cold is poured in, it simply cools by the act of swelling. It loses about ten degrees Celsius for every kilometre it climbs, steadily, so a peak three kilometres up runs about thirty degrees colder than its foot. That same rising chill is the cloud-maker: push moist air up a mountainside or a weather front and it cools until the water it carries can stay vapour no longer, and out comes cloud, then rain. Send the air back down the far side and it warms again, dry -- the reason one slope is green and rainy and the other is a dry rain-shadow. The engine checked the numbers.
wisdom: For he saith to the snow, Be thou on the earth; likewise to the small rain, and to the great rain of his strength (Job 37:6). The snow on the high cold places and the rain on the low fall each at his word.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. Rising air expands and cools (~10 C/km, adiabatic), which is why peaks are cold and why lifted air cools to its dew point and forms clouds/rain; descending air warms (rain shadow).

title: Radioactive decay as a clock -- reading elapsed time from the parent/daughter ratio, and the three things that reading assumes
situation: Earth as a complete system carries clocks inside its rocks. That radioactive decay IS exponential -- a nucleus and a capacitor obeying one law -- is already established in the corpus (connection_exponential_decay_two_witnesses); this card is about turning that law into a CLOCK and being honest about what the clock presumes. The decay law N = N0*exp(-L*t) solves dN/dt = -L*N: the number decaying is always proportional to the number left, so the survivors halve every half-life. After one half-life, half remain; after three, an eighth; the ladder is exact. Run it BACKWARD and you have a dating method: measure how much parent isotope is left as a fraction of what began, and the elapsed time falls out as t = -ln(fraction)/L. A quarter of the parent remaining reads as exactly two half-lives of age (ln(1/4)/ln(1/2) = 2); for carbon-14 (half-life 5730 years) that is 11,460 years. The MATH is exact and machine-checkable. But the AGE it yields is only as good as three assumptions, which must be named, not hidden: (1) the decay rate L has held constant over the whole interval; (2) the starting amount of parent (and daughter) is known; (3) the sample stayed a CLOSED system -- nothing added or lost but by decay. Where those hold, the clock is trustworthy; where they are uncertain, so is the date. The engine seals the law and the inversion, and marks the assumptions plainly -- it maps the clock, it does not pronounce the age.
use: To read time from decay, invert the exponential: t = -ln(N/N0)/L = (half-life) * log2(N0/N). Use the half-life ladder (1/2, 1/4, 1/8, ...) for quick estimates and pick an isotope whose half-life suits the span (carbon-14 ~ thousands of years for once-living things; uranium-lead and potassium-argon for far longer for minerals). ALWAYS carry the three assumptions with the number: constant decay rate, known initial amounts, closed system. State the date as a conditional ('if the system stayed closed and the rate held, then ...'), and treat a date as only as firm as its weakest assumption. This is the engine's discipline applied to any measurement-by-proxy: the formula is exact; the inputs and assumptions are where the honesty lives.

bonds: half-life ladder<->(1/2)^n remaining after n half-lives
constant-rate / known-initial / closed-system<->the three assumptions of a date
measurement-by-proxy<->name the assumptions, state the conditional

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 4/4. N=N0 exp(-Lt) solves dN/dt=-L*N. After 3 half-lives, (1/2)^3 = 1/8 remains. Dating inversion: a 1/4 parent fraction reads as ln(1/4)/ln(1/2) = 2 half-lives. Carbon-14 (5730 yr): two half-lives = 11,460 yr. A control claiming 1/4 = 4 half-lives MISMATCHED.

domain_results.note: decay rate proportional to amount present
the exact half-life ladder: 1/8 after three
the clock read backward: 1/4 parent = 2 half-lives
carbon-14 worked example: 11,460 years

pre_run.conjecture_boundary: HONESTY GATE. Sealed = the decay LAW and the dating INVERSION (exact, machine-checked). NOT sealed = any specific AGE or the age of the Earth. A radiometric date is valid only under three assumptions, named here and not asserted as proven for any sample: (1) the decay constant L was constant over the whole interval; (2) the initial parent/daughter amounts are known; (3) the system stayed closed. The engine maps the clock and its assumptions; it does not pronounce a date.

wisdom: Of old hast thou laid the foundation of the earth ... They shall perish, but thou shalt endure ... thou art the same, and thy years shall have no end (Psalm 102:25-27). The stones run down like a clock; the One who reads all clocks does not.

title: Decay makes a clock in the rocks -- but a clock is only as trustworthy as the things it quietly assumes
verification: Some atoms fall apart at a steady, dependable pace -- so steady that half of a given kind is gone after a fixed 'half-life,' a quarter after two, an eighth after three. That regularity makes a clock: measure how much is left compared to what began, and the math hands you the time elapsed (a quarter remaining means two half-lives have passed). The arithmetic is exact and the engine checked it. But here is the honest part, and it is a lesson far beyond rocks: a reading like that rests on quiet assumptions -- that the pace never changed, that you know how much there was to start, and that nothing leaked in or out in between. Where those hold, trust the clock. Where they are shaky, the number is only as firm as its weakest assumption. Always ask of any measurement: what is this quietly taking for granted?
wisdom: Remember the days of old, consider the years of many generations: ask thy father, and he will shew thee (Deuteronomy 32:7). To reckon the years rightly we lean on what is given and handed down -- and we do well to know what we are leaning on.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. Steady decay is an exact clock (1/2, 1/4, 1/8 ...), and reading time from it is exact math -- but the AGE rests on three assumptions (constant rate, known start, closed system); a clock is only as good as what it assumes.

title: Why a stick looks bent in water and the set sun still shows -- Snell's law: light bends to take the fastest path, n1 sin t1 = n2 sin t2
situation: Earth as a complete system, with its own atmosphere and oceans for lenses. Light travels slower in denser stuff (speed v = c/n, so water at n = 4/3 slows light to three-quarters of c). When a ray crosses from one medium into another it BENDS, by Snell's law n1*sin(t1) = n2*sin(t2). Entering a denser medium it bends toward the perpendicular -- the angle's sine shrinks (1*sin30 = 2*(1/4): the ray that came in at 30 degrees leaves at the smaller angle whose sine is a quarter). The deep reason is Fermat's principle of LEAST TIME: light follows not the shortest path but the QUICKEST, just as a lifeguard cuts the angle to spend less time in the slow water -- and bending at the boundary is exactly what minimizes the travel time. From this one law the world's optical illusions unfold. A stick in water looks bent and a pool looks shallower than it is (a four-foot pool seen from above looks three feet deep, shrunk by the index n). The atmosphere, denser near the ground, is a weak lens: it lifts the image of the setting Sun so you still see it after it has truly dropped below the horizon, and it makes a MIRAGE where hot thin air near a road bends light up so the sky seems to pool on the pavement. And in a million raindrops, refraction in and out (with one bounce inside) sorts sunlight into the rainbow. Total internal reflection -- the same law pushed past its critical angle -- is sealed separately (fiber optics); this card is the bending itself.
use: To predict where light goes across a boundary, apply n1*sin(t1) = n2*sin(t2): toward the normal into a denser (slower, higher-n) medium, away from it into a thinner one. Remember the cause is least TIME (Fermat), the same optimization principle behind lenses, the eye, and the brachistochrone. Use it to correct what you see through water or air: spearing a fish, judging a pool's depth (divide by n), or knowing the Sun is already geometrically below the horizon when its lower limb appears to touch it. Read a mirage as light bent by a density (temperature) gradient, and a rainbow as refraction sorting colors because n varies slightly with wavelength (dispersion). When the same law is pushed past the critical angle, light cannot leave at all -- total internal reflection (sealed in the fiber-optics card).

bonds: Snell bending<->Fermat least time (light takes the quickest path)
n = c/v<->denser medium slower, bends toward normal
apparent depth = real/n<->bent stick, shallow pool
atmospheric gradient<->mirage and the lifted setting Sun
refraction past critical angle<->total internal reflection (fiber card)

pre_run.summary: Sealed HOLDS 4/4. Snell: 1*sin(90) = 2*sin(30) (n1 sin t1 = n2 sin t2). Bends toward normal into denser medium: 1*sin(30) = 2*(1/4), the angle's sine shrinks. Apparent depth shallower by n: 4 ft / (4/3) = 3 ft. Denser medium slows light: c/(4/3) = 3c/4. A control equating sin(90) to 2*sin(90) MISMATCHED.

domain_results.note: n1 sin t1 = n2 sin t2, the refraction law
into denser medium the angle's sine shrinks (bends toward normal)
a 4 ft pool looks 3 ft deep (real depth / n)
v = c/n: water (n=4/3) slows light to 3c/4 -- the basis of least-time bending

wisdom: For now we see through a glass, darkly; but then face to face (1 Corinthians 13:12). Light reaches us bent and indirect, the image shifted from the thing -- a true seeing, but not yet the straight, face-to-face seeing that is promised.

keywords: why the sun lingers after sunset

title: Light bends when it changes speed -- so the stick looks broken, the pool looks shallow, and the sun lingers after it has set
verification: Light slows down when it passes into water or glass, and where it slows it bends -- always toward the fastest route, the way a runner angles across sand to spend less time in the slow part. That single habit of light fools the eye in gentle, everyday ways. A straight stick dipped in water looks broken at the surface; a clear pool looks shallower than it is (about three feet for every four of real depth); a spear aimed where the fish appears will miss the fish. The very air does it too: being denser near the ground, it lifts the image of the setting sun so you still see the sun for a minute or two after it has truly gone below the horizon, and it makes the shimmering 'water' mirage on a hot road. And when a sky full of raindrops each bends the light just so, the colors fan out into a rainbow. The engine checked the geometry.
wisdom: I do set my bow in the cloud, and it shall be for a token of a covenant between me and the earth (Genesis 9:13). The fairest fruit of bending light is hung in the sky as a promise.

pre_run.summary: Human-facing. Light bends when it changes speed (Snell, least time): bent stick, shallow-looking pool, the sun lifted after sunset, mirages, the rainbow.

title: Why a planet sits at the temperature it does -- sunlight in must equal heat out: T = ((1-A)S/(4 sigma))^(1/4)
situation: Earth as a complete system finds its temperature by a simple book-balancing. Energy IN: the planet catches sunlight across its shadow -- its circular cross-section, area pi*R^2 -- and reflects a fraction A (its albedo, ~0.3 for Earth, so it keeps 70%). Energy OUT: the whole spherical surface, area 4*pi*R^2, glows with thermal radiation, and a warm body radiates as sigma*T^4 (the Stefan-Boltzmann law, where T^4 means doubling the absolute temperature multiplies the outflow sixteenfold). Set in equal to out, (1-A)*S*pi*R^2 = sigma*T^4*4*pi*R^2, and the R cancels: the planet's size drops out entirely. Solving gives T = ((1-A)*S/(4*sigma))^(1/4). The mysterious '4' is pure geometry -- a sphere's surface is exactly four times its shadow (4*pi*R^2 / pi*R^2 = 4), so the catch is spread over four times the area before it is re-radiated. Put in Earth's numbers and this bare radiative temperature comes out about 255 K (-18 C). The actual surface averages about 288 K (15 C); the ~33 K difference is the greenhouse effect -- the atmosphere re-radiating some heat back down (named here, established; the size of FUTURE changes is a separate, contested question and is not sealed). The same balance, with each planet's own sunlight and albedo, sets the temperature of Venus, Mars, and every world.
use: To estimate any planet's (or moon's, or asteroid's) temperature, balance absorbed sunlight against radiated heat: T = ((1-A)S/(4 sigma))^(1/4), using that body's solar flux S and albedo A. The result is the EFFECTIVE (radiative) temperature; a real surface with an atmosphere runs warmer by its greenhouse offset, named separately. Use the geometric factor 4 (surface/shadow) wherever a disk catches and a sphere emits. The T^4 steepness explains why a small temperature rise sheds a large extra heat flow (a strong negative feedback that stabilizes climate), why clear desert nights cool fast (nothing to send the radiation back), and why a white (high-albedo) surface stays cooler than a dark one. Energy in equals energy out is the master ledger for any body in steady state.

one balance<->every planet's temperature (Venus, Mars, Earth)

title: A world settles at the temperature where the sunlight it keeps exactly equals the heat it gives back
verification: A planet has no thermostat -- and yet it settles to a steady temperature, by a balance as plain as a household budget. Sunlight comes in and warms it; the warm globe glows with heat and sends energy back out to space. It heats up until those two exactly match, and there it sits. Two facts set the figure. The Earth catches sun on its round shadow but radiates from its whole surface, which is four times as large -- so the catch is spread thin. And a warm thing radiates very steeply with temperature (raise it a little and it sheds a lot more), which keeps the balance tightly held. Run the numbers and the bare figure for Earth is a chilly -18 C; our actual 15 C is the gift of the atmosphere, a blanket that sends some heat back down. Mercury, Mars, and Venus each find their own balance the same way. The engine checked the ledger.

title: Why a tsunami crosses the ocean at jetliner speed yet rears up at the shore -- a shallow-water wave, v = sqrt(g*h), set only by depth
situation: Earth as a complete system, with the whole ocean as one wave medium. Water waves come in two regimes, decided by how the wavelength compares to the depth. A WIND swell is a deep-water wave (its wavelength is small next to the ocean depth); its speed grows with its length, c = sqrt(g*lambda/2*pi) -- the law already sealed in the oceanography card, and the reason a long swell outruns the storm that made it. A TSUNAMI is the opposite: its wavelength is hundreds of kilometres, so even the four-kilometre-deep ocean is 'shallow' to it. A shallow-water wave's speed depends ONLY on the depth: v = sqrt(g*h). In 4000 m of water that is sqrt(10*4000) = 200 m/s -- about 720 km/h, the speed of a jetliner, crossing an ocean in half a day. Out in the deep it is almost unnoticed: only a metre or so high, spread over a wavelength so long a ship rides over it unaware. But as it runs into shallowing water near the coast the depth h falls, so the speed falls with sqrt(h) -- at 10 m depth it has slowed to 10 m/s, a twentieth of its ocean pace. The front slows while the back still races; the wave bunches up and its energy, with nowhere else to go, piles into HEIGHT. That is the deadly rise at the shore -- the same wave, merely slowed and stacked. Depth alone steers it.
use: Classify a water wave by wavelength vs depth: deep-water (lambda << h) waves are DISPERSIVE, speed set by wavelength (sqrt(g*lambda/2pi)); shallow-water (lambda >> h) waves are NON-dispersive, speed set by depth (sqrt(g*h)). Use v = sqrt(g*h) for tsunamis, tides, and bores: estimate arrival time across an ocean from the depth profile, and expect the wave to slow and grow (shoal) as it nears shore -- the warning is that a far-deep quake's wave arrives fast and small, then rears up. The same shallow-water law governs a flood surge down a river and the hydraulic jump below a weir. Pair this card with the deep-water oceanography card for the full picture: the ocean obeys two wave laws, and which one rules depends on whether the wave 'feels the bottom'.

tsunami lambda >> depth<->whole ocean is 'shallow' to it
non-dispersive<->the wave holds its shape across an ocean
same law<->river flood surge, tidal bore, hydraulic jump

title: The Earth is a complete system -- a closed laboratory in which the same handful of universal laws each recur at planetary scale
situation: This is the capstone of a sweep through the planet. Matt's thesis was that the Earth is a great system for finding mechanical similarities -- 'a complete system' -- and the corpus now bears it out: a dozen separate findings, each sealed on its own, turn out to be the SAME small laws that hold in a hand or a lab, simply run at the size of a world. RESONANCE: the Earth-ionosphere cavity rings at its natural notes exactly as an organ pipe does (f ~ sqrt(n(n+1))). GRAVITATION: the tide is the derivative of gravity, a 1/r^3 force one power steeper than the 1/r^2 pull. BUOYANCY: mountains float on the mantle by Archimedes' law, with a hidden root like an iceberg's. ROTATION: the spin deflects every wind and current by the rotating-frame law, f = 2*Omega*sin(latitude). CONDUCTION: the planet's inner heat climbs to the surface by Fourier's law, a straight temperature line. GEOMETRY: the seasons are a cosine, S*cos(angle). THERMODYNAMICS: rising air cools adiabatically, so mountaintops are cold and clouds form. RADIATION: the whole globe sits at the temperature where absorbed sunlight equals Stefan-Boltzmann re-radiation, the famous factor 4 being a sphere's surface over its shadow. OPTICS: the air and sea bend light by Snell's least-time law -- mirage, rainbow, the lingering sun. WAVES: a tsunami crosses the ocean by the shallow-water law v = sqrt(g*h). DECAY: the rocks keep an exponential clock (its reading only as good as its named assumptions). Twelve doors, one house. The planet is not a special exception to physics; it is physics made whole and visible -- the universal parts (the periodic map's families) assembled into one working machine. We MAP these recurrences; we do not author them. That the same law fits the lab and the world is the strongest sign the map is true.
use: Treat any self-contained system -- a planet, a cell, an economy, a machine -- as a place to FIND the universal laws at a new scale: identify which family each behavior belongs to (resonance, transport, buoyancy, rotation, radiation, wave, decay, equilibrium) and carry the verified small-scale law up or down. The Earth is the worked example: twelve of its signature phenomena reduce to laws already mapped elsewhere. When a planetary fact resists this, that resistance is a lead -- either a missing law to map or an assumption to name. This card is the geoscience anchor into the wider thesis that reality is consistently mappable because it is built from a few recurring forms; it gathers the twelve sealed Earth-cards under one receipt.

verification: Here is a quiet marvel: the planet does not need its own special rulebook. The very laws you can see at home run the whole Earth. A pipe organ's note and the Earth ringing after a lightning storm are the same kind of music. An iceberg in a glass and a mountain on the map float by the same rule. A cooling cup of coffee and the heat creeping up from deep in the ground obey the same law of conduction. The cosine that dims a slanted flashlight is the reason for winter. The balance of a household budget is the reason the planet sits at the temperature it does. title: The integers are one structure, not a heap -- primes are the atoms, modular arithmetic the clock, coprimality the hinge
situation: Capstone of the number-theory cluster. The whole numbers look like a simple list, but they are a tightly-built structure, and the sealed cards of this corpus are its load-bearing pillars. ATOMS: every integer is one and only one product of primes (60 = 2^2 * 3 * 5) -- unique factorization makes the primes the atoms of arithmetic, and their distribution thins out like the logarithm (the prime number theorem). HINGE: the greatest common divisor, found by Euclid's ancient remainder-recursion, measures how numbers share factors; when gcd is 1 they are coprime, and that single condition is the hinge on which clock-arithmetic, the circle of fifths (gcd(7,12)=1), and the Chinese Remainder Theorem all turn. CLOCK: arithmetic mod n wraps the line into a ring, and there Fermat's little theorem holds (2^6 = 1 mod 7) -- the engine behind RSA and Diffie-Hellman, where easy-forward / hard-backward locks the world's secrets. HARMONY: perfect numbers tie primes to their divisors (Euclid-Euler: 6 = 1+2+3 = 2^1(2^2-1)), and the zeta function folds the integers into pi (zeta(2) = pi^2/6) while guarding the deepest open question, the Riemann Hypothesis (named, not sealed). One structure shows four faces -- factorization, divisibility, congruence, and analysis -- and the same integers reappear as music, cryptography, and the shape of the primes. We map the structure; we do not invent it.
use: Treat any question about whole numbers by asking which face of the one structure it touches: factorization (primes as atoms, unique decomposition), divisibility (gcd / Euclid / coprimality), congruence (modular arithmetic, Fermat/Euler, the basis of public-key cryptography), or analysis (prime counting, the zeta function). Coprimality (gcd = 1) is the recurring hinge -- look for it in cycles, tilings, gears, and scales. Easy-forward / hard-backward (multiply vs factor) is the recurring lock -- look for it wherever security lives. This capstone gathers the cluster's sealed cards (Euclid GCD, CRT, Fermat little theorem, RSA, Diffie-Hellman, perfect numbers, prime number theorem, the zeta/Riemann cards, coprimality and the circle of fifths) under one receipt, and feeds the grand thesis that the same verified structure recurs across every domain.

title: The counting numbers are not a heap -- they are built, with the primes as their bricks
verification: It is easy to think of 1, 2, 3, 4 as just a line that goes on forever. But the whole numbers are built, not piled. The primes -- 2, 3, 5, 7, 11 -- are the bricks: every other number is one and only one stack of them (60 is two 2s, a 3, and a 5, and nothing else). From that one fact a whole architecture follows: how numbers share factors (the oldest recipe still in daily use, Euclid's), the 'clock arithmetic' that wraps around and quietly locks every password and bank transfer in the world, the rare 'perfect' numbers that equal the sum of their own parts, and patterns so deep that the hardest unsolved problem in mathematics lives among them. The numbers reappear everywhere -- in music's scales, in secret codes, in the thinning-out of the primes. We did not make this order; we only trace it. The engine checked each brick.

title: Chance has a shape and a law -- randomness piled up becomes the bell curve, its spread shrinks as sqrt(n), and surprise is just arithmetic
situation: Capstone of the statistics and probability cluster. Chance is not lawless; it has a shape and obeys rules, and the sealed cards of this corpus are its pillars. SHAPE: add up many small independent random nudges and the total always piles into the same bell curve -- the Central Limit Theorem, which the Galton board builds out of falling beads (the binomial row 1,4,6,4,1 summing to 16 = 2^4 is the bell in miniature). AVERAGING: independent variations partly cancel, so the spread of a sum grows only as the square root of the count (sqrt(100) = 10) -- the law of large numbers, why a big sample is steadier than a small one and why the average is far tighter than any single draw. COMBINING: independent chances MULTIPLY (a half and a half make a quarter) while their information ADDS; probabilities over all outcomes must sum to one (Mendel's 3/4 + 1/4 = 1; the dihybrid 9:3:3:1 over 16). SURPRISE: our intuition misreads chance, but the arithmetic does not -- a 99%-accurate test for a 1-in-100 disease gives a positive result only a 50% chance of being real (the base-rate fact), the single most useful correction in all of applied reasoning. And a process that forgets its past settles to a fixed long-run distribution (the Markov equilibrium). Lots, dice, genes, measurement error, and polls all bow to the same few laws. We map the laws of chance; we do not author them -- and chance itself answers to a hand above it.
use: Read any uncertain situation through the cluster's laws: expect sums of many independent parts to be NORMAL (bell-shaped); expect the average to tighten as 1/sqrt(n) (quadruple the sample to halve the error); MULTIPLY independent probabilities, and make outcomes sum to one; and ALWAYS fold in the base rate before trusting a positive test or a 'significant' headline (a rare condition makes even an accurate test mostly false alarms). Use the long-run (Markov) equilibrium for processes that forget their start. This capstone gathers the cluster's sealed cards -- the normal distribution, the Galton board / CLT, the sqrt(n) law of aggregation, independence-multiplies, the Mendelian ratios, the base-rate/positive-test card, and the Markov eigenvector equilibrium -- under one receipt, feeding the grand thesis that one verified structure recurs across every domain.

title: Chance is not chaos -- pile up enough of it and it falls into a dependable shape every time
verification: One coin flip is anybody's guess. But flip ten thousand and the count of heads lands, almost without fail, very near half -- and the pile of outcomes always takes the same gentle bell shape. That is the quiet miracle of chance: a single event is unpredictable, yet MANY events are deeply lawful. Two useful truths fall out. First, the more you gather, the steadier the average: to halve your uncertainty you must quadruple your sample. Second, beware the rare case -- if a disease strikes one person in a hundred, even a test that is 99% accurate will be WRONG about half the time it cries alarm, because most of the people it flags are healthy. Our gut misjudges this every time; the arithmetic never does. So count before you trust, and weigh the base rate before you worry. The engine checked the numbers.
wisdom: I returned, and saw under the sun, that the race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong ... but time and chance happeneth to them all (Ecclesiastes 9:11). Time and chance touch every life -- yet chance itself keeps faithful laws.
